EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along an old outflow boundary across parts of central and east central Missouri. Some of the storms produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread wind damage across Farmington. Numerous trees, tree limbs and power lines were blown down. Also, a couple of power poles were knocked down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought a prolonged 24 to 30 hour period of freezing drizzle across the area, beginning during the early overnight hours of January 29th and continuing throughout the day on January 29th before ending in the early morning hours of January 30th. An additional wintry mix of mainly heavy sleet as well as light snow and light freezing rain impacted areas mainly along and south of the Interstate 44 corridor, starting in the mid to late morning and ending from west to east across the area during the afternoon into early evening. ||Ice accretions were a widespread glaze, with ASOS measured flat ice accumulations ranging from 0.05 inches in West Plains to a max of 0.33 inches in Springfield. Sleet and snow accumulations, which were mainly in the form of sleet, ranged from around 0.25 inches in locations near Interstate 44 to between 1.0 to 1.5 inches near the southern Missouri border.||There were numerous crashes and slide offs across the area on January 29th and 30th, with the Missouri Department of Transportation showing covered and slick roadways throughout the event. One of the crashes in Morgan County also resulted in a fatality. Some roads south of Interstate 44 even remained covered into the day on January 31st. Numerous schools and businesses across the area were closed on January 30th due to the winter road impacts, with numerous schools remaining closed on January 31st along and south of Interstate 44 where roads remained covered and slick.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of precipitation fell across the county, beginning during the very early morning hours of January 29th as freezing drizzle and lingering into the very early morning of January 30th. There was an estimated light glaze of ice across the county. Roads became covered and slick with several schools also closed on January 30th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning and afternoon hours of September 23rd, storms brought large hail and damaging wind gusts to portions of western Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported along Missouri Route 45 south of Lewis and Clark Village.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front was across portions of southeast Missouri. With plenty of instability, moisture and lift near the boundary, several strong to severe storms developed in this area. There were several reports of damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down several large trees, numerous tree limbs and several power lines around town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along an old outflow boundary across parts of central and east central Missouri. Some of the storms produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down several power lines on Highway Z about 2 miles south of Murry. In Columbia, Numerous trees, tree limbs and power lines were blown down. On tree fell onto a house near the intersection of Ash Street and Greenwood causing moderate roof damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developed over the central Plains near the Kansas and Nebraska border during the afternoon of the 7th and moved southeastward across central and south central Missouri from the pre-dawn hours to the early morning of the 8th. These storms left an outflow boundary over central Missouri that sparked a second round of storms during the afternoon. These storms were much stronger and produced hail across central Missouri through the evening of the  8th. The strongest storm produced hail to the size of baseballs in Dent County near Stone Hill. Rainfall amounts over two inches occurred with these storms near Nevada, Lake of the Ozarks, and north of Salem.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball sized hail was reported near the Mimosa Beach Resort. Several docks, boats and other buildings received roof and window damage.

Thunderstorms developed over central Missouri during the early evening hours. They moved east/southeast through the overnight hours. Numerous large hail and damaging wind reports were received. During the late evening through overnight hours, widespread heavy rain fell causing flash flooding. There was fairly widespread 1-3 rainfall totals, with isolated 6-8+ in central Missouri and south-central Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to five inches of rain fell in a short amount of time causing flash flooding over southwestern portions of Franklin County. Water was flowing over Highway 185 at the intersection with Spring Bluff Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Isolated flash flooding occurred just northeast of Farmington. One to two inches of rain fell during the evening hours of August 2nd. Then a second round occurred during the early morning hours of August 3rd with an additional 2 to 3 inches. They were rescued and did not sustain any injuries.

Storms occurred from the early afternoon to the evening of the 17th ahead of the southward moving cold front. The storms mainly affected locations along and east of a line from Stockton to Springfield to Branson, and produced hail up to 3 inches in diameter at Preston in Hickory County. Wind damage and wind gusts over 70 mph were also reported. The town of Theodosia in Ozark County was especially hard hit with widespread tree damage. Rainfall accumulations averaged between one to two inches, with isolated accumulations over three inches in Laclede and Camden counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to golf balls in size was reported with minor roof and vehicle damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving weather system brought a quick round of light sleet, light snow, and patchy freezing drizzle to counties mainly along and south of the Interstate 44 corridor across southern Missouri from around midday through mid to late afternoon on January 31st. This precipitation generally fell at any one location for around 2 to 3 hours before ending. While no measurements of snow, sleet, or ice was reported, observations did show light snow mixed with sleet and patchy freezing drizzle. The Missouri Department of Transportation traveler map showed that this light precipitation was enough to partly cover or completely cover roads and result in slick conditions into the evening commute on January 31st, and some lingering slick spots on roads into February 1st. A few isolated slide offs and crashes occurred during the morning and afternoon of February 1st due to the lingering slick spots on roadways despite no falling precipitation. Numerous schools south of Interstate 44 were closed for January 31st due to the precipitation forecast and road conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A quick moving system brought a brief mix of light sleet, light snow, and patchy freezing drizzle to the county during the afternoon on January 31st. No measurements of snow, sleet, or ice were reported. The Missouri Department of Transportation traveler map showed roads becoming covered and slick, with slick roads lingering into the evening commute and patchy slick spots into February 1st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of thunderstorms moved southeast across parts of southeast Missouri. A few of these storms produced strong winds, including isolated wind damage at Poplar Bluff. During the peak heating hours in the mid to late afternoon, thunderstorms gradually increased in coverage along a weak surface boundary over southeast Missouri. Wind shear was weak, but strong instability and high precipitable water values resulted in a wet microburst. Mixed-layer cape ranged from 2500 to 3500 J/kg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was down on power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system and cold front brought light to moderate snowfall across portions of central Missouri, with light snow as far south as the Highway 60 corridor. A band of precipitation with some convective elements moved into portions of central and west central Missouri during the early morning hours of January 12th. Some of the convective elements briefly had sleet with them throughout the morning hours, with precipitation changing over to all snow within about 30 minutes to 2 hours after precipitation onset. Some thunder occurred with the snow in the Lake of the Ozarks region during the early morning hours. As a result, the highest snowfall amounts and rates occurred over central Missouri, with the snow band weakening as it dove southeast across the area during the morning. Precipitation came to an end as flurries and light rain over south central Missouri by early afternoon on January 12th. ||Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 3 to 5 inches across central Missouri and the Lake Ozarks region to as little as 0.5 inches along and just south of the Interstate 44 and Highway 60 corridors across southwestern Missouri. Roads north of Interstate 44, and especially towards the Highway 54 corridor and across the Lake of the Ozarks region, became mostly to completely snow covered during the morning, with partly covered roadways farther south into the Springfield metropolitan area during the morning rush hour into late morning. Multiple slide offs and accidents were reported. About one dozen schools closed for the day, all of which were located across central Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of convective showers had some non-impactful sleet briefly mix in before changing over to light snow during the morning hours of January 12th. Roads remained mostly clear, but were slick at times due to slushy accumulations during higher snowfall rates. A tractor trailer overturned in the county, but injury reports were unknown with this incident. The Cooperative Observer 3 miles north northeast of Northview reported a total snow accumulation of 1.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent storm system lifted northeast from Oklahoma through Arkansas and Missouri on January 24th and 25th, bringing widespread precipitation across the Missouri Ozarks beginning as rain during the late morning and afternoon on January 24th before changing over to snow during the afternoon and evening. This snow continued into the morning hours of January 25th before coming to an end as the storm system exited the region.||Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 1 to 3 inches for areas along and northwest of a Joplin to Stockton to Lake of the Ozarks line. There was a sharp cutoff in heavy snow near Interstate 44, with many locations seeing 6 to 8 inches south of Interstate 44 and up to 12 inches near West Plains. Roads became completely covered and slick across the region for the evening commute of January 24th and also into the morning commute on January 25th due to the ongoing snow. Several slide offs and vehicle accidents occurred as a result of the slick road conditions. Numerous schools were closed across the region on January 25th, with some even doing early dismissals on January 24th. Some schools also remain closed into January 26th in the areas that received higher snow amounts across south central Missouri. Total power outages reached over 50 thousand across southern and south central Missouri from the heavy snow, with numerous power outages continuing in Howell, Shannon and Oregon Counties into January 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation started as rain during the afternoon of January 24th before changing over to snow during the late afternoon, with the snow then continuing into the morning of January 25th. Snow amounts between 2 and 2.5 inches fell across the county. Roads became snow covered and slippery, with a one vehicle injury accident occurring on Highway B east of Sheldon as the vehicle slid off the road, struck an embankment and overturned. Some schools were closed on January 25th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful cold front raced across the Mid-South during the morning hours of March 3, 2023. Strong winds ahead of the front caused scattered damage across northeast Mississippi. Storms developed along the cold front producing damaging winds across northeast Mississippi and a weak tornado in Itawamba County, Mississippi. Behind the departing cold front, a very tight pressure gradient developed resulting in high winds across the Mid-South through the afternoon hours on March 3rd. Winds gusted to over 60 mph at times producing damage across the Mid-South.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted over 58 mph at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Even though an old outflow boundary was across southeast Missouri and the far southern portions of Illinois during the morning hours, the boundary dissipated, allowing 75+ dew points to surge north along the Mississippi River. As skies cleared the atmosphere was able to quickly destabilize. Surface based CAPEs were in excess of 3000 J/kg with 40kts of 0 to 6km bulk shear. This combined with a shortwave trough that moved eastward across the region, produced strong to severe storms. There were numerous reports of damaging winds and some reports of large hail. There were three fatalities due to fallen trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread wind damage across the northern portions of the city as a line of storms moved through. Numerous large trees, tree limbs, power poles and power lines were blown down. A woman who was waiting for the storm to pass was killed when a large tree was blown over, crushing the car in the alley of the 4100 block of Chouteau Avenue. Two historical cemeteries, Calvary and Bellefontaine, sustained widespread damage. Around 100 trees at Calvary and nearly 40 significant trees and 100 smaller ones at Bellefontaine, were either uprooted or snapped off near the base, as well as numerous tree limbs. Several gravestones were either damaged or destroyed by fallen trees and/or tree limbs. A large tree was uprooted in the 3000 block of Longfellow Boulevard, landing on a house causing major roof damage. A couple of power poles were snapped off along Hall Street. Over 100,000 customers (St. Louis County/St. Louis City) were without power due to this storm. It took a little over 4 days to restore power to all of them.

Strong upper level ridge built up over the region from July 25th through 29th. This ushered in much warmer air and higher dewpoints. So heat index values 100+ were reported from July 25th through 29th. The hottest days were July 27th, 28th and 29th, so an excessive heat warning was issued for portions of northeast/east central Missouri and west central/southwest Illinois. At least 50 people were seen in hospital emergency room departments during this three day period in St. Louis County/City for heat-related illnesses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive heat across the county with highs in the mid to upper 90s each day. Heat index values as high as 110 both days.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front was across portions of southeast Missouri. With plenty of instability, moisture and lift near the boundary, several strong to severe storms developed in this area. There were several reports of damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread hail damage was reported in Ellington due to hail up to golf ball size. Several windshields on vehicles were broken, as well as windows on several homes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down numerous trees, tree limbs and power lines in portions of south St. Louis County, especially in Affton, Lakeshire, Wilbur Park, and St. George. A storm system brought a prolonged 24 to 30 hour period of freezing drizzle across the area, beginning during the early overnight hours of January 29th and continuing throughout the day on January 29th before ending in the early morning hours of January 30th. An additional wintry mix of mainly heavy sleet as well as light snow and light freezing rain impacted areas mainly along and south of the Interstate 44 corridor, starting in the mid to late morning and ending from west to east across the area during the afternoon into early evening. ||Ice accretions were a widespread glaze, with ASOS measured flat ice accumulations ranging from 0.05 inches in West Plains to a max of 0.33 inches in Springfield. Sleet and snow accumulations, which were mainly in the form of sleet, ranged from around 0.25 inches in locations near Interstate 44 to between 1.0 to 1.5 inches near the southern Missouri border.||There were numerous crashes and slide offs across the area on January 29th and 30th, with the Missouri Department of Transportation showing covered and slick roadways throughout the event. One of the crashes in Morgan County also resulted in a fatality. Some roads south of Interstate 44 even remained covered into the day on January 31st. Numerous schools and businesses across the area were closed on January 30th due to the winter road impacts, with numerous schools remaining closed on January 31st along and south of Interstate 44 where roads remained covered and slick.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of wintry precipitation fell across the county. The first round began during the very early morning hours of January 29th as freezing drizzle and lingered into the early morning of January 30th. The second round fell briefly during the morning of January 30th and was mainly sleet. A public report 1 mile ENE of Airport Drive in Joplin measured a 30 hour radial ice accretion of 0.05 inches, which converts to a flat ice accretion of 0.13 inches. The Joplin ASOS measured a trace accumulation of sleet. Roads became covered and slick with numerous schools closed on January 30th. Two crashes occurred during the late evening of January 29th. The first crash was on Highway P just west of Joplin when a westbound vehicle slide into the eastbound lane and struck another vehicle, resulting in one injury. The second crash was a tractor trailer eastbound on Interstate 44 ran into a guard rail, which also closed off the left lane of traffic and resulted in one injury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent storm system lifted northeast from Oklahoma through Arkansas and Missouri on January 24th and 25th, bringing widespread precipitation across the Missouri Ozarks beginning as rain during the late morning and afternoon on January 24th before changing over to snow during the afternoon and evening. This snow continued into the morning hours of January 25th before coming to an end as the storm system exited the region.||Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 1 to 3 inches for areas along and northwest of a Joplin to Stockton to Lake of the Ozarks line. There was a sharp cutoff in heavy snow near Interstate 44, with many locations seeing 6 to 8 inches south of Interstate 44 and up to 12 inches near West Plains. Roads became completely covered and slick across the region for the evening commute of January 24th and also into the morning commute on January 25th due to the ongoing snow. Several slide offs and vehicle accidents occurred as a result of the slick road conditions. Numerous schools were closed across the region on January 25th, with some even doing early dismissals on January 24th. Some schools also remain closed into January 26th in the areas that received higher snow amounts across south central Missouri. Total power outages reached over 50 thousand across southern and south central Missouri from the heavy snow, with numerous power outages continuing in Howell, Shannon and Oregon Counties into January 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation started as rain during the afternoon of January 24th before changing over to snow during the late afternoon, with the snow then continuing into the morning of January 25th. Widespread snow amounts between 3.5 and 6 inches fell across the county. A public report of a measured storm total of 5.8 inches of snow was received from Waynesville, with a measured 3.5 inches in Saint Robert. Roads became snow covered and slippery. Around 400 power outages also occurred across the county due to the heavy snow. Some schools had early release on January 24th, with numerous schools closed on January 25th and January 26th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving weather system brought a quick round of light sleet, light snow, and patchy freezing drizzle to counties mainly along and south of the Interstate 44 corridor across southern Missouri from around midday through mid to late afternoon on January 31st. This precipitation generally fell at any one location for around 2 to 3 hours before ending. While no measurements of snow, sleet, or ice was reported, observations did show light snow mixed with sleet and patchy freezing drizzle. The Missouri Department of Transportation traveler map showed that this light precipitation was enough to partly cover or completely cover roads and result in slick conditions into the evening commute on January 31st, and some lingering slick spots on roads into February 1st. A few isolated slide offs and crashes occurred during the morning and afternoon of February 1st due to the lingering slick spots on roadways despite no falling precipitation. Numerous schools south of Interstate 44 were closed for January 31st due to the precipitation forecast and road conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A quick moving system brought a brief mix of light sleet, light snow, and patchy freezing drizzle to the county from midday through mid afternoon on January 31st. No measurements of snow, sleet, or ice were reported. The Missouri Department of Transportation traveler map showed roads becoming covered and slick, with slick roads lingering into the evening commute and patchy slick spots into February 1st. Lingering ice on roadways during the evening of February 1st resulted in a car skidding on an ice covered road and striking another vehicle during the evening of February 1st, resulting in one injury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance interacted with moisture overriding a stalled front over Arkansas to produce showers and a few thunderstorms over the area from the afternoon of the 7th to the early morning of the 8th. While most of the area received between one-quarter to one-half inch of rain, locations along Interstate 44 from Joplin to Springfield and along Highway 60 from Springfield to Cabool received over one and a half inches. Isolated reports of flooding were received from Lawrence, Douglas and Wright counties where soils were already saturated from heavy rain earlier in the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Highway AB at Wolf Creek was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along an old outflow boundary across parts of central and east central Missouri. Some of the storms produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread wind damage in Desloge, Park Hills and Leadington areas. Numerous trees, tree limbs and power lines were blown down. One tree was blown down onto a house briefly trapping 5 occupants in the home. No one was injured. Otherwise, the home will need extensive repairs before family can return to it.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along an old outflow boundary across parts of central and east central Missouri. Some of the storms produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread wind damage across Bismarck. Several large trees and power lines as well as numerous tree limbs were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated storms developed in an area where dewpoints in the low to mid 70s pooled. There was some weak surface convergence, ML CAPEs over 2000 J/kg, and steep low-level lapse rates, so there were some localized damaging microbursts with the severe storms. An area of storms over northern St. Clair County in Illinois produced up to 5 inches of rain in about a two hour period with some reports of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down several trees and numerous tree limbs just east of Taos.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The most impactful storm system to affect the region during the month of March began during the late afternoon and early evening of the 23rd as strong to severe thunderstorms trained along and north of a front that had stalled over northwest Arkansas and south central Missouri. While the storms produced sporadic wind damage and hail, rainfall accumulations exceeded three inches within a 6-hour period during the evening of the 23rd. This led to numerous flooded low water crossings, evacuations at Roaring River State Park, and swift water rescues from McDonald County to Dent County. Unfortunately, there were three flash flood related fatalities that occurred - two fatalities occurred along Parks Creek to the southwest of Grovespring in Wright County, and one fatality occurred in southern Webster County along Finley Creek. In both instances, people drove across flooded low water crossings and were swept off the road. Minor to moderate river flooding occurred along many of the larger creeks and rivers including the James, Jacks Fork, Elk, Spring, Current, Big Piney, Roubidoux and Gasconade Rivers and Shoal Creek. Rainfall accumulations averaging around two inches for the 24-hour period ending at 6 PM on the 24th occurred along and east of a line from Neosho to Osage Beach. Within this area, there was a swath of over five inches of rain from Billings to Cabool.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickle sized hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated storms developed in an area where dewpoints in the low to mid 70s pooled. There was some weak surface convergence, ML CAPEs over 2000 J/kg, and steep low-level lapse rates, so there were some localized damaging microbursts with the severe storms. An area of storms over northern St. Clair County in Illinois produced up to 5 inches of rain in about a two hour period with some reports of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread wind damage across Midway and Columbia. Numerous trees, tree limbs and power lines were knocked down. One power pole on Rangeline Road, east of Columbia, was leaning with power lines down in the area as well. One large tree was blown down blocking Randy Lane near intersection with Bluff Blvd. Another tree was blown down blocking St. Charles Road near Kipling Way. A power line was blown down blocking Route Z near I-70. A tree was blown down onto Bonne Femme Church Road near Gans Creek Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lingering warm front as well as remnant outflow boundaries were the triggering mechanisms for storms. Some of the storms produced large hail, damaging winds and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down several trees on the southeast side of Washington. One of the trees was uprooted.

Along and southeast of a line from Doniphan to Poplar Bluff to Cape Girardeau, winds gusted over 40 mph. Isolated storms developed in an area where dewpoints in the low to mid 70s pooled. There was some weak surface convergence, ML CAPEs over 2000 J/kg, and steep low-level lapse rates, so there were some localized damaging microbursts with the severe storms. An area of storms over northern St. Clair County in Illinois produced up to 5 inches of rain in about a two hour period with some reports of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down several large tree limbs and power lines around town. One large tree limb landed on a house causing minor roof damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent storm system lifted northeast from Oklahoma through Arkansas and Missouri on January 24th and 25th, bringing widespread precipitation across the Missouri Ozarks beginning as rain during the late morning and afternoon on January 24th before changing over to snow during the afternoon and evening. This snow continued into the morning hours of January 25th before coming to an end as the storm system exited the region.||Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 1 to 3 inches for areas along and northwest of a Joplin to Stockton to Lake of the Ozarks line. There was a sharp cutoff in heavy snow near Interstate 44, with many locations seeing 6 to 8 inches south of Interstate 44 and up to 12 inches near West Plains. Roads became completely covered and slick across the region for the evening commute of January 24th and also into the morning commute on January 25th due to the ongoing snow. Several slide offs and vehicle accidents occurred as a result of the slick road conditions. Numerous schools were closed across the region on January 25th, with some even doing early dismissals on January 24th. Some schools also remain closed into January 26th in the areas that received higher snow amounts across south central Missouri. Total power outages reached over 50 thousand across southern and south central Missouri from the heavy snow, with numerous power outages continuing in Howell, Shannon and Oregon Counties into January 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation started as rain during the morning of January 24th before changing over to snow during the late afternoon, with the snow then continuing into the morning of January 25th. Widespread snow amounts between 1 to around 3 inches fell across the county. A public report of a measured storm total of 2.8 inches of snow was received from 1 mile NE of Dennis Acres, with a measured 1.5 inches from a CoCoRaHS observer in Shoal Creek Estate. Roads became snow covered and slippery, a one vehicle injury accident occurring on Interstate 49 one mile north of Tipton Ford as the vehicle ran off the road and struck a guard rail. Some schools had early release on January 24th, with numerous schools closed on January 25th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong upper ridge of high pressure that has dominated the weather across the southwest US pushed east into the Mid-South toward the end of June. High temperatures combined with high humidity produced heat index values over 105 degrees and in some cases over 110 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat index values reached or exceeded 110 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system and cold front brought light to moderate snowfall across portions of central Missouri, with light snow as far south as the Highway 60 corridor. A band of precipitation with some convective elements moved into portions of central and west central Missouri during the early morning hours of January 12th. Some of the convective elements briefly had sleet with them throughout the morning hours, with precipitation changing over to all snow within about 30 minutes to 2 hours after precipitation onset. Some thunder occurred with the snow in the Lake of the Ozarks region during the early morning hours. As a result, the highest snowfall amounts and rates occurred over central Missouri, with the snow band weakening as it dove southeast across the area during the morning. Precipitation came to an end as flurries and light rain over south central Missouri by early afternoon on January 12th. ||Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 3 to 5 inches across central Missouri and the Lake Ozarks region to as little as 0.5 inches along and just south of the Interstate 44 and Highway 60 corridors across southwestern Missouri. Roads north of Interstate 44, and especially towards the Highway 54 corridor and across the Lake of the Ozarks region, became mostly to completely snow covered during the morning, with partly covered roadways farther south into the Springfield metropolitan area during the morning rush hour into late morning. Multiple slide offs and accidents were reported. About one dozen schools closed for the day, all of which were located across central Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of light to moderate snow moved through during the early morning hours of January 12th. Roads became covered, with slowdowns reported. School closings occurred at Hermitage, Weaubleau, and Hickory County R-1. Public reports from Hermitage and Pittsburg observed 2.0 inches of total snow accumulations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy, wet snow fell across the entire forecast area starting late Tuesday night in southwestern Missouri and moved northeast through the night. Travel became treacherous overnight and impacted the Wednesday morning commute. There were two waves of snow, one overnight Tuesday into early Wednesday, and a second during the Wednesday morning commute. The highest snowfall totals were along and south of I-44 in Missouri and I-64 in Illinois, where a swath of 6 inches plus of snow occurred. Localized totals of 8 inches plus fell across the Ozarks. Snowfall rates of an inch an hour occurred at times late Tuesday night into Wednesday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 6 and 10 inches of snow fell across Reynolds County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Stronger and deeper lift developed with an afternoon event over portions of southeast Missouri on January 30th.  Forecast soundings showed saturation in the DGZ with a shallow warm layer introduced. This resulted in mostly snow and sleet with this event. Conditions deteriorated rapidly with dangerous conditions for travel, especially on the hilly terrain in the warned area. Up to an inch of sleet was reported across the warned area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to six tenths of an inch of sleet was reported in Reynolds County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread snowfall blanketed southeast Missouri during the night of the 24th into the early morning hours of the 25th. Heavy snowfall occurred in most areas except the Kentucky border counties of Mississippi and New Madrid. The heaviest snowfall was in the Ozark foothills north and northwest of Poplar Bluff, over 6 inches in some areas. Carter and Bollinger Counties both reported 7 to 9 inches of snow, which was wet and heavy enough to bring down a lot of tree limbs. Power outages were widespread, and several roads were blocked. Snowfall amounts of 4 to 6 inches were common elsewhere to the north and west of Cape Girardeau, including Perryville, Greenville, Poplar Bluff, and Doniphan. Three to four inches fell from the Dexter/Bloomfield area to Benton in Scott County. Snowfall rates were quite heavy for a few hours, averaging an inch per hour. Only an inch or so fell in Mississippi and New Madrid Counties. Roads were snow-covered and slippery during the heavy snow, with numerous vehicles sliding off the roads. Temperatures rose above freezing soon after the snow ended, so treated main roads improved rather quickly later in the morning. Due to the heavy wet nature of the snow combining with wind gusts of 15 to 20 mph, significant power outages occurred across the Ozark foothills of southeast Missouri. There were over 75,000 customers without power across south central and southeast Missouri at its peak on the morning of January 25th. Numerous downed trees also occurred in these areas.

Widespread snow amounts between 3 and 7 inches fell across the county. A public report of a measured storm total of 7.0 inches of snow was received from Lebanon, with a measured 6.0 inches in Conway. Roads became snow covered and slippery. Up to around 4800 power outages also occurred across the county due to the heavy snow. Some schools had early release on January 24th, with numerous schools closed on January 25th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread snowfall blanketed southeast Missouri during the night of the 24th into the early morning hours of the 25th. Heavy snowfall occurred in most areas except the Kentucky border counties of Mississippi and New Madrid. The heaviest snowfall was in the Ozark foothills north and northwest of Poplar Bluff, over 6 inches in some areas. Carter and Bollinger Counties both reported 7 to 9 inches of snow, which was wet and heavy enough to bring down a lot of tree limbs. Power outages were widespread, and several roads were blocked. Snowfall amounts of 4 to 6 inches were common elsewhere to the north and west of Cape Girardeau, including Perryville, Greenville, Poplar Bluff, and Doniphan. Three to four inches fell from the Dexter/Bloomfield area to Benton in Scott County. Snowfall rates were quite heavy for a few hours, averaging an inch per hour. Only an inch or so fell in Mississippi and New Madrid Counties. Roads were snow-covered and slippery during the heavy snow, with numerous vehicles sliding off the roads. Temperatures rose above freezing soon after the snow ended, so treated main roads improved rather quickly later in the morning. Due to the heavy wet nature of the snow combining with wind gusts of 15 to 20 mph, significant power outages occurred across the Ozark foothills of southeast Missouri. There were over 75,000 customers without power across south central and southeast Missouri at its peak on the morning of January 25th. Numerous downed trees also occurred in these areas.

This round of storms was due to an outflow boundary from earlier activity. As it moved east through the afternoon hours it interacted with a rather unstable air mass with surface based CAPE in excess of 3000 J/kg. The main threat from these storms was damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down a large tree limb.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent storm system lifted northeast from Oklahoma through Arkansas and Missouri on January 24th and 25th, bringing widespread precipitation across the Missouri Ozarks beginning as rain during the late morning and afternoon on January 24th before changing over to snow during the afternoon and evening. This snow continued into the morning hours of January 25th before coming to an end as the storm system exited the region.||Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 1 to 3 inches for areas along and northwest of a Joplin to Stockton to Lake of the Ozarks line. There was a sharp cutoff in heavy snow near Interstate 44, with many locations seeing 6 to 8 inches south of Interstate 44 and up to 12 inches near West Plains. Roads became completely covered and slick across the region for the evening commute of January 24th and also into the morning commute on January 25th due to the ongoing snow. Several slide offs and vehicle accidents occurred as a result of the slick road conditions. Numerous schools were closed across the region on January 25th, with some even doing early dismissals on January 24th. Some schools also remain closed into January 26th in the areas that received higher snow amounts across south central Missouri. Total power outages reached over 50 thousand across southern and south central Missouri from the heavy snow, with numerous power outages continuing in Howell, Shannon and Oregon Counties into January 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation started as rain during the afternoon of January 24th before changing over to snow during the late afternoon, with the snow then continuing into the morning of January 25th. An estimated 1 to 2 inches of snow fell across the county. Roads became partly snow covered and slippery. Around 25 power outages also occurred across the county due to the snow. Some schools had early release on January 24th, with several closed on January 25th.

Strong gradient winds developed across the Delta region ahead of an approaching cold front on February 22, 2023. Winds gusted to over 50 mph causing some minor damage. A couple of severe storms developed along the cold front during the afternoon hours of February 22, 2023, resulting in some damaging straight-line winds across the Delta.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight-line winds damaged the roof of a building at the Malden airport. A small pump station was also damaged a few hundred yards east of the airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought a prolonged 24 to 30 hour period of freezing drizzle across the area, beginning during the early overnight hours of January 29th and continuing throughout the day on January 29th before ending in the early morning hours of January 30th. An additional wintry mix of mainly heavy sleet as well as light snow and light freezing rain impacted areas mainly along and south of the Interstate 44 corridor, starting in the mid to late morning and ending from west to east across the area during the afternoon into early evening. ||Ice accretions were a widespread glaze, with ASOS measured flat ice accumulations ranging from 0.05 inches in West Plains to a max of 0.33 inches in Springfield. Sleet and snow accumulations, which were mainly in the form of sleet, ranged from around 0.25 inches in locations near Interstate 44 to between 1.0 to 1.5 inches near the southern Missouri border.||There were numerous crashes and slide offs across the area on January 29th and 30th, with the Missouri Department of Transportation showing covered and slick roadways throughout the event. One of the crashes in Morgan County also resulted in a fatality. Some roads south of Interstate 44 even remained covered into the day on January 31st. Numerous schools and businesses across the area were closed on January 30th due to the winter road impacts, with numerous schools remaining closed on January 31st along and south of Interstate 44 where roads remained covered and slick.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of precipitation fell across the county, beginning during the early morning hours of January 29th as freezing drizzle and lingering until around midnight early on January 30th. No sleet accumulation reports were available in the county. There was also an estimated light glaze of ice across the county. Roads became covered and slick with numerous schools also closed on January 30th. A one vehicle crash also occurred during the morning of January 29th on Highway 54 two miles west of Collins as a vehicle ran off the slick roadway and overturned, resulting in one injury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm consisting primarily of sleet impacted southeast Missouri during the late afternoon and evening on January 30th. The precipitation moved out before sunrise on the 31st. The precipitation fell quite heavily as soon as it started, and temperatures were well below freezing, so roads quickly became covered with sleet. The sleet compacted down to an icy coating, making for dangerous travel conditions. For the next few days that followed the storm, many secondary roads were ice-covered as temperatures remained well below freezing through the morning of February 2nd. There were numerous vehicle slide-offs and some accidents. Three police officers were injured in three separate crashes on the night of the 31st. A police officer in Miner (Scott County) received minor injuries when a vehicle bounced off a guard rail and into his police cruiser. In Mississippi County, another officer was injured seriously when a semi truck travelling northbound on Interstate 57 crashed into his cruiser, which was parked at a crash scene. Interstate 57 was closed due to the crashes. Another officer was injured on U.S. Highway 60 near Poplar Bluff when a semi rig slid into a tow truck, which then slid into his police cruiser. All lanes of U.S. Highway 60 were closed for several hours due to multiple jackknifed semis. Interstate 55 was also extremely icy and strewn with wrecked vehicles. The Missouri State Highway Patrol troop serving southeast Missouri reported they responded to several dozen crashes and over 100 calls for service. Schools were cancelled in most counties from January 30th through February 2nd. The heaviest sleet accumulations occurred along and north of a line from Doniphan and Poplar Bluff to Sikeston and Charleston, or roughly the U.S. Highway 60 corridor. There was one-half to one inch of sleet in those areas. A light glaze of icing also accompanied the sleet in many areas. The wintry precipitation was lighter to the south. From one-quarter to one-half inch of sleet fell south of Highway 60, including New Madrid. Arctic high pressure over the central Plains pumped low-level cold air into the region on brisk northeast winds. At the same time, a southwest wind flow at 500 mb brought a moist air mass emanating from the sub-tropical Pacific.

The heaviest sleet accumulations occurred along and north of a line from Doniphan and Poplar Bluff to Sikeston and Charleston, or roughly the U.S. Highway 60 corridor. There was one-half to one inch of sleet in those areas. A light glaze of icing also accompanied the sleet in many areas. The wintry precipitation was lighter to the south. From one-quarter to one-half inch of sleet fell south of Highway 60, including New Madrid. Arctic high pressure over the central Plains pumped low-level cold air into the region on brisk northeast winds. At the same time, a southwest wind flow at 500 mb brought a moist air mass emanating from the sub-tropical Pacific.

A winter storm consisting primarily of sleet impacted southeast Missouri during the late afternoon and evening on January 30th. The precipitation moved out before sunrise on the 31st. The precipitation fell quite heavily as soon as it started, and temperatures were well below freezing, so roads quickly became covered with sleet. The sleet compacted down to an icy coating, making for dangerous travel conditions. For the next few days that followed the storm, many secondary roads were ice-covered as temperatures remained well below freezing through the morning of February 2nd. There were numerous vehicle slide-offs and some accidents. Three police officers were injured in three separate crashes on the night of the 31st. A police officer in Miner (Scott County) received minor injuries when a vehicle bounced off a guard rail and into his police cruiser. In Mississippi County, another officer was injured seriously when a semi truck travelling northbound on Interstate 57 crashed into his cruiser, which was parked at a crash scene. Interstate 57 was closed due to the crashes. Another officer was injured on U.S. Highway 60 near Poplar Bluff when a semi rig slid into a tow truck, which then slid into his police cruiser. All lanes of U.S. Highway 60 were closed for several hours due to multiple jackknifed semis. Interstate 55 was also extremely icy and strewn with wrecked vehicles. A period of light freezing rain caused very icy conditions during the overnight and early morning hours. Some places received up to a tenth of an inch of ice, mainly in the Cape Girardeau area. The Missouri State Highway Patrol reported about 40 accidents in the morning, many of which were caused by icy bridges and overpasses. Five of the crashes involved injuries. A particularly bad area was Interstate 55 in Scott and Cape Girardeau Counties, where there were numerous wrecks. The precipitation fell in a shallow cold northeast wind flow around a strong high pressure system over the Plains. Moist southwest winds above the surface cold air generated areas of light freezing rain.

Widespread snowfall blanketed southeast Missouri during the night of the 24th into the early morning hours of the 25th. Heavy snowfall occurred in most areas except the Kentucky border counties of Mississippi and New Madrid. The heaviest snowfall was in the Ozark foothills north and northwest of Poplar Bluff, over 6 inches in some areas. Carter and Bollinger Counties both reported 7 to 9 inches of snow, which was wet and heavy enough to bring down a lot of tree limbs. Power outages were widespread, and several roads were blocked. Snowfall amounts of 4 to 6 inches were common elsewhere to the north and west of Cape Girardeau, including Perryville, Greenville, Poplar Bluff, and Doniphan. Three to four inches fell from the Dexter/Bloomfield area to Benton in Scott County. Snowfall rates were quite heavy for a few hours, averaging an inch per hour. Only an inch or so fell in Mississippi and New Madrid Counties. Roads were snow-covered and slippery during the heavy snow, with numerous vehicles sliding off the roads. Temperatures rose above freezing soon after the snow ended, so treated main roads improved rather quickly later in the morning. Due to the heavy wet nature of the snow combining with wind gusts of 15 to 20 mph, significant power outages occurred across the Ozark foothills of southeast Missouri. There were over 75,000 customers without power across south central and southeast Missouri at its peak on the morning of January 25th. Numerous downed trees also occurred in these areas.

A storm system and cold front brought light to moderate snowfall across portions of central Missouri, with light snow as far south as the Highway 60 corridor. A band of precipitation with some convective elements moved into portions of central and west central Missouri during the early morning hours of January 12th. Some of the convective elements briefly had sleet with them throughout the morning hours, with precipitation changing over to all snow within about 30 minutes to 2 hours after precipitation onset. Some thunder occurred with the snow in the Lake of the Ozarks region during the early morning hours. As a result, the highest snowfall amounts and rates occurred over central Missouri, with the snow band weakening as it dove southeast across the area during the morning. Precipitation came to an end as flurries and light rain over south central Missouri by early afternoon on January 12th. ||Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 3 to 5 inches across central Missouri and the Lake Ozarks region to as little as 0.5 inches along and just south of the Interstate 44 and Highway 60 corridors across southwestern Missouri. Roads north of Interstate 44, and especially towards the Highway 54 corridor and across the Lake of the Ozarks region, became mostly to completely snow covered during the morning, with partly covered roadways farther south into the Springfield metropolitan area during the morning rush hour into late morning. Multiple slide offs and accidents were reported. About one dozen schools closed for the day, all of which were located across central Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of convective showers had some non-impactful sleet briefly mix in before changing over to moderate snow during the morning hours of January 12th. Roads became partly covered, but were slick at times. A vehicle traveling on Highway 32 lost control and traveled off of the left side of the road before overturning. Two injuries were reported with the incident. Total snow accumulations ranged from 1.6 inches at the CoCoRaHS observing site 1 mile north of Lebanon to 4.0 inches from a public report in Eldridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed over northwest Arkansas during the late morning of the 2nd and moved northeastward while expanding in coverage and intensity during the afternoon and evening as a strong upper level trough moved northeastward from west Texas toward the Missouri and Kansas border. The rain persisted until the late morning of the 3rd. Rainfall accumulations in excess of two inches occurred along and east of a line from Cassville to Osage Beach, with embedded accumulations over three inches near Lebanon, Houston and Salem. Low water crossings became flooded at many locations east of Springfield, and minor to moderate river flooding occurred along the Big Piney, Jacks Fork, Gasconade and James Rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway J at the Osage Fork of the Gasconade River due to heavy rainfall and flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent storm system lifted northeast from Oklahoma through Arkansas and Missouri on January 24th and 25th, bringing widespread precipitation across the Missouri Ozarks beginning as rain during the late morning and afternoon on January 24th before changing over to snow during the afternoon and evening. This snow continued into the morning hours of January 25th before coming to an end as the storm system exited the region.||Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 1 to 3 inches for areas along and northwest of a Joplin to Stockton to Lake of the Ozarks line. There was a sharp cutoff in heavy snow near Interstate 44, with many locations seeing 6 to 8 inches south of Interstate 44 and up to 12 inches near West Plains. Roads became completely covered and slick across the region for the evening commute of January 24th and also into the morning commute on January 25th due to the ongoing snow. Several slide offs and vehicle accidents occurred as a result of the slick road conditions. Numerous schools were closed across the region on January 25th, with some even doing early dismissals on January 24th. Some schools also remain closed into January 26th in the areas that received higher snow amounts across south central Missouri. Total power outages reached over 50 thousand across southern and south central Missouri from the heavy snow, with numerous power outages continuing in Howell, Shannon and Oregon Counties into January 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation started as rain during the afternoon of January 24th before changing over to snow during the late afternoon, with the snow then continuing into the morning of January 25th. Widespread snow amounts between 5 and 8 inches fell across the county. A public report of a measured storm total of 8.0 inches of snow was received from Roby, with a measured 7.0 inches in Success and 6.5 inches in Houston. Roads became snow covered and slippery. Around 2700 power outages also occurred across the county due to the heavy snow. Some schools had early release on January 24th, with numerous schools closed on January 25th and January 26th.

A storm system brought a prolonged 24 to 30 hour period of freezing drizzle across the area, beginning during the early overnight hours of January 29th and continuing throughout the day on January 29th before ending in the early morning hours of January 30th. An additional wintry mix of mainly heavy sleet as well as light snow and light freezing rain impacted areas mainly along and south of the Interstate 44 corridor, starting in the mid to late morning and ending from west to east across the area during the afternoon into early evening. ||Ice accretions were a widespread glaze, with ASOS measured flat ice accumulations ranging from 0.05 inches in West Plains to a max of 0.33 inches in Springfield. Sleet and snow accumulations, which were mainly in the form of sleet, ranged from around 0.25 inches in locations near Interstate 44 to between 1.0 to 1.5 inches near the southern Missouri border.||There were numerous crashes and slide offs across the area on January 29th and 30th, with the Missouri Department of Transportation showing covered and slick roadways throughout the event. One of the crashes in Morgan County also resulted in a fatality. Some roads south of Interstate 44 even remained covered into the day on January 31st. Numerous schools and businesses across the area were closed on January 30th due to the winter road impacts, with numerous schools remaining closed on January 31st along and south of Interstate 44 where roads remained covered and slick.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged round of wintry precipitation fell across the county, beginning during the early morning hours of January 29th as freezing drizzle and lingered into the early morning of January 30th. Some sleet mixed in during the last few hours of the freezing drizzle before precipitation ended during the early morning of January 30th. No sleet accumulation reports were available in the county. There was also an estimated tenth to quarter inch of ice accretion from the prolonged freezing drizzle across the county. Numerous schools also closed on January 30th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance interacted with moisture overriding a stalled front over Arkansas to produce showers and a few thunderstorms over the area from the afternoon of the 7th to the early morning of the 8th. While most of the area received between one-quarter to one-half inch of rain, locations along Interstate 44 from Joplin to Springfield and along Highway 60 from Springfield to Cabool received over one and a half inches. Isolated reports of flooding were received from Lawrence, Douglas and Wright counties where soils were already saturated from heavy rain earlier in the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Missouri Highway 97 at the Spring River was closed due to flooding.

This snow continued into the morning hours of January 25th before coming to an end as the storm system exited the region.||Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 1 to 3 inches for areas along and northwest of a Joplin to Stockton to Lake of the Ozarks line. There was a sharp cutoff in heavy snow near Interstate 44, with many locations seeing 6 to 8 inches south of Interstate 44 and up to 12 inches near West Plains. Roads became completely covered and slick across the region for the evening commute of January 24th and also into the morning commute on January 25th due to the ongoing snow. Several slide offs and vehicle accidents occurred as a result of the slick road conditions. Numerous schools were closed across the region on January 25th, with some even doing early dismissals on January 24th. Some schools also remain closed into January 26th in the areas that received higher snow amounts across south central Missouri. Total power outages reached over 50 thousand across southern and south central Missouri from the heavy snow, with numerous power outages continuing in Howell, Shannon and Oregon Counties into January 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation started as rain during the afternoon of January 24th before changing over to snow during the late afternoon, with the snow then continuing into the morning of January 25th. Widespread snow amounts between 5 and 8 inches fell across the county. A COOP observer report of a measured storm total of 8.0 inches of snow was received from 3 miles SSE of Summersville, with a measured 6.5 inches 4 miles WSW of Winona. Roads became snow covered and slippery, with a road closure on Highway 19 between Winona and Alton due to the heavy snow. Around 2600 power outages also occurred across the county due to the heavy snow, with power outages lingering into January 27th. Some schools had early release on January 24th, with numerous schools closed on January 25th and January 26th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed over northwest Arkansas during the late morning of the 2nd and moved northeastward while expanding in coverage and intensity during the afternoon and evening as a strong upper level trough moved northeastward from west Texas toward the Missouri and Kansas border. The rain persisted until the late morning of the 3rd. Rainfall accumulations in excess of two inches occurred along and east of a line from Cassville to Osage Beach, with embedded accumulations over three inches near Lebanon, Houston and Salem. Low water crossings became flooded at many locations east of Springfield, and minor to moderate river flooding occurred along the Big Piney, Jacks Fork, Gasconade and James Rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway TT at Crooked Creek was closed due to flooding from heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of light freezing rain caused very icy conditions during the overnight and early morning hours. Some places received up to a tenth of an inch of ice, mainly in the Cape Girardeau area. The Missouri State Highway Patrol reported about 40 accidents in the morning, many of which were caused by icy bridges and overpasses. Five of the crashes involved injuries. A particularly bad area was Interstate 55 in Scott and Cape Girardeau Counties, where there were numerous wrecks. The precipitation fell in a shallow cold northeast wind flow around a strong high pressure system over the Plains. Moist southwest winds above the surface cold air generated areas of light freezing rain.

Multiple bands of thunderstorms occurred during the evening hours. The storms occurred in the warm sector of a surface low centered over northeast Kansas. The storms were far removed from the primary surface fronts, but a 500 mb shortwave rotating northeast toward the lower and mid-Mississippi Valley provided strong forcing for storms. The storms produced locally heavy rainfall that caused minor flooding of roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported minor flooding of low-lying roads in southern portions of the county. Rainfall amounts were generally between 2 and 3 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought a prolonged 24 to 30 hour period of freezing drizzle across the area, beginning during the early overnight hours of January 29th and continuing throughout the day on January 29th before ending in the early morning hours of January 30th. An additional wintry mix of mainly heavy sleet as well as light snow and light freezing rain impacted areas mainly along and south of the Interstate 44 corridor, starting in the mid to late morning and ending from west to east across the area during the afternoon into early evening. ||Ice accretions were a widespread glaze, with ASOS measured flat ice accumulations ranging from 0.05 inches in West Plains to a max of 0.33 inches in Springfield. Sleet and snow accumulations, which were mainly in the form of sleet, ranged from around 0.25 inches in locations near Interstate 44 to between 1.0 to 1.5 inches near the southern Missouri border.||There were numerous crashes and slide offs across the area on January 29th and 30th, with the Missouri Department of Transportation showing covered and slick roadways throughout the event. One of the crashes in Morgan County also resulted in a fatality. Some roads south of Interstate 44 even remained covered into the day on January 31st. Numerous schools and businesses across the area were closed on January 30th due to the winter road impacts, with numerous schools remaining closed on January 31st along and south of Interstate 44 where roads remained covered and slick.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of precipitation fell across the county, beginning during the early morning hours of January 29th as freezing drizzle and lingering into the very early morning of January 30th. There was also an estimated light glaze of ice across the county. Roads became covered and slick with numerous schools also closed on January 30th. During the evening of January 29th, a vehicle on Highway U near Highway EE was driving too fast for the road conditions and traveled off the road, overturned, and then struck a fence, resulting in two injuries to the occupants of the vehicle.

No reports of sleet were reported across the county. Roads became covered and slick with numerous schools closed on January 30th. The slick roads on January 29th also resulted in an eastbound tractor trailer on Interstate 44 jack knifing, which closed off both lanes of traffic.

Each vehicle lost control on an ice bridge, ran off the side of the road, and then collided with a ditch and bridge, respectively.

Upper-level disturbances moving through strong northwest flow aloft combined with ample moisture and instability to produce scattered showers and thunderstorms across much of the Mid-South during the afternoon hour of June 16, 2023. Some storms were severe with large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar size hail just west of Arbyrd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas of moderate to heavy rain fell over Douglas County causing one low water crossing to be flooded for a brief period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Missouri Highway Y at Cowskin Creek was flooded and impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong upper ridge of high pressure that dominated the weather across the southwest US during the summer of 2023 pushed east into the Mid-South toward the middle of July. High temperatures combined with high humidity produced heat index values over 105 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat index values reached or exceeded 105 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper-level disturbances moving through strong northwest flow aloft combined with ample moisture and instability to produce scattered showers and thunderstorms across much of the Mid-South during the afternoon hour of June 16, 2023. Some storms were severe with large hail and damaging winds.

This round of storms was due to an outflow boundary from earlier activity. As it moved east through the afternoon hours it interacted with a rather unstable air mass with surface based CAPE in excess of 3000 J/kg. The main threat from these storms was damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down numerous tree limbs. In St. Charles, a business sign was blown down onto an unoccupied car causing moderate damage to the vehicle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy, wet snow fell across the entire forecast area starting late Tuesday night in southwestern Missouri and moved northeast through the night. Travel became treacherous overnight and impacted the Wednesday morning commute. There were two waves of snow, one overnight Tuesday into early Wednesday, and a second during the Wednesday morning commute. The highest snowfall totals were along and south of I-44 in Missouri and I-64 in Illinois, where a swath of 6 inches plus of snow occurred. Localized totals of 8 inches plus fell across the Ozarks. Snowfall rates of an inch an hour occurred at times late Tuesday night into Wednesday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 6 and 10 inches of snow fell across St. Francois County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system and cold front brought light to moderate snowfall across portions of central Missouri, with light snow as far south as the Highway 60 corridor. A band of precipitation with some convective elements moved into portions of central and west central Missouri during the early morning hours of January 12th. Some of the convective elements briefly had sleet with them throughout the morning hours, with precipitation changing over to all snow within about 30 minutes to 2 hours after precipitation onset. Some thunder occurred with the snow in the Lake of the Ozarks region during the early morning hours. As a result, the highest snowfall amounts and rates occurred over central Missouri, with the snow band weakening as it dove southeast across the area during the morning. Precipitation came to an end as flurries and light rain over south central Missouri by early afternoon on January 12th. ||Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 3 to 5 inches across central Missouri and the Lake Ozarks region to as little as 0.5 inches along and just south of the Interstate 44 and Highway 60 corridors across southwestern Missouri. Roads north of Interstate 44, and especially towards the Highway 54 corridor and across the Lake of the Ozarks region, became mostly to completely snow covered during the morning, with partly covered roadways farther south into the Springfield metropolitan area during the morning rush hour into late morning. Multiple slide offs and accidents were reported. About one dozen schools closed for the day, all of which were located across central Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of convective showers changed over to moderate to heavy snowfall rates at times during the early morning hours of January 12th, with some thundersnow even reported. Roads became covered, with widespread slowdowns across the county. School closings occurred in Camdenton, Macks Creek, and Climax Springs. There were approximately 100 power outages across the county, as well. Total snow accumulations ranged from 2.0 inches with a public report 3 miles west southwest of Osage Beach to 4.5 inches from a CoCoRaHS observer 1 mile west southwest of Laurie.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 7.5 of snow fell across Daviess County on Jan 21st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A bowing line of thunderstorms produced damaging wind and large hail along a stalled front across southern Missouri.
An upper low tracked through the Great Lakes with weak mid-level troughing across the Mid-South. A landspout developed near Holland, Missouri causing no damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A landspout occurred near Holland, MO.

Just east of Steelville, three trees were blown down along Long Spring Road. About two miles west of Anthonies Mill, a large tree was blown down onto Highway N.

A band of prefrontal showers and embedded thunderstorms associated with a surface low centered over northwestern Missouri, pushed through central  and northeast Missouri then into west central Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down several power lines and a tree on the east side of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early morning squall line brought severe thunderstorm wind gusts and damage to portions of western Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds from a squall line downed a cottonwood tree, blocking a road, near Lake Annette.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early morning squall line brought severe thunderstorm wind gusts and damage to portions of western Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A squall line produced a measured wind gusts of 70 mph just west of Bunceton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southwest winds gradually turned into the west during the day. Peak wind gusts ranged from 48 to 58 mph. The peak measured wind gust in southeast Missouri was 58 mph at the Cape Girardeau airport near Scott City. The peak gust at the Sikeston airport was 52 mph. A strong low pressure system tracked eastward along the Missouri/Iowa border, then across northern Illinois to northern Ohio. The low pressure center weakened from around 984 mb to 990 mb along this track.

A strong upper level trough followed a southern path and interacted with a nearly stationary front near the Arkansas Border to produce strong to severe storms from the late evening of the 15th to the early morning of the 16th. Hail up to two and a half inches in diameter and wind gusts over 60 mph occurred along and south of Highway 60 from Shell Knob to Mountain Grove.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple large 1 to 2 inch diameter limbs were downed from a measured wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system intensified rapidly as it moved northeastward across the central Plains into northern Missouri and southern Iowa during the late evening of the 26th and early morning of the 27th. Strong to severe storms developed ahead of the trailing cold front and produced widespread wind damage and three tornadoes over southwest Missouri during the overnight hours. Numerous reports of wind damage were received east of a line from Joplin to Warsaw.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service storm survey concluded that an EF-1 tornado, with maximum wind speeds of 105 mph, damaged or destroyed more than 10 outbuildings and overturned two RVs. Minor roof damage occurred to a few homes. A few trees were uprooted with large branches broken off of numerous trees. The tornado path was 0.7 miles long with a maximum width of 200 yards. The tornado crossed I-49 with visible debris in the median.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions saw improvement across portions of the southwest Missouri Ozarks during the month. While drought conditions remained, the 3 southwestern Missouri counties that began the month in Severe Drought (D2) had improved to Moderate (D1) drought or Abnormally Dry (D0) conditions or better. The primary impacts of the drought continued to occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop failures, dry stock ponds, very low soil moisture and low flows on some streams and rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall through the month allowed for improvement across the county though Moderate (D1) Drought conditions continued across the region through the end of February. The US Drought Monitor indicated only the northwestern corner of the county remained in Moderate (D1) Drought with the rest of the county having improved to either Abnormally (D0) dry or no drought. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture with farm and stock ponds low but improved. Dry conditions caused grasses to dry and significantly impact hay production causing losses in hay cutting. Many farmers saw impacts to row crops such as soy beans, corn and other specialty crops. Crop loss estimates notated in this month encompass losses from initial drought development in 2022.

Numerous reports of wind damage were received east of a line from Joplin to Warsaw.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service storm survey concluded that an EF-0 tornado, with maximum wind speeds of 76 mph, uprooted a few weak trees with large branches also broken off. No damage to structures was noted along the tornadoes path of 1.2 miles long with a maximum width of 100 yards.

Numerous reports of wind damage were received east of a line from Joplin to Warsaw.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two large trees were reported down and blocking Highway NN. A shed was also reported to have collapsed due to high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation deficits over the previous several months yielded D2 drought in Atchison County MO going into February. D2 drought finally improved by Feb 21st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: February began with D2 drought across much of Atchison County, MO. Precipitation in early to mid February finally ended the D2 drought by the Feb 21st US Drought Monitor update.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions saw some minor improvements across portions of the southwest Missouri Ozarks during the month but drought remains. Across the Ozarks, the month started with 3 counties in Severe Drought (D2) or Moderate (D1) and by the end of the month no changes had occurred. The Severe Drought (D2) saw little improvement with only limited rainfall occurring. The primary impacts of the drought continued to occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop failures, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Limited rainfall during the month gave little improvement across the county, drought conditions continued across the region through the month of January. The US Drought Monitor indicated the southwestern half of the county as being in Severe (D2) Drought with the remainder of county in Moderate (D1) Drought to Abnormally Dry (D0). Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture. Farm and stock ponds saw some continuing impacts with ground water levels and reservoirs showing slight drought condition improvement. Estimated monetary crop losses from 2022 through the end of drought in 2023 will be included in a future report when the drought ends and loses are assessed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developed over the central Plains near the Kansas and Nebraska border during the afternoon of the 7th and moved southeastward across central and south central Missouri from the pre-dawn hours to the early morning of the 8th. These storms left an outflow boundary over central Missouri that sparked a second round of storms during the afternoon. These storms were much stronger and produced hail across central Missouri through the evening of the  8th. The strongest storm produced hail to the size of baseballs in Dent County near Stone Hill. Rainfall amounts over two inches occurred with these storms near Nevada, Lake of the Ozarks, and north of Salem.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to quarter sized hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The upper-level ridge over the Mid-South began to weaken and northwest flow aloft developed across the region. Outflow boundaries from earlier convection over Missouri helped trigger isolated to scattered showers and thunderstorms over mainly the northern half of the Mid-South throughout the day into the evening. Adequate wind shear combined with strong instability helped some storms become severe with damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two large trees were uprooted and fell on a home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This round of storms was due to an outflow boundary from earlier activity. As it moved east through the afternoon hours it interacted with a rather unstable air mass with surface based CAPE in excess of 3000 J/kg. The main threat from these storms was damaging winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions saw improvement across portions of the southwest Missouri Ozarks during the month. While drought conditions remained, the 3 southwestern Missouri counties that began the month in Severe Drought (D2) had improved to Moderate (D1) drought or Abnormally Dry (D0) conditions or better. The primary impacts of the drought continued to occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop failures, dry stock ponds, very low soil moisture and low flows on some streams and rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall through the month allowed for improvement across the county though Moderate (D1) Drought conditions continued across the region through the end of February. The US Drought Monitor indicated only the southwestern third of the county  remained in Moderate (D1) Drought with the rest of the county having improved to either Abnormally (D0) dry or no drought.  Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture with farm and stock ponds low but improved. Dry conditions caused grasses to dry and significantly impact hay production causing losses in hay cutting. Many farmers saw impacts to row crops such as soy beans, corn and other specialty crops. Crop loss estimates notated in this month encompass losses from initial drought development in 2022.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure developed over western Arkansas, with a warm front arcing east-northeast toward the lower Ohio Valley. Very small amounts of instability near the warm front were sufficient for narrow lines of convective rain showers. Strong winds aloft translated down to the surface in the heaviest showers, causing isolated minor damage. The lines of heavy showers repeatedly moved over some of the same areas, resulting in isolated pockets of flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong and gusty southwest winds occurred during the overnight hours of the 8th into the late morning on the 9th. The strong winds occurred as a low pressure center tracked northeast from central Missouri to the Michigan/Indiana border area. Peak wind gusts were measured up to 52 mph at Cape Girardeau, otherwise mostly from 40 to 45 mph. The strong winds were confined to areas from Poplar Bluff east and northeast, including Marble Hill and Perryville.

A low pressure system intensified rapidly as it moved northeastward across the central Plains into northern Missouri and southern Iowa during the late evening of the 26th and early morning of the 27th. Strong to severe storms developed ahead of the trailing cold front and produced widespread wind damage and three tornadoes over southwest Missouri during the overnight hours. Numerous reports of wind damage were received east of a line from Joplin to Warsaw.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developed just south of the I-70 corridor during the early morning hours of July 17th. With precipitable water values between 1.5 and 2.0 inches and the storms oriented in a west to east line, training caused heavy rainfall in some isolated areas with 1 to 3 inches (very localized 4 to 5 inches) of rain. So there were reports of flash flooding in a few locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 4 inches of rain fell in a short amount of time, mainly over southern portions of St. Louis County, causing flash flooding. Several secondary roads had water over them. Also, Mattesse Creek, rose to 12 feet, a foot above Flood Stage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was knocked down on power lines in Senath.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat index values reached or exceeded 110 degrees.
