EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough followed a southern path and interacted with a nearly stationary front near the Arkansas Border to produce strong to severe storms from the late evening of the 15th to the early morning of the 16th. Hail up to two and a half inches in diameter and wind gusts over 60 mph occurred along and south of Highway 60 from Shell Knob to Mountain Grove.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail to the size of quarters was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of thunderstorms intensified as it raced east-northeast, causing several locally damaging wind events. The storms were associated with a compact 500 mb shortwave trough and related jet streak that raced northeast from the southern Plains. Within the warm sector of the surface low, a low-topped broken line of thunderstorms advanced northeast across southeast Missouri. Unseasonably warm surface temperatures in the lower 70's and surface dew points in the lower 60's contributed to a modest diurnal uptick in intensity related to afternoon heating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were down due to the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developed over the central Plains near the Kansas and Nebraska border during the afternoon of the 7th and moved southeastward across central and south central Missouri from the pre-dawn hours to the early morning of the 8th. These storms left an outflow boundary over central Missouri that sparked a second round of storms during the afternoon. These storms were much stronger and produced hail across central Missouri through the evening of the  8th. The strongest storm produced hail to the size of baseballs in Dent County near Stone Hill. Rainfall amounts over two inches occurred with these storms near Nevada, Lake of the Ozarks, and north of Salem.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickle to quarter sized hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southwest winds gradually turned into the west during the day. Peak wind gusts ranged from 48 to 58 mph. The peak measured wind gust in southeast Missouri was 58 mph at the Cape Girardeau airport near Scott City. The peak gust at the Sikeston airport was 52 mph. A strong low pressure system tracked eastward along the Missouri/Iowa border, then across northern Illinois to northern Ohio. The low pressure center weakened from around 984 mb to 990 mb along this track.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions saw some minor improvements across portions of the southwest Missouri Ozarks during the month but drought remains. Across the Ozarks, the month started with 3 counties in Severe Drought (D2) or Moderate (D1) and by the end of the month no changes had occurred. The Severe Drought (D2) saw little improvement with only limited rainfall occurring. The primary impacts of the drought continued to occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop failures, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Limited rainfall through the month and very little improvement occurred across the county, drought conditions continued across the region through the month of January. The US Drought Monitor indicated most of the county as being in Severe (D2) Drought with the remainder of county in Moderate (D1) Drought. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture. Farm and stock ponds saw some continuing impacts with ground water levels and reservoirs showing slight drought condition improvement.  Estimated monetary crop losses from 2022 through the end of drought in 2023 will be included in a future report when the drought ends and loses are assessed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercells brought widespread severe weather to portions of Missouri on the afternoon and evening hours of April 4th and the early morning hours of April 5th. Hail was the primary hazard, with around 30 hail LSRs, including a few 2 hail reports. The severe weather for this day was well advertised, with an enhanced risk from SPC over the vast majority of the forecast area.

Strong southwest winds gradually turned into the west during the day. Peak wind gusts ranged from 48 to 58 mph. The peak measured wind gust in southeast Missouri was 58 mph at the Cape Girardeau airport near Scott City. The peak gust at the Sikeston airport was 52 mph. A strong low pressure system tracked eastward along the Missouri/Iowa border, then across northern Illinois to northern Ohio. The low pressure center weakened from around 984 mb to 990 mb along this track.

Even though an old outflow boundary was across southeast Missouri and the far southern portions of Illinois during the morning hours, the boundary dissipated, allowing 75+ dew points to surge north along the Mississippi River. As skies cleared the atmosphere was able to quickly destabilize. Surface based CAPEs were in excess of 3000 J/kg with 40kts of 0 to 6km bulk shear. This combined with a shortwave trough that moved eastward across the region, produced strong to severe storms. There were numerous reports of damaging winds and some reports of large hail. There were three fatalities due to fallen trees.

The most impactful storm system to affect the region during the month of March began during the late afternoon and early evening of the 23rd as strong to severe thunderstorms trained along and north of a front that had stalled over northwest Arkansas and south central Missouri. While the storms produced sporadic wind damage and hail, rainfall accumulations exceeded three inches within a 6-hour period during the evening of the 23rd. This led to numerous flooded low water crossings, evacuations at Roaring River State Park, and swift water rescues from McDonald County to Dent County. Unfortunately, there were three flash flood related fatalities that occurred - two fatalities occurred along Parks Creek to the southwest of Grovespring in Wright County, and one fatality occurred in southern Webster County along Finley Creek. In both instances, people drove across flooded low water crossings and were swept off the road. Minor to moderate river flooding occurred along many of the larger creeks and rivers including the James, Jacks Fork, Elk, Spring, Current, Big Piney, Roubidoux and Gasconade Rivers and Shoal Creek. Rainfall accumulations averaging around two inches for the 24-hour period ending at 6 PM on the 24th occurred along and east of a line from Neosho to Osage Beach. Within this area, there was a swath of over five inches of rain from Billings to Cabool.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roof damage was reported to several barns and a residence in the Sarvis Point Road area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A highly-anticipated regional severe weather outbreak was forecast and occurred during the afternoon and evening of March 31st. Convective initiation initially occurred relatively early (7-9 am) in southeast Kansas and evolved into east central Kansas into west central Missouri. These storms were generally elevated, posing a hail threat while rapidly moving off to the northeast at 65 mph.  A new line of scattered storms developed around midday and quickly matured into several long-lived supercells. Significant hail near 3.00��� was reported near Fayette, MO. ||Towards the end of the severe weather, very deep mixing occurred behind the dryline, leading to strong wind gusts at the surface. The gusts may have also been enhanced by virga/light showers, aiding downward momentum. Multiple non-thunderstorm wind gusts in excess of 58 mph, with multiple damage reports were received, including the KC Metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A supercell produced 1.25 hail at Whiteman AFB in eastern Johnson County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong upper level ridge built up over the region from July 25th through 29th. This ushered in much warmer air and higher dewpoints. So heat index values 100+ were reported from July 25th through 29th. The hottest days were July 27th, 28th and 29th, so an excessive heat warning was issued for portions of northeast/east central Missouri and west central/southwest Illinois. At least 50 people were seen in hospital emergency room departments during this three day period in St. Louis County/City for heat-related illnesses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive heat across the county with highs in the mid to upper 90s each day. Heat index values as high as 110 both days.

Convective initiation initially occurred relatively early (7-9 am) in southeast Kansas and evolved into east central Kansas into west central Missouri. These storms were generally elevated, posing a hail threat while rapidly moving off to the northeast at 65 mph.  A new line of scattered storms developed around midday and quickly matured into several long-lived supercells. Significant hail near 3.00��� was reported near Fayette, MO. ||Towards the end of the severe weather, very deep mixing occurred behind the dryline, leading to strong wind gusts at the surface. The gusts may have also been enhanced by virga/light showers, aiding downward momentum. Multiple non-thunderstorm wind gusts in excess of 58 mph, with multiple damage reports were received, including the KC Metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A supercell produced 1 hail in just east of Nelson in northwestern Cooper County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Even though an old outflow boundary was across southeast Missouri and the far southern portions of Illinois during the morning hours, the boundary dissipated, allowing 75+ dew points to surge north along the Mississippi River. As skies cleared the atmosphere was able to quickly destabilize. Surface based CAPEs were in excess of 3000 J/kg with 40kts of 0 to 6km bulk shear. This combined with a shortwave trough that moved eastward across the region, produced strong to severe storms. There were numerous reports of damaging winds and some reports of large hail. There were three fatalities due to fallen trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds snapped a maple tree off near the base.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Even though an old outflow boundary was across southeast Missouri and the far southern portions of Illinois during the morning hours, the boundary dissipated, allowing 75+ dew points to surge north along the Mississippi River. As skies cleared the atmosphere was able to quickly destabilize. Surface based CAPEs were in excess of 3000 J/kg with 40kts of 0 to 6km bulk shear. This combined with a shortwave trough that moved eastward across the region, produced strong to severe storms. There were numerous reports of damaging winds and some reports of large hail. There were three fatalities due to fallen trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread wind damage across the northern portions of St. Louis County as a line of storms moved through. Numerous large trees, tree limbs, power poles and power lines were blown down. In Jennings, a very large tree was snapped off (possibly due to a lightning strike) and landed on a home, crushing a 5 year old boy in his bedroom. He was rescued and taken to a nearby hospital, but was pronounced dead soon after. The house next door sustained major roof damage from this large tree as well.  A large tree branch, approximately two feet in diameter, was blown down onto an unoccupied car just east of Chesterfield. In Kirkwood, a large tree was uprooted and as it fell it damaged the corner of a home. Otherwise, widespread tree damage was reported in St. Ann, Clayton, University City, Creve Coeur, Overland, Florissant, Black Jack and Spanish Lake. Over 100,000 customers (St. Louis County/St. Louis City) were without power due to this storm. It took a little over 4 days to restore power to all of them.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The most impactful storm system to affect the region during the month of March began during the late afternoon and early evening of the 23rd as strong to severe thunderstorms trained along and north of a front that had stalled over northwest Arkansas and south central Missouri. While the storms produced sporadic wind damage and hail, rainfall accumulations exceeded three inches within a 6-hour period during the evening of the 23rd. This led to numerous flooded low water crossings, evacuations at Roaring River State Park, and swift water rescues from McDonald County to Dent County. Unfortunately, there were three flash flood related fatalities that occurred - two fatalities occurred along Parks Creek to the southwest of Grovespring in Wright County, and one fatality occurred in southern Webster County along Finley Creek. In both instances, people drove across flooded low water crossings and were swept off the road. Minor to moderate river flooding occurred along many of the larger creeks and rivers including the James, Jacks Fork, Elk, Spring, Current, Big Piney, Roubidoux and Gasconade Rivers and Shoal Creek. Rainfall accumulations averaging around two inches for the 24-hour period ending at 6 PM on the 24th occurred along and east of a line from Neosho to Osage Beach. Within this area, there was a swath of over five inches of rain from Billings to Cabool.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree limb was reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass settled over the Mid-South from late January into early February with a front stalled just south of the region. A series of upper-level disturbances overrunning that arctic air resulted in three rounds of wintry precipitation with some significant tree and powerline damage. Travel was also impacted across the Mid-South. The first round started late on January 30th and continued until the morning of January 31st. Heavy sleet fell across the northern tier of the Mid-South from northeast Arkansas into the Missouri Bootheel and northwest Tennessee. Heavy freezing rain fell across parts of east-central Arkansas with lighter amounts into West Tennessee along the I-40 corridor. Round two moved into the Mid-South during the afternoon and evening of January 31st with sleet along and north of the Tennessee/Mississippi state line, heaviest across the Memphis metropolitan area, and light freezing rain across extreme northern Mississippi.  The third round started on the evening of February 1st and ended on the morning of February 2nd. Freezing rain spread across areas along and south of the Interstate 40 corridor including east-central Arkansas, northwest Mississippi, and southwest Tennessee. This round resulted in numerous power outages across east-central Arkansas, northwest Mississippi, and southwest Tennessee. There was significant tree damage across extreme northwest Mississippi.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over one-half inch of sleet fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A highly-anticipated regional severe weather outbreak was forecast and occurred during the afternoon and evening of March 31st. Convective initiation initially occurred relatively early (7-9 am) in southeast Kansas and evolved into east central Kansas into west central Missouri. These storms were generally elevated, posing a hail threat while rapidly moving off to the northeast at 65 mph.  A new line of scattered storms developed around midday and quickly matured into several long-lived supercells. Significant hail near 3.00��� was reported near Fayette, MO. ||Towards the end of the severe weather, very deep mixing occurred behind the dryline, leading to strong wind gusts at the surface. The gusts may have also been enhanced by virga/light showers, aiding downward momentum. Multiple non-thunderstorm wind gusts in excess of 58 mph, with multiple damage reports were received, including the KC Metro area.

A mesoscale convective vortex moved through weak west-northwest flow aloft over the Lower Mississippi Valley and triggered a round of showers and thunderstorms across northeast Arkansas and the Missouri Bootheel during the evening hours of July 6, 2023. There were a few damaging wind reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and power lines were knocked down near the intersection of Highway C and Highway N in the Europa community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a cluster of storms moved east across southern Missouri into southern Illinois, some of the storms became severe with a few reports of damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds split a large tree in town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of very hot and extremely humid weather began on Sunday, August 20 and lasted through Friday, August 25. Although the temperature rarely threatened climatological records, the humidity caused the heat index to reach extreme levels. The combination of heat and humidity led to several heat related illnesses across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of very hot and extremely humid weather began on Sunday, August 20 and lasted through Friday, August 25. The combination of heat and humidity led to heat index values at the Jefferson City ASOS reaching as high as 117.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted northward off the Gulf Coast, causing rapid moisture transport to the Mid-South. In addition, an upper-level trough and associated cold front approached the area. This led to showers in the afternoon and then severe thunderstorms that continued through the overnight hours. Damaging wind, hail, tornadoes, and flash flooding were the result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rainfall total of 3.75 inches was reported from the Mesonet Station MOA11 Portageville-Delta Center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A marginally severe environment resulted in generally disorganized scattered convection in the late afternoon. A weak convective line and separate cell produced severe wind gusts and isolated damage in portions of southeast and east central Missouri, respectively.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple sources including the public and amateur radio operators reported estimated 60mph wind gusts in the Farmington area. Only minor tree damage was noted from the gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A highly-anticipated regional severe weather outbreak was forecast and occurred during the afternoon and evening of March 31st. Convective initiation initially occurred relatively early (7-9 am) in southeast Kansas and evolved into east central Kansas into west central Missouri. These storms were generally elevated, posing a hail threat while rapidly moving off to the northeast at 65 mph.  A new line of scattered storms developed around midday and quickly matured into several long-lived supercells. Significant hail near 3.00��� was reported near Fayette, MO. ||Towards the end of the severe weather, very deep mixing occurred behind the dryline, leading to strong wind gusts at the surface. The gusts may have also been enhanced by virga/light showers, aiding downward momentum. Multiple non-thunderstorm wind gusts in excess of 58 mph, with multiple damage reports were received, including the KC Metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A metal lean-to was knocked over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A west-to-east oriented instability gradient over Missouri resulted in weakening thunderstorms as they entered central Missouri. One dying thunderstorm produced sporadic tree damage in Boone County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sporadic tree damage was reported in Ashland, including tree that was uprooted. No information on the trees was given.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass settled over the Mid-South from late January into early February with a front stalled just south of the region. A series of upper-level disturbances overrunning that arctic air resulted in three rounds of wintry precipitation with some significant tree and powerline damage. Travel was also impacted across the Mid-South. The first round started late on January 30th and continued until the morning of January 31st. Heavy sleet fell across the northern tier of the Mid-South from northeast Arkansas into the Missouri Bootheel and northwest Tennessee. Heavy freezing rain fell across parts of east-central Arkansas with lighter amounts into West Tennessee along the I-40 corridor. Round two moved into the Mid-South during the afternoon and evening of January 31st with sleet along and north of the Tennessee/Mississippi state line, heaviest across the Memphis metropolitan area, and light freezing rain across extreme northern Mississippi.  The third round started on the evening of February 1st and ended on the morning of February 2nd. Freezing rain spread across areas along and south of the Interstate 40 corridor including east-central Arkansas, northwest Mississippi, and southwest Tennessee. This round resulted in numerous power outages across east-central Arkansas, northwest Mississippi, and southwest Tennessee. There was significant tree damage across extreme northwest Mississippi.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over one-half inch of sleet fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of very hot and extremely humid weather began on Sunday, August 20 and lasted through Friday, August 25. Although the temperature rarely threatened climatological records, the humidity caused the heat index to reach extreme levels. The combination of heat and humidity led to several heat related illnesses across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of very hot and extremely humid weather began on Sunday, August 20 and lasted through Friday, August 25. The combination of heat and humidity led to heat index values of 110 to 120 as observed at numerous reporting sites in the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A highly-anticipated regional severe weather outbreak was forecast and occurred during the afternoon and evening of March 31st. Convective initiation initially occurred relatively early (7-9 am) in southeast Kansas and evolved into east central Kansas into west central Missouri. These storms were generally elevated, posing a hail threat while rapidly moving off to the northeast at 65 mph.  A new line of scattered storms developed around midday and quickly matured into several long-lived supercells. Significant hail near 3.00��� was reported near Fayette, MO. ||Towards the end of the severe weather, very deep mixing occurred behind the dryline, leading to strong wind gusts at the surface. The gusts may have also been enhanced by virga/light showers, aiding downward momentum. Multiple non-thunderstorm wind gusts in excess of 58 mph, with multiple damage reports were received, including the KC Metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong synoptically driven winds brought gusts between 50 and 60 mph to the Kansas City metro area. Aside from numerous locations reporting stronger than 58 mph winds, there was a residence in Greenwood that had shingles and tar paper removed from the house. There were also numerous powerlines and trees down around that area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Even though an old outflow boundary was across southeast Missouri and the far southern portions of Illinois during the morning hours, the boundary dissipated, allowing 75+ dew points to surge north along the Mississippi River. As skies cleared the atmosphere was able to quickly destabilize. Surface based CAPEs were in excess of 3000 J/kg with 40kts of 0 to 6km bulk shear. This combined with a shortwave trough that moved eastward across the region, produced strong to severe storms. There were numerous reports of damaging winds and some reports of large hail. There were three fatalities due to fallen trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down several trees and tree limbs around Knob Lick.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled surface boundary extending from near Kansas City through central Illinois provided a focus for organized convection during the afternoon. Thunderstorms were mainly hail producers, with up to 1.75 inch diameter hailstones reported in the St. Louis metropolitan area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A discrete thunderstorm produced mainly half inch to nickel sized hailstones in southern St. Louis County according to emergency managers, trained spotters, and the public. There was one public report of one inch hail supported by a photo of the hailstone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled surface boundary extending from near Kansas City through central Illinois provided a focus for organized convection during the afternoon. Thunderstorms were mainly hail producers, with up to 1.75 inch diameter hailstones reported in the St. Louis metropolitan area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An intensifying discrete thunderstorm produced quarter sized hail along I-70 in Warren County. This storm went on to produce up to golf ball sized hail just after crossing the border into St. Charles County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The most impactful storm system to affect the region during the month of March began during the late afternoon and early evening of the 23rd as strong to severe thunderstorms trained along and north of a front that had stalled over northwest Arkansas and south central Missouri. While the storms produced sporadic wind damage and hail, rainfall accumulations exceeded three inches within a 6-hour period during the evening of the 23rd. This led to numerous flooded low water crossings, evacuations at Roaring River State Park, and swift water rescues from McDonald County to Dent County. Unfortunately, there were three flash flood related fatalities that occurred - two fatalities occurred along Parks Creek to the southwest of Grovespring in Wright County, and one fatality occurred in southern Webster County along Finley Creek. In both instances, people drove across flooded low water crossings and were swept off the road. Minor to moderate river flooding occurred along many of the larger creeks and rivers including the James, Jacks Fork, Elk, Spring, Current, Big Piney, Roubidoux and Gasconade Rivers and Shoal Creek. Rainfall accumulations averaging around two inches for the 24-hour period ending at 6 PM on the 24th occurred along and east of a line from Neosho to Osage Beach. Within this area, there was a swath of over five inches of rain from Billings to Cabool.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar sized hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A highly-anticipated regional severe weather outbreak was forecast and occurred during the afternoon and evening of March 31st. Convective initiation initially occurred relatively early (7-9 am) in southeast Kansas and evolved into east central Kansas into west central Missouri. These storms were generally elevated, posing a hail threat while rapidly moving off to the northeast at 65 mph.  A new line of scattered storms developed around midday and quickly matured into several long-lived supercells. Significant hail near 3.00��� was reported near Fayette, MO. ||Towards the end of the severe weather, very deep mixing occurred behind the dryline, leading to strong wind gusts at the surface. The gusts may have also been enhanced by virga/light showers, aiding downward momentum. Multiple non-thunderstorm wind gusts in excess of 58 mph, with multiple damage reports were received, including the KC Metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong, synoptically driven winds played a role in a shed being destroyed in La Plata. The fire and rescue personnel estimated 65 mph wind.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developed just south of the I-70 corridor during the early morning hours of July 17th. With precipitable water values between 1.5 and 2.0 inches and the storms oriented in a west to east line, training caused heavy rainfall in some isolated areas with 1 to 3 inches (very localized 4 to 5 inches) of rain. So there were reports of flash flooding in a few locations.

Storms developed just south of the I-70 corridor during the early morning hours of July 17th. With precipitable water values between 1.5 and 2.0 inches and the storms oriented in a west to east line, training caused heavy rainfall in some isolated areas with 1 to 3 inches (very localized 4 to 5 inches) of rain. So there were reports of flash flooding in a few locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 4 and 5 inches of rain fell in a short amount of time causing flash flooding. Portions of U.S. Highway 50 was flooded, particularly around the intersection of U.S. Highway 50 and Highway 19.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Even though an old outflow boundary was across southeast Missouri and the far southern portions of Illinois during the morning hours, the boundary dissipated, allowing 75+ dew points to surge north along the Mississippi River. As skies cleared the atmosphere was able to quickly destabilize. Surface based CAPEs were in excess of 3000 J/kg with 40kts of 0 to 6km bulk shear. This combined with a shortwave trough that moved eastward across the region, produced strong to severe storms. There were numerous reports of damaging winds and some reports of large hail. There were three fatalities due to fallen trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of trees and tree limbs blown down between Cherryville and Leasburg. Some of the fallen trees blocked roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A highly-anticipated regional severe weather outbreak was forecast and occurred during the afternoon and evening of March 31st. Convective initiation initially occurred relatively early (7-9 am) in southeast Kansas and evolved into east central Kansas into west central Missouri. These storms were generally elevated, posing a hail threat while rapidly moving off to the northeast at 65 mph.  A new line of scattered storms developed around midday and quickly matured into several long-lived supercells. Significant hail near 3.00��� was reported near Fayette, MO. ||Towards the end of the severe weather, very deep mixing occurred behind the dryline, leading to strong wind gusts at the surface. The gusts may have also been enhanced by virga/light showers, aiding downward momentum. Multiple non-thunderstorm wind gusts in excess of 58 mph, with multiple damage reports were received, including the KC Metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A supercell produced 1 hail in Salisbury in Chariton County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Even though an old outflow boundary was across southeast Missouri and the far southern portions of Illinois during the morning hours, the boundary dissipated, allowing 75+ dew points to surge north along the Mississippi River. As skies cleared the atmosphere was able to quickly destabilize. Surface based CAPEs were in excess of 3000 J/kg with 40kts of 0 to 6km bulk shear. This combined with a shortwave trough that moved eastward across the region, produced strong to severe storms. There were numerous reports of damaging winds and some reports of large hail. There were three fatalities due to fallen trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down a large tree damaging an unoccupied car in Ste. Genevieve. In Saint Mary, several large tree limbs were blown down around town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several strong to severe thunderstorms moved into eastern Missouri in the afternoon hours of April 15th. These storms were initially producing copious large hail, but rapidly transitioned into tornadic storms. The first tornado was associated with a supercell ahead of a congealing line of convection moving rapidly toward the Mississippi River. A portion of this line of storms rapidly surged eastward, resulting in numerous tornadoes in Jefferson county in Missouri, and Monroe, Madison, St. Clair, and Washington counties in Illinois. In total, 12 tornadoes have been identified, including 3 EF-1s, 6 EF-0s, and 3 tornadoes of unknown intensity that produced no damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: South of the tornado path, there was scattered wind damage with minor shingle damage on several homes and businesses as well as numerous tree limbs blown down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down numerous large tree limbs between St. Louis Lambert International Airport and Black Jack.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cyclic supercell moved northeast from Arkansas, producing two tornadoes in southeast Missouri. Both tornadoes occurred in heavily forested areas. The second tornado struck a small community called Glenallen, located near Marble Hill in Bollinger County. Several fatalities were associated with this significant tornado. The supercell was the product of a favorable environment for tornadic storms. Moderate instability and strong deep-layer wind shear coincided over the region. Mixed-layer capes were between 1000 and 1500 j/kg in the presence of effective bulk shear around 55 knots. A surface low tracked from central Kansas into Iowa, resulting in a large unstable warm sector over Missouri and Illinois. Strong low-level south to southwest winds brought unseasonably warm late-night temperatures in the mid 70's and dew points in the 60's.  The onset of storms was delayed by capping in the warm sector and pockets of dry low-level air. Wind fields strengthened throughout the night and capping weakened, leading to nocturnal supercell tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado passed from Bollinger County into Cape Girardeau County for just a minute or so before dissipating. The only visible damage was broken large branches on County Road 352 near the county line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developed along the southwest Missouri/southeast Kansas border during the early afternoon and tracked eastward across the area through the early to mid evening. Hail up to four inches in diameter was reported near the town of Arcola, in Dade County. Other reports of two inch hail or larger were received from Barton, Cedar, Dallas, Greene, Hickory, Polk, and Taney Counties. The Automated Surface Observation System (ASOS) at Vichy in Maries County recorded a wind gust of 97 mph. It was later confirmed that an EF-2 tornado moved over the area, resulting in 5 injuries to the residents of a house that was heavily damaged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado formed around 6:24pm CDT near the corner of Elm and 3rd streets on the northwest side of downtown Hillsboro, causing minor damage to the siding of the Good Shepherd Catholic Church. It continued eastward along the northern periphery of the downtown area, producing sporadic minor tree damage as it did so. When it approached Business Highway 21, the tornado damaged the facade and steeple of the First Baptist Church. Across the highway along 4th Street, the roof of the fire department building and a residence received minor damage. As the tornado moved over Vreeland Road and tracked along Sunny Lane, it briefly intensified, with several trees uprooted along the lane and minor roof damage occurring on an outbuilding. The tornado then weakened as it neared the end of Sunny Lane, dissipating in the wooded area just beyond the end of the roadway. Overall the tornado was rated EF0 with a max wind speed of 77 mph. The path length was 0.57 miles with a max path width of 200 yards. No deaths or injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado formed on the northwest side of Pevely, MO around 634 PM CDT and tracked to the southeast into the northern portions of town producing minor roof, tree, siding and sign damage. After it crossed Pleasant Road it tracked in a more easterly direction, causing additional minor tree, power line and roof damage before lifting and dissipating on the western side of Oak Valley Golf Course and Resort. Overall the tornado was rated EF1 with a max wind speed of 100 mph. The path length was 0.93 miles with a max path width of 150 yards. No deaths or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several strong to severe thunderstorms moved into eastern Missouri in the afternoon hours of April 15th. The first tornado was associated with a supercell ahead of a congealing line of convection moving rapidly toward the Mississippi River. A portion of this line of storms rapidly surged eastward, resulting in numerous tornadoes in Jefferson county in Missouri, and Monroe, Madison, St. Clair, and Washington counties in Illinois. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado formed northeast of Festus, MO along Old A Highway just after 6:30pm CDT on April 15. It moved northeastward through a subdivision off of Old A Highway and west of West City Park, producing sporadic minor structural damage. As the tornado moved into West City Park, it increased in strength, snapping and uprooting several trees. As it moved over Horine Road, it reached peak strength and produced significant damage to a home. The tornado weakened slightly as it continued on to Interstate 55, producing minor damage to homes, outbuildings, and trees as it did so. As the tornado crossed I-55, it overturned a southbound semi causing minor injuries to the driver. After crossing Interstate 55, the tornado turned more northeasterly and crossed McNutt Street and Lake Drive in Herculaneum, continuing to produce additional minor structural and tree damage. The tornado then turned more easterly and tracked along Joachim Avenue and just south of Herculaneum High School, where it produced minor roof damage to a church and knocked over light poles. As it entered the Dunkin and Fletcher Memorial Park it weakened and dissipated. Overall the tornado was rated EF1 with a max wind speed of 100 mph. The path length was 3.97 miles with a max path width of 360 yards. One injury was reported, but no fatalities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developed along the southwest Missouri/southeast Kansas border during the early afternoon and tracked eastward across the area through the early to mid evening. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service storms survey determined that an EF-2 tornado with maximum winds of 122 mph touched down west of Vichy and traveled 6.8 miles lifting just northeast of the Vichy/Rolla airport. The path of the tornado was intermittent with mostly EF-1 damage but EF-2 damage did occur west of Vichy to north east of Vichy.  Along the path, which was 880 yards at its widest, multiple trees were uprooted or snapped. A home suffered significant damage to exterior walls and some interior walls. Several other homes suffered minor siding damage. Two metal frame buildings lost roofs, and another metal building had two walls blown down at the Vichy/Rolla Airport where the ASOS measured 97 mph winds. Several outbuildings suffered minor to major damage, two aircraft suffered minor damage. Five people suffered minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of very hot and extremely humid weather began on Sunday, August 20 and lasted through Friday, August 25. Although the temperature rarely threatened climatological records, the humidity caused the heat index to reach extreme levels. The combination of heat and humidity led to several heat related illnesses across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of very hot and extremely humid weather began on Sunday, August 20 and lasted through Friday, August 25. The combination of heat and humidity led to heat index values of 110 to 115 as observed at numerous reporting sites in the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong south to southwest winds gusted from 40 to 50 mph ahead of a cold front that trailed southwest from northwest Illinois across south central and southwest Missouri. The strong gusts over 40 mph were confined to areas north and west of Sikeston, including Poplar Bluff and Cape Girardeau. The peak measured wind gusts at both the Cape Girardeau and Poplar Bluff airports were 46 mph. The highest gust at the Perryville airport was 41 mph.

Hail up to four inches in diameter was reported near the town of Arcola, in Dade County. Other reports of two inch hail or larger were received from Barton, Cedar, Dallas, Greene, Hickory, Polk, and Taney Counties. The Automated Surface Observation System (ASOS) at Vichy in Maries County recorded a wind gust of 97 mph. It was later confirmed that an EF-2 tornado moved over the area, resulting in 5 injuries to the residents of a house that was heavily damaged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickle sized hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong south to southwest winds gusted from 40 to 50 mph ahead of a cold front that trailed southwest from northwest Illinois across south central and southwest Missouri. The strong gusts over 40 mph were confined to areas north and west of Sikeston, including Poplar Bluff and Cape Girardeau. The peak measured wind gusts at both the Cape Girardeau and Poplar Bluff airports were 46 mph. The highest gust at the Perryville airport was 41 mph.

As a warm front lifted northward across the forecast area, a strong southwesterly low-level jet brought in plenty of low-level moisture as well as increasing instability, veering vertical wind profiles and steep lapse rates. A stout cap eroded across portions of central/northeast Missouri as well as west central Illinois, so storms developed in this area through the afternoon and evening hours. Some of the severe storms produced very large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wide swath of large hail fell across northern portions of Boone County from Harrisburg to Sturgeon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A loosely organized cluster of thunderstorms evolved into a broken line of thunderstorms as they moved east from the Ozark foothills into the Poplar Bluff area. Wind damage occurred with a couple of the storms, especially over Ripley County. Dime to nickel-size hail was also reported with a few storms. The storms formed along a cold front that trailed southward from a low pressure system over the western Great Lakes. The air mass remained moderately unstable into the evening hours, when mixed-layer capes decreased to around 1500 j/kg. Effective bulk wind shear averaged from 35 to 40 knots in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail was reported at the intersection of U.S. Highway 67 and M Highway.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down a large tree onto N. Barnett School Road. Also, another large tree was blown down near the intersection of N. Ogden Street and E. Stone Street in Sturgeon.

Several strong to severe thunderstorms moved into eastern Missouri in the afternoon hours of April 15th. These storms were initially producing copious large hail, but rapidly transitioned into tornadic storms. The first tornado was associated with a supercell ahead of a congealing line of convection moving rapidly toward the Mississippi River. A portion of this line of storms rapidly surged eastward, resulting in numerous tornadoes in Jefferson county in Missouri, and Monroe, Madison, St. Clair, and Washington counties in Illinois. In total, 12 tornadoes have been identified, including 3 EF-1s, 6 EF-0s, and 3 tornadoes of unknown intensity that produced no damage.

Synoptic wind gusts up to 60 mph were recorded at Kirksville Regional Airport during the late afternoon hours of April 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KIRK ASOS measured 3 instances of 52 knot wind gusts between 1625 LST and 1631 LST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cyclic supercell moved northeast from Arkansas, producing two tornadoes in southeast Missouri. Both tornadoes occurred in heavily forested areas. The second tornado struck a small community called Glenallen, located near Marble Hill in Bollinger County. Several fatalities were associated with this significant tornado. The supercell was the product of a favorable environment for tornadic storms. Moderate instability and strong deep-layer wind shear coincided over the region. Mixed-layer capes were between 1000 and 1500 j/kg in the presence of effective bulk shear around 55 knots. A surface low tracked from central Kansas into Iowa, resulting in a large unstable warm sector over Missouri and Illinois. Strong low-level south to southwest winds brought unseasonably warm late-night temperatures in the mid 70's and dew points in the 60's.  The onset of storms was delayed by capping in the warm sector and pockets of dry low-level air. Wind fields strengthened throughout the night and capping weakened, leading to nocturnal supercell tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was the start of the tornado track as determined by an aerial survey from the USDA Forest Service. Trees were uprooted. Peak winds were estimated near 85 mph in this very short section of track in Carter County. The tornado quickly entered Wayne County in a highly forested area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of very hot and extremely humid weather began on Sunday, August 20 and lasted through Friday, August 25. Although the temperature rarely threatened climatological records, the humidity caused the heat index to reach extreme levels. The combination of heat and humidity led to several heat related illnesses across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of very hot and extremely humid weather began on Sunday, August 20 and lasted through Friday, August 25. The combination of heat and humidity led to heat index values of 115 to 120 as observed at numerous reporting sites in the region.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado entered a heavily forested area of Wayne County after forming just over the county line in Carter County. The tornado produced a swath of tree destruction up to 300 yards wide. The tornado paralleled Highway A for about a mile or so. The tornado caused shingle damage to a house located along Highway A. Where the tornado crossed Highway 49, the most intense tree damage was observed. This is where peak winds were estimated near 110 mph and where acres of trees were flattened. The tree damage swath became narrow and much less intense east of Highway 49 in hilly forested terrain.

Hail up to the size of golf balls occurred over Taney County, and wind gusts over 60 mph were measured at the Springfield-Branson National Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickle to quarter sized hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developed along the southwest Missouri/southeast Kansas border during the early afternoon and tracked eastward across the area through the early to mid evening. Hail up to four inches in diameter was reported near the town of Arcola, in Dade County. Other reports of two inch hail or larger were received from Barton, Cedar, Dallas, Greene, Hickory, Polk, and Taney Counties. The Automated Surface Observation System (ASOS) at Vichy in Maries County recorded a wind gust of 97 mph. It was later confirmed that an EF-2 tornado moved over the area, resulting in 5 injuries to the residents of a house that was heavily damaged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball sized hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several strong to severe thunderstorms moved into eastern Missouri in the afternoon hours of April 15th. These storms were initially producing copious large hail, but rapidly transitioned into tornadic storms. The first tornado was associated with a supercell ahead of a congealing line of convection moving rapidly toward the Mississippi River. A portion of this line of storms rapidly surged eastward, resulting in numerous tornadoes in Jefferson county in Missouri, and Monroe, Madison, St. Clair, and Washington counties in Illinois. In total, 12 tornadoes have been identified, including 3 EF-1s, 6 EF-0s, and 3 tornadoes of unknown intensity that produced no damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wide swath of large hail fell across Franklin County from Union through Villa Ridge, Grey Summit and Pacific. The largest hail stones, 3 inches in diameter, were reported on the north side of Pacific.

A strong cold front moved through the region during the afternoon hours. There was a narrow corridor of 1000 to 2000 J/kg of MLCAPE along and ahead of the cold front where dewpoints reached the low 60s. This instability, combined with deep-layer wind shear of 30 to 40 kts, supporting organized thunderstorms. There were numerous reports of large hail and some damaging winds with the severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds snapped a couple of power poles in Elston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm moved east across the Ozark foothills during the warmest and most unstable hours of the afternoon. Hail up to quarter-size was reported with the storm. The storm occurred in warm moist south flow ahead of a cold front that trailed southwest from northwest Illinois across south central  and southwest Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two separate reports of dime to quarter-size hail were received from Van Buren.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cyclic supercell moved northeast from Arkansas, producing two tornadoes in southeast Missouri. Both tornadoes occurred in heavily forested areas. The second tornado struck a small community called Glenallen, located near Marble Hill in Bollinger County. Several fatalities were associated with this significant tornado. The supercell was the product of a favorable environment for tornadic storms. Moderate instability and strong deep-layer wind shear coincided over the region. Mixed-layer capes were between 1000 and 1500 j/kg in the presence of effective bulk shear around 55 knots. A surface low tracked from central Kansas into Iowa, resulting in a large unstable warm sector over Missouri and Illinois. Strong low-level south to southwest winds brought unseasonably warm late-night temperatures in the mid 70's and dew points in the 60's.  The onset of storms was delayed by capping in the warm sector and pockets of dry low-level air. Wind fields strengthened throughout the night and capping weakened, leading to nocturnal supercell tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado moved northeast across central Bollinger County, passing just north and west of Marble Hill. The most severe damage occurred in Glenallen (near Marble Hill), where several fatalities and injuries occurred. All five occupants of a demolished mobile home were killed there, including two teenagers, a middle-aged adult, and two older adults. Several manufactured homes were totally destroyed. Approximately a couple of dozen homes suffered major structural damage, including roof damage, broken windows, and collapse of walls. Severe tree damage occurred in the vicinity of Glenallen, where hundreds of trees were snapped at the base. A dozen or so farm outbuildings sustained major damage along the path of the tornado. Peak winds were estimated near 130 mph at Glenallen. The maximum path width and damage rating were assigned to the Glenallen area along Highway 34. The tornado was very close to dissipating when it crossed into Cape Girardeau County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) Drought conditions spread slightly farther east into the southwestern Missouri Ozarks through the end of April. Despite some rainfall, drought conditions deteriorated during the month with Severe (D2) Drought edging farther into Vernon and Barton Counties with Moderate (D1) to Abnormally Dry (D0) around the Severe Drought areas. The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop and vegetation impacts due to limited soil and surface moisture, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers. Final reports of damages will be placed in the last month the drought occurred in the counties impacted in 2023.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Limited rainfall through the month allowed severe drought conditions to spread farther east across the county as drought conditions deepened across the much of region through the month of April. The US Drought Monitor indicated the western third of the county remained in Severe (D2) Drought with the remainder of the county in Moderate (D1) Drought or Abnormally Dry (D0) conditions. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture. Farm and stock ponds saw continuing impacts with ground water levels and reservoirs showing limited drought condition improvement. Early spring crop and vegetation growth was showing signs of stress due to limited moisture. Monetary crop losses for 2023 beginning January first will be included in future reports when the drought ends and loses are assessed.

A loosely organized cluster of thunderstorms evolved into a broken line of thunderstorms as they moved east from the Ozark foothills into the Poplar Bluff area. Wind damage occurred with a couple of the storms, especially over Ripley County. Dime to nickel-size hail was also reported with a few storms. The storms formed along a cold front that trailed southward from a low pressure system over the western Great Lakes. The air mass remained moderately unstable into the evening hours, when mixed-layer capes decreased to around 1500 j/kg. Effective bulk wind shear averaged from 35 to 40 knots in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The roof was blown off a mobile home, and there was tree damage along Highway H-6 southwest of Naylor.

The Automated Surface Observation System (ASOS) at Vichy in Maries County recorded a wind gust of 97 mph. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several strong to severe thunderstorms moved into eastern Missouri in the afternoon hours of April 15th. These storms were initially producing copious large hail, but rapidly transitioned into tornadic storms. The first tornado was associated with a supercell ahead of a congealing line of convection moving rapidly toward the Mississippi River. A portion of this line of storms rapidly surged eastward, resulting in numerous tornadoes in Jefferson county in Missouri, and Monroe, Madison, St. Clair, and Washington counties in Illinois. In total, 12 tornadoes have been identified, including 3 EF-1s, 6 EF-0s, and 3 tornadoes of unknown intensity that produced no damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado touched down on Valley Drive at 619 PM CDT just south of Lucie Avenue, snapping off several trees and causing minor roof damage to a couple of houses. It then moved northeast crossing Highway 141 about a third of a mile south of the I-44/Highway 141 interchange.  As it continued to the northeast paralleling I-44, it caused minor damage to trees, roofs and light posts. As the tornado approached Bowles Avenue, it began to move in an easterly direction across a light industrial area, but still paralleling I-44. Damage in this area remained minor with roof and tree damage. As it crossed the Meramec River, about half a mile south of I-44, it tracked in a more east southeasterly direction through Tapawingo National Golf Course causing damage to numerous trees and minor structural damage. The tornado lifted and dissipated near the intersection of West Watson Road and Bond Place. Overall the tornado was rated EF1 with a max wind speed of 100 mph. The path length was 4.47 miles with a max path width of 100 yards. No deaths or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of very hot and extremely humid weather began on Sunday, August 20 and lasted through Friday, August 25. Although the temperature rarely threatened climatological records, the humidity caused the heat index to reach extreme levels. The combination of heat and humidity led to several heat related illnesses across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of very hot and extremely humid weather began on Sunday, August 20 and lasted through Friday, August 25. The combination of heat and humidity led to heat index values of 110 to 120 as observed at numerous reporting sites in the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms developed over far southwest Missouri and southeast Kansas during the early morning of the 9th as a low level jet interacted with a stalled front along the northwest Arkansas and northeast Oklahoma border. These storms dissipated during the late morning, but left another outflow boundary that interacted with an approaching storm complex to produce another round of strong to severe storms during the afternoon and evening of the 9th. Numerous reports of quarter to golf ball sized hail and wind gusts over 60 mph were reported over southwest Missouri and southeast Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail to the size of hen eggs was reported. Minimal damage was reported to a home and a few vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developed over the central Plains near the Kansas and Nebraska border during the afternoon of the 7th and moved southeastward across central and south central Missouri from the pre-dawn hours to the early morning of the 8th. These storms left an outflow boundary over central Missouri that sparked a second round of storms during the afternoon. These storms were much stronger and produced hail across central Missouri through the evening of the  8th. The strongest storm produced hail to the size of baseballs in Dent County near Stone Hill. Rainfall amounts over two inches occurred with these storms near Nevada, Lake of the Ozarks, and north of Salem.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ping pong ball sized hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass settled over the Mid-South from late January into early February with a front stalled just south of the region. A series of upper-level disturbances overrunning that arctic air resulted in three rounds of wintry precipitation with some significant tree and powerline damage. Travel was also impacted across the Mid-South. The first round started late on January 30th and continued until the morning of January 31st. Heavy sleet fell across the northern tier of the Mid-South from northeast Arkansas into the Missouri Bootheel and northwest Tennessee. Heavy freezing rain fell across parts of east-central Arkansas with lighter amounts into West Tennessee along the I-40 corridor. Round two moved into the Mid-South during the afternoon and evening of January 31st with sleet along and north of the Tennessee/Mississippi state line, heaviest across the Memphis metropolitan area, and light freezing rain across extreme northern Mississippi. The third round started on the evening of February 1st and ended on the morning of February 2nd. Freezing rain spread across areas along and south of the Interstate 40 corridor including east-central Arkansas, northwest Mississippi, and southwest Tennessee. This round resulted in numerous power outages across east-central Arkansas, northwest Mississippi, and southwest Tennessee. There was significant tree damage across extreme northwest Mississippi.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total accumulation of one-half to one inch of sleet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of May 6 strong thunderstorms formed in far northwest Missouri. Through the afternoon these storms strengthened as they moved east across northern Missouri, ultimately turning into robust supercells that started diving southeast toward Highway 36. Along the way they produced giant hail reaching, and perhaps exceeding 4 inches at times. As the main storm pushed southeast toward Linneus, Missouri it produced a tornado that at times was several hundred yards wide. This tornado produced EF-2 damage just north of Linneus. Perhaps as noteworthy, if not more so, was the extremely strong and wide rear flank downdraft that produced some intense damage well south of the main tornado track. This RFD destroyed numerous homes along the way and produced extreme tree damage to the city of Linneus, which was largely spared a direct hit from the actual tornado. Overall the storm produced three separate tornadoes, the most noteworthy of which was the EF-2 just north of Linneus, Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Missouri Mesonet reported wind of 87 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple thunderstorms acquired supercell characteristics for short periods of time, generally less than an hour. These storms formed over the Ozark foothills and moved slowly east to the Mississippi River delta flatlands before weakening. Large hail up to the size of golf balls accompanied these storms. A well-developed mesocyclone with one of the storms was accompanied by a funnel cloud. The storms formed during the warmth of the afternoon along a slow-moving warm front that was draped across southeast Missouri. Periods of sunshine resulted in moderate instability south of the front, with capes around 2000 J/kg. Backed surface winds near the front, beneath 25 to 30 knots of mid-level westerly flow, supported transient supercell structures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a pretty wet late winter and early spring a fairly prolonged dry spell caused areas of western into central Missouri to fall well behind seasonal normal rainfall. By mid to late May severe drought was declared for a number of counties across Missouri. As of at least late May there were no widespread impacts from this drought. Drought conditions worsened into early June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: By mid to late May conditions had become much drier from the wet early start to early spring. By mid to late spring the area became deficit of normal rainfall by several inches, resulting in a declaration of severe (D2) drought. Impacts from this drought still appear to be minor, but drought conditions worsened as it went into June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of July 14, 2023 a strong storm formed in northeast Kansas and eventually transformed into a strong squall line that impacted far eastern Kansas and western Missouri. This storm left a swath of wind damage mainly through the heart of the Kansas City metro area in the form of widespread tree damage. Several structures were impacted by falling trees as the storm moved through the city, and into central Missouri. The highest reported wind gust came from western Johnson County where a personal weather station recorded 89 mph wind just west of Lenexa. Numerous wind reports, both estimated and measured were over 70 mph. At one point nearly 200,000 people across the Kansas City metro lost power. As of the next day, roughly 60,000 Kansas Citians were still without power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Medium sized tree snapped at the base.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An organized line of severe thunderstorms produced widespread wind damage across several counties in southeast Missouri. Numerous trees were blown down during the evening from Wayne County, east-southeast across northern Stoddard and southern Bollinger Counties, through Scott County. Widespread power outages occurred, and several thousand customers remained without power into the morning of the following day. There were at least two measurements of wind gusts between 70 and 75 mph. Ahead of the organized line of severe thunderstorms, an isolated severe thunderstorm produced large hail. Storms initiated over western Missouri late in the afternoon and gradually evolved into a line that moved eastward along a frontal corridor across southern Missouri. Given adequate westerly wind flow through a deep layer, the storms organized into a squall line with considerable damaging wind potential.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms produced widespread tree damage across much of the county. The county dispatch center reported trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms accompanied a compact but well-developed circulation center as it moved slowly east-northeast across southern Missouri. Precipitable water values were around 150 to 200 percent of normal, which led to heavy rainfall in the storms. The ground was already saturated from earlier supercell thunderstorms. Pockets of flash flooding were observed in the Ozark foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Parts of Dogwood Lane in Piedmont were washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lingering warm front as well as remnant outflow boundaries were the triggering mechanisms for storms. Some of the storms produced large hail, damaging winds and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 3 and 6 inches of rain fell in a short amount of time over portions of southeastern St. Louis County. Several roads were flooded in Affton, Crestwood, Shrewsbury, and Oakville. Both directions of I-55 was briefly flooded near the Bayless Avenue exit. Also, the parking lot at Cedar Creek Lodge Apartments was flooded with several feet of water from the nearby creek. At least a dozen cars were flooded in the parking lot.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple thunderstorms acquired supercell characteristics for short periods of time, generally less than an hour. These storms formed over the Ozark foothills and moved slowly east to the Mississippi River delta flatlands before weakening. Large hail up to the size of golf balls accompanied these storms. A well-developed mesocyclone with one of the storms was accompanied by a funnel cloud. The storms formed during the warmth of the afternoon along a slow-moving warm front that was draped across southeast Missouri. Periods of sunshine resulted in moderate instability south of the front, with capes around 2000 J/kg. Backed surface winds near the front, beneath 25 to 30 knots of mid-level westerly flow, supported transient supercell structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail fell in the Leora area, about 9 miles northwest of Bloomfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms developed over far southwest Missouri and southeast Kansas during the early morning of the 9th as a low level jet interacted with a stalled front along the northwest Arkansas and northeast Oklahoma border. These storms dissipated during the late morning, but left another outflow boundary that interacted with an approaching storm complex to produce another round of strong to severe storms during the afternoon and evening of the 9th. Numerous reports of quarter to golf ball sized hail and wind gusts over 60 mph were reported over southwest Missouri and southeast Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees and large limbs were reported down across town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After another month of below average precipitation, the area saw generally worsening drought conditions across central and northern Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After another relatively dry month across the area central and northern Missouri saw generally deteriorating drought conditions. By the middle to end of the month almost the entire area was covered in D3 extreme drought conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated to widely scattered storms developed along remnant outflow boundaries on the  afternoon the 15th. Saturated soils from previous rainfall in the week led to flash flooding with the afternoon and evening storms. Minor to moderate river flooding also occurred along the Jacks Fork, Current, and Big Piney rivers along with Roubidoux Creek. A few severe storms occurred and one storm near Fair Grove produced an intermittent EF-0 tornado that impacted homes, trees and outbuildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Walnut Street and the Oak Hill Bridge was closed at Brushy Creek due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of July 14, 2023 a strong storm formed in northeast Kansas and eventually transformed into a strong squall line that impacted far eastern Kansas and western Missouri. This storm left a swath of wind damage mainly through the heart of the Kansas City metro area in the form of widespread tree damage. Several structures were impacted by falling trees as the storm moved through the city, and into central Missouri. The highest reported wind gust came from western Johnson County where a personal weather station recorded 89 mph wind just west of Lenexa. Numerous wind reports, both estimated and measured were over 70 mph. At one point nearly 200,000 people across the Kansas City metro lost power. As of the next day, roughly 60,000 Kansas Citians were still without power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail in Ferrelview.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of July 30th, a squall line moved south southeast out of SE Nebraska into eastern Kansas and western Missouri. This squall line produced widespread 60 to 70 mph wind gusts with a few gusts up to 80 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured wind gust of 76 mph at Kansas City Downtown Airport (MKC).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm developed near the Mississippi River and then moved northeast. Large hail was reported in the storm.  A warm and humid air mass was present in the afternoon, with temperatures in the mid 80's and dew points near 70. Instability was quite strong, with mixed-layer cape values around 3000 J/kg. A mid-level shortwave trough traversed southern Missouri, triggering the storm development.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developed over the central Plains near the Kansas and Nebraska border during the afternoon of the 7th and moved southeastward across central and south central Missouri from the pre-dawn hours to the early morning of the 8th. These storms left an outflow boundary over central Missouri that sparked a second round of storms during the afternoon. These storms were much stronger and produced hail across central Missouri through the evening of the  8th. The strongest storm produced hail to the size of baseballs in Dent County near Stone Hill. Rainfall amounts over two inches occurred with these storms near Nevada, Lake of the Ozarks, and north of Salem.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickle to Quarter sized hail was reported via MPing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of May 6 strong thunderstorms formed in far northwest Missouri. Through the afternoon these storms strengthened as they moved east across northern Missouri, ultimately turning into robust supercells that started diving southeast toward Highway 36. Along the way they produced giant hail reaching, and perhaps exceeding 4 inches at times. As the main storm pushed southeast toward Linneus, Missouri it produced a tornado that at times was several hundred yards wide. This tornado produced EF-2 damage just north of Linneus. Perhaps as noteworthy, if not more so, was the extremely strong and wide rear flank downdraft that produced some intense damage well south of the main tornado track. This RFD destroyed numerous homes along the way and produced extreme tree damage to the city of Linneus, which was largely spared a direct hit from the actual tornado. Overall the storm produced three separate tornadoes, the most noteworthy of which was the EF-2 just north of Linneus, Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball sized hail was reported in Westboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of May 6 strong thunderstorms formed in far northwest Missouri. Through the afternoon these storms strengthened as they moved east across northern Missouri, ultimately turning into robust supercells that started diving southeast toward Highway 36. Along the way they produced giant hail reaching, and perhaps exceeding 4 inches at times. As the main storm pushed southeast toward Linneus, Missouri it produced a tornado that at times was several hundred yards wide. This tornado produced EF-2 damage just north of Linneus. Perhaps as noteworthy, if not more so, was the extremely strong and wide rear flank downdraft that produced some intense damage well south of the main tornado track. EVENT_NARRATIVE: After creating a brief tornado in the city of Trenton the supercell went on to produce a more massive and long-lived tornado across central Linn County. No incorporated cities were directly impacted by the tornado, but several residences across the county were damaged. The most extensive residential damage occurred just north of Linneus as the storm crossed Highway 5. The tornado eventually dissipated east of Linneus before producing again as it approached Bucklin. One notable aspect of this event was the extreme rear flank downdraft, which produced some of the more extensive damage. This damage was determined to be straight line winds due to being 1-2 miles away from the main tornado circulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While July did provide some increase in moisture across the area the exceptionally dry conditions leading up to the month pushed the area into extreme drought. By the middle of the month exceptional (D4) drought was declared in these counties. Some marginal improvement was made due to late month thunderstorms, but the month still ended with these counties in (D3) extreme drought.

By the middle of the month exceptional (D4) drought was declared in these counties. Some marginal improvement was made due to late month thunderstorms, but the month still ended with these counties in (D3) extreme drought. As of now, there have been no widespread impacts from the drought reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) Drought conditions developed across portions central Missouri through the end of May. Despite some rainfall, drought conditions deepened over most of central Missouri during the month with Extreme (D3) drought developing as rainfall remained lacking. Severe (D2) Drought persisted but improved in some areas while Moderate (D1) to Abnormally Dry (D0) expanded across southwestern Missouri. The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop and vegetation impacts due to limited soil and surface moisture, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers. Final reports of damages will be placed in the last month the drought occurred in the counties impacted in 2023.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Beneficial rainfall through the month allowed drought conditions to improve for much of the county though Severe (D2) drought remained in portions of the county through the month of May. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of May 6 strong thunderstorms formed in far northwest Missouri. Through the afternoon these storms strengthened as they moved east across northern Missouri, ultimately turning into robust supercells that started diving southeast toward Highway 36. Along the way they produced giant hail reaching, and perhaps exceeding 4 inches at times. As the main storm pushed southeast toward Linneus, Missouri it produced a tornado that at times was several hundred yards wide. This tornado produced EF-2 damage just north of Linneus. Perhaps as noteworthy, if not more so, was the extremely strong and wide rear flank downdraft that produced some intense damage well south of the main tornado track. This RFD destroyed numerous homes along the way and produced extreme tree damage to the city of Linneus, which was largely spared a direct hit from the actual tornado. Overall the storm produced three separate tornadoes, the most noteworthy of which was the EF-2 just north of Linneus, Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About a mile to two miles south of the main tornado circulation widespread unidirectional wind damage occurred, likely as a result of a strong rear flank downdraft. This RFD wind was responsible for numerous damaged residences and outbuildings across Linn County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An organized line of severe thunderstorms produced widespread wind damage across several counties in southeast Missouri. Numerous trees were blown down during the evening from Wayne County, east-southeast across northern Stoddard and southern Bollinger Counties, through Scott County. Widespread power outages occurred, and several thousand customers remained without power into the morning of the following day. There were at least two measurements of wind gusts between 70 and 75 mph. Ahead of the organized line of severe thunderstorms, an isolated severe thunderstorm produced large hail. Storms initiated over western Missouri late in the afternoon and gradually evolved into a line that moved eastward along a frontal corridor across southern Missouri. Given adequate westerly wind flow through a deep layer, the storms organized into a squall line with considerable damaging wind potential.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter-size hail was covering the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms developed over far southwest Missouri and southeast Kansas during the early morning of the 9th as a low level jet interacted with a stalled front along the northwest Arkansas and northeast Oklahoma border. These storms dissipated during the late morning, but left another outflow boundary that interacted with an approaching storm complex to produce another round of strong to severe storms during the afternoon and evening of the 9th. Numerous reports of quarter to golf ball sized hail and wind gusts over 60 mph were reported over southwest Missouri and southeast Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a pretty wet late winter and early spring a fairly prolonged dry spell caused areas of western into central Missouri to fall well behind seasonal normal rainfall. By early May severe drought was declared for a number of counties across Missouri. As of at least late May there were no widespread impacts from this drought. Drought conditions worsened into early June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: By early May conditions had become much drier from the wet early start to early spring. By mid to late spring the area became deficit of normal rainfall by several inches, resulting in a declaration of severe (D2) drought. Impacts from this drought still appear to be minor, but drought conditions worsened as it went into June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass settled over the Mid-South from late January into early February with a front stalled just south of the region. A series of upper-level disturbances overrunning that arctic air resulted in three rounds of wintry precipitation with some significant tree and powerline damage. Travel was also impacted across the Mid-South. The first round started late on January 30th and continued until the morning of January 31st. Heavy sleet fell across the northern tier of the Mid-South from northeast Arkansas into the Missouri Bootheel and northwest Tennessee. Heavy freezing rain fell across parts of east-central Arkansas with lighter amounts into West Tennessee along the I-40 corridor. Round two moved into the Mid-South during the afternoon and evening of January 31st with sleet along and north of the Tennessee/Mississippi state line, heaviest across the Memphis metropolitan area, and light freezing rain across extreme northern Mississippi. The third round started on the evening of February 1st and ended on the morning of February 2nd. Freezing rain spread across areas along and south of the Interstate 40 corridor including east-central Arkansas, northwest Mississippi, and southwest Tennessee. This round resulted in numerous power outages across east-central Arkansas, northwest Mississippi, and southwest Tennessee. There was significant tree damage across extreme northwest Mississippi.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total accumulation of one-half to one inch of sleet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of May 6 strong thunderstorms formed in far northwest Missouri. Through the afternoon these storms strengthened as they moved east across northern Missouri, ultimately turning into robust supercells that started diving southeast toward Highway 36. Along the way they produced giant hail reaching, and perhaps exceeding 4 inches at times. As the main storm pushed southeast toward Linneus, Missouri it produced a tornado that at times was several hundred yards wide. This tornado produced EF-2 damage just north of Linneus. Perhaps as noteworthy, if not more so, was the extremely strong and wide rear flank downdraft that produced some intense damage well south of the main tornado track. This RFD destroyed numerous homes along the way and produced extreme tree damage to the city of Linneus, which was largely spared a direct hit from the actual tornado. Overall the storm produced three separate tornadoes, the most noteworthy of which was the EF-2 just north of Linneus, Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About a mile to two miles south of the main tornado circulation widespread unidirectional wind damage occurred, likely as a result of a strong rear flank downdraft. This RFD wind was responsible for numerous damaged residences and outbuildings across Linn County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An organized line of severe thunderstorms produced widespread wind damage across several counties in southeast Missouri. Numerous trees were blown down during the evening from Wayne County, east-southeast across northern Stoddard and southern Bollinger Counties, through Scott County. Widespread power outages occurred, and several thousand customers remained without power into the morning of the following day. There were at least two measurements of wind gusts between 70 and 75 mph. Ahead of the organized line of severe thunderstorms, an isolated severe thunderstorm produced large hail. Storms initiated over western Missouri late in the afternoon and gradually evolved into a line that moved eastward along a frontal corridor across southern Missouri. Given adequate westerly wind flow through a deep layer, the storms organized into a squall line with considerable damaging wind potential.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A personal weather station recorded a wind gust to 72 mph.
