After a pretty wet late winter and early spring a fairly prolonged dry spell caused areas of western into central Missouri to fall well behind seasonal normal rainfall. By early May severe drought was declared for a number of counties across Missouri. As of at least late May there were no widespread impacts from this drought. Drought conditions worsened into early June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: By early May conditions had become much drier from the wet early start to early spring. By mid to late spring the area became deficit of normal rainfall by several inches, resulting in a declaration of severe (D2) drought. By May 30, extreme (D3) was declared due to the ongoing dry conditions. Impacts from this drought still appear to be minor, but drought conditions worsened as it went into June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms developed over far southwest Missouri and southeast Kansas during the early morning of the 9th as a low level jet interacted with a stalled front along the northwest Arkansas and northeast Oklahoma border. These storms dissipated during the late morning, but left another outflow boundary that interacted with an approaching storm complex to produce another round of strong to severe storms during the afternoon and evening of the 9th. Numerous reports of quarter to golf ball sized hail and wind gusts over 60 mph were reported over southwest Missouri and southeast Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar sized hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developed over the central Plains near the Kansas and Nebraska border during the afternoon of the 7th and moved southeastward across central and south central Missouri from the pre-dawn hours to the early morning of the 8th. These storms left an outflow boundary over central Missouri that sparked a second round of storms during the afternoon. These storms were much stronger and produced hail across central Missouri through the evening of the  8th. The strongest storm produced hail to the size of baseballs in Dent County near Stone Hill. Rainfall amounts over two inches occurred with these storms near Nevada, Lake of the Ozarks, and north of Salem.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball to tennis ball sized hail was reported in a mostly rural area. One home and a business suffered roof damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While July did provide some increase in moisture across the area the exceptionally dry conditions leading up to the month pushed the area into extreme drought. By the middle of the month exceptional (D4) drought was declared in these counties. Some marginal improvement was made due to late month thunderstorms, but the month still ended with these counties in (D3) extreme drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: While July did provide some increase in moisture across the area the exceptionally dry conditions leading up to the month pushed the area into extreme drought. By the middle of the month exceptional (D4) drought was declared in these counties. Some marginal improvement was made due to late month thunderstorms, but the month still ended with these counties in (D3) extreme drought. As of now, there have been no widespread impacts from the drought reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) Drought conditions developed across portions central Missouri through the end of May. Despite some rainfall, drought conditions deepened over most of central Missouri during the month with Extreme (D3) drought developing as rainfall remained lacking. Severe (D2) Drought persisted but improved in some areas while Moderate (D1) to Abnormally Dry (D0) expanded across southwestern Missouri. The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop and vegetation impacts due to limited soil and surface moisture, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers. Final reports of damages will be placed in the last month the drought occurred in the counties impacted in 2023.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Limited rainfall through the month allowed Extreme (D3) drought conditions to return across portions of the county through the month of May. The US Drought Monitor indicated that extreme northern portions of the county deteriorated to Extreme (D3) Drought conditions with the remainder of the county in Severe (D2) Drought to Abnormally Dry (D0) drought conditions. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture. Farm and stock ponds saw continuing impacts with ground water levels and reservoirs showing limited drought condition improvement. Early spring crop and vegetation growth was showing signs of stress due to limited moisture. Monetary crop losses for 2023 beginning May first will be included in future reports when the drought ends and loses are assessed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple thunderstorms acquired supercell characteristics for short periods of time, generally less than an hour. These storms formed over the Ozark foothills and moved slowly east to the Mississippi River delta flatlands before weakening. Large hail up to the size of golf balls accompanied these storms. A well-developed mesocyclone with one of the storms was accompanied by a funnel cloud. The storms formed during the warmth of the afternoon along a slow-moving warm front that was draped across southeast Missouri. Periods of sunshine resulted in moderate instability south of the front, with capes around 2000 J/kg. Backed surface winds near the front, beneath 25 to 30 knots of mid-level westerly flow, supported transient supercell structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was reported in southern Wayne County, between Williamsville and U.S. Highway 67 south of Greenville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a pretty wet late winter and early spring a fairly prolonged dry spell caused areas of western into central Missouri to fall well behind seasonal normal rainfall. By early May severe drought was declared for a number of counties across Missouri. As of at least late May there were no widespread impacts from this drought. Drought conditions worsened into early June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: By early May conditions had become much drier from the wet early start to early spring. By mid to late spring the area became deficit of normal rainfall by several inches, resulting in a declaration of severe (D2) drought. By May 9, extreme (D3) was declared due to the ongoing dry conditions. Impacts from this drought still appear to be minor, but drought conditions worsened as it went into June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system led to significant impacts. Heavy rainfall amounts of 3 to 4.5 inches produced flash flooding of a few creeks and roads. Small rivers such as the Black, Current, and St. Francis experienced minor flooding. Strong wind gusts occurred, ranging between 40 and 60 mph. These winds were produced by a strong pressure gradient on the back side of a very intense low pressure system. The highest measured wind gust was 58 mph in Mississippi County along the Mississippi River. Other peak wind gusts included 56 mph at the Cape Girardeau airport, 49 mph at the Sikeston airport, and 48 mph at the Poplar Bluff airport. A period of strong northeast winds during the early morning hours was followed by even stronger northwest winds following the passage of the low pressure center in the afternoon. The magnitude and duration of this wind produced scattered damage to trees and power lines. Soggy ground due to the excessive rainfall likely contributed to some trees being more susceptible to being blown over. Scattered power outages occurred, particularly in the Mississippi River counties. Finally, the lowest barometric pressure of all-time was observed in Paducah, Kentucky (977.7 mb) as the center of the low tracked from the bootheel of extreme southeast Missouri northeast right along the Ohio River.

A strong cold front moved south through the region with plenty of instability, high mid-level lapse rates and strong 0 to 6km bulk shear. Given this environment, a number of storms became severe with widespread reports of large hail, some in excess of 2 inches in diameter.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down several trees onto Highway 21 about 3 miles south of Ellington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed over northwest Arkansas during the late morning of the 2nd and moved northeastward while expanding in coverage and intensity during the afternoon and evening as a strong upper level trough moved northeastward from west Texas toward the Missouri and Kansas border. The rain persisted until the late morning of the 3rd. Rainfall accumulations in excess of two inches occurred along and east of a line from Cassville to Osage Beach, with embedded accumulations over three inches near Lebanon, Houston and Salem. Low water crossings became flooded at many locations east of Springfield, and minor to moderate river flooding occurred along the Big Piney, Jacks Fork, Gasconade and James Rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway Z at the Finley Creek was flooded and impassable.

Other peak wind gusts included 56 mph at the Cape Girardeau airport, 49 mph at the Sikeston airport, and 48 mph at the Poplar Bluff airport. A period of strong northeast winds during the early morning hours was followed by even stronger northwest winds following the passage of the low pressure center in the afternoon. The magnitude and duration of this wind produced scattered damage to trees and power lines. Soggy ground due to the excessive rainfall likely contributed to some trees being more susceptible to being blown over. Scattered power outages occurred, particularly in the Mississippi River counties. Finally, the lowest barometric pressure of all-time was observed in Paducah, Kentucky (977.7 mb) as the center of the low tracked from the bootheel of extreme southeast Missouri northeast right along the Ohio River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) Drought conditions spread back into portions of the southwestern Missouri Ozarks through the end of March. Despite some rainfall, drought conditions deteriorated  during the month with Severe (D2) Drought  edging into Vernon and Barton Counties  with Moderate (D1) to Abnormally Dry (D0) around the Severe Drought areas. The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop and vegetation impacts due to limited soil and surface moisture, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers. Final reports of damages will be placed in the last month the drought occurred in the counties impacted in 2023.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Limited rainfall through the month allowed severe drought conditions to spread back into the county as drought conditions deepened across the much of region through the month of March. The US Drought Monitor indicated the far southwestern portion of the county as developing Severe (D2) Drought with the remainder of the county in Moderate (D1) Drought or Abnormally Dry (D0) conditions. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture. Farm and stock ponds saw continuing impacts with ground water levels and reservoirs showing limited drought condition improvement. Early spring crop and vegetation growth was showing signs of stress due to limited moisture. Monetary crop losses for 2023 beginning January first will be included in future reports when the drought ends and loses are assessed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved south through the region with plenty of instability, high mid-level lapse rates and strong 0 to 6km bulk shear. Given this environment, a number of storms became severe with widespread reports of large hail, some in excess of 2 inches in diameter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Via broadcast media with a picture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary front draped itself from central Texas northeast through the Ohio River Valley and produced multiple rounds of showers and thunderstorms, some with very heavy rainfall. Scattered showers and thunderstorms resulted in widespread flooding of low water crossings in the hilly terrain of Carter, Wayne, and Bollinger Counties. One to three inches of rain fell across the region with isolated higher amounts before the front passed east on the 25th. Moderate to heavy rainfall quickly inundated ditches and once again flooded roads around the region. Flooding of the Castor River basin resulted in a motorist fatality in Bollinger County well after the heavy rain had ended. Most other rivers experienced minor to moderate flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low water crossings across the county were flooded with one to two feet of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed over northwest Arkansas during the late morning of the 2nd and moved northeastward while expanding in coverage and intensity during the afternoon and evening as a strong upper level trough moved northeastward from west Texas toward the Missouri and Kansas border. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lingering warm front as well as remnant outflow boundaries were the triggering mechanisms for storms. Some of the storms produced large hail, damaging winds and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 3 and 6 inches rain fell in a short amount of time causing flash flooding over southern portions of the city. The fire department reported that several vehicles were trapped in standing water in the Carondelet neighborhood. On I-64 at Tamm Avenue overpass, flash flooding caused standing water on the highway. Several vehicles became stranded in this location after trying to drive through the flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary front draped itself from central Texas northeast through the Ohio River Valley and produced multiple rounds of showers and thunderstorms, some with very heavy rainfall. Scattered showers and thunderstorms resulted in widespread flooding of low water crossings in the hilly terrain of Carter, Wayne, and Bollinger Counties. One to three inches of rain fell across the region with isolated higher amounts before the front passed east on the 25th. Moderate to heavy rainfall quickly inundated ditches and once again flooded roads around the region. Flooding of the Castor River basin resulted in a motorist fatality in Bollinger County well after the heavy rain had ended. Most other rivers experienced minor to moderate flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low water crossings were flooded across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system led to significant impacts. Heavy rainfall amounts of 3 to 4.5 inches produced flash flooding of a few creeks and roads. Small rivers such as the Black, Current, and St. Francis experienced minor flooding. Strong wind gusts occurred, ranging between 40 and 60 mph. These winds were produced by a strong pressure gradient on the back side of a very intense low pressure system. The highest measured wind gust was 58 mph in Mississippi County along the Mississippi River. Other peak wind gusts included 56 mph at the Cape Girardeau airport, 49 mph at the Sikeston airport, and 48 mph at the Poplar Bluff airport. A period of strong northeast winds during the early morning hours was followed by even stronger northwest winds following the passage of the low pressure center in the afternoon. The magnitude and duration of this wind produced scattered damage to trees and power lines. Soggy ground due to the excessive rainfall likely contributed to some trees being more susceptible to being blown over. Scattered power outages occurred, particularly in the Mississippi River counties. Finally, the lowest barometric pressure of all-time was observed in Paducah, Kentucky (977.7 mb) as the center of the low tracked from the bootheel of extreme southeast Missouri northeast right along the Ohio River.

Severe storms developed over far southwest Missouri and southeast Kansas during the early morning of the 9th as a low level jet interacted with a stalled front along the northwest Arkansas and northeast Oklahoma border. These storms dissipated during the late morning, but left another outflow boundary that interacted with an approaching storm complex to produce another round of strong to severe storms during the afternoon and evening of the 9th. Numerous reports of quarter to golf ball sized hail and wind gusts over 60 mph were reported over southwest Missouri and southeast Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball sized hail was reported in a mainly rural area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and storms persistent over the region as moisture was pulled northward ahead of an upper level low that moved northward from central Texas into western Missouri. There were a few reports of severe hail and wind damage, but overall less severe weather than the previous few days. Rainfall accumulations of one to two inches occurred mainly south of I-44 and east of I-49.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickle sized hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developed over the central Plains near the Kansas and Nebraska border during the afternoon of the 7th and moved southeastward across central and south central Missouri from the pre-dawn hours to the early morning of the 8th. These storms left an outflow boundary over central Missouri that sparked a second round of storms during the afternoon. These storms were much stronger and produced hail across central Missouri through the evening of the  8th. The strongest storm produced hail to the size of baseballs in Dent County near Stone Hill. Rainfall amounts over two inches occurred with these storms near Nevada, Lake of the Ozarks, and north of Salem.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ping pong ball sized hail was reported in a mostly rural area along with winds estimated at 60 mph.

Then as the front moved through the activity became more linear before moving out by late evening. There were numerous reports of large hail, damaging winds and two tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wide swath of large hail up to golf ball sized fell across Hannibal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms developed over far southwest Missouri and southeast Kansas during the early morning of the 9th as a low level jet interacted with a stalled front along the northwest Arkansas and northeast Oklahoma border. These storms dissipated during the late morning, but left another outflow boundary that interacted with an approaching storm complex to produce another round of strong to severe storms during the afternoon and evening of the 9th. Numerous reports of quarter to golf ball sized hail and wind gusts over 60 mph were reported over southwest Missouri and southeast Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball sized hail was reported on the campus of Southwest Baptist University. Multiple vehicles suffered damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developed over the central Plains near the Kansas and Nebraska border during the afternoon of the 7th and moved southeastward across central and south central Missouri from the pre-dawn hours to the early morning of the 8th. These storms left an outflow boundary over central Missouri that sparked a second round of storms during the afternoon. These storms were much stronger and produced hail across central Missouri through the evening of the  8th. The strongest storm produced hail to the size of baseballs in Dent County near Stone Hill. Rainfall amounts over two inches occurred with these storms near Nevada, Lake of the Ozarks, and north of Salem.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to quarter sized hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of very hot and extremely humid weather began on Sunday, August 20 and lasted through Friday, August 25. Although the temperature rarely threatened climatological records, the humidity caused the heat index to reach extreme levels. The combination of heat and humidity led to several heat related illnesses across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of very hot and extremely humid weather began on Sunday, August 20 and lasted through Friday, August 25. The combination of heat and humidity led to heat index values of 115 to 120 as observed at numerous reporting sites in the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Earlier in the day on June 30th, an MCS made its way across southern Iowa and northern Missouri into north-central Illinois. As it moved eastward, an outflow boundary moved south and interacted with a surface boundary stretching across central Missouri. There was a strong gradient of surface-based CAPE. Storms developed and followed that gradient and frontal boundary southeast across central Missouri into south central Illinois. The severe storms produced large hail and damaging winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service storm survey determined that an intermittent EF-0 tornado touched down near Fair Grove, Missouri and tracked to the northwest about a mile and a half. Maximum wind speeds were 77 mph with a maximum path width of 75 yards. At the start point of the tornado, a home sustained minor damage to siding., otherwise sporadic and sparse tree damage was noted along the tornadoes path. Just before lifting, several outbuildings suffered significant damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong system developed on the morning of March 31st and tracked across the state of Iowa. This system pulled unseasonably warm and moist air into the Midwest, which combined with a very favorable wind profile to create a volatile environment for severe thunderstorms.  Discrete supercell thunderstorms occurred initially, but quickly transitioned into a QLCS mode. This system produced a historic tornado outbreak for the area, with a record 29 tornadoes on the day, including 13 rated EF-2, 1 rated EF-3, and 1 rated EF-4. This was the first EF-4 tornado in the NWS Quad Cities service area since 1995. 11 injuries were reported, with no known fatalities. In addition to the numerous tornadoes, there were many large hail and damaging wind reports. Maximum straight line thunderstorm winds were estimated at 80 to 90 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported a tree blown down over Highway 81.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated damaging wind event occurred during the heat of the late afternoon hours. The storm formed in a broad west-southwest wind flow at the surface and near a weakness in a 500 mb ridge that extended from the western Great Lakes to the western Gulf coast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Excessive heat and humidity invaded southeast Missouri for the first time in the 2023 summer season. Peak heat indices ranged from 112 to 118 degrees at most locations on the 29th and 30th. The highest heat indices at selected cities on the 30th were 118 at Cape Girardeau, 114 at Poplar Bluff, and 113 at Doniphan. The heat and humidity arrived on southwest winds ahead of a slow-moving cold front over western Missouri and Illinois. Actual air temperatures were near 100 degrees in some places on the 30th, including 99 at Cape Girardeau.

Showers and storms persistent over the region as moisture was pulled northward ahead of an upper level low that moved northward from central Texas into western Missouri. There were a few reports of severe hail and wind damage, but overall less severe weather than the previous few days. Rainfall accumulations of one to two inches occurred mainly south of I-44 and east of I-49.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters estimated hail from nickle to ping pong ball sized hail fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rainfall caused flooding. Rescue crews pulled four people from floodwaters early in the morning after the people fell into quickly rising flood waters. All people were rescued successfully.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two people trapped by flood waters along with two people who had attempted to help were successfully rescued by the Eastern Douglas County Fire Department and Missouri Highway Patrol after being swept downstream.  No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled surface boundary extending from near Kansas City through central Illinois provided a focus for organized convection during the afternoon. Thunderstorms were mainly hail producers, with up to 1.75 inch diameter hailstones reported in the St. Louis metropolitan area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The discrete thunderstorm that produced severe hail in Franklin County produced wind damage in Jefferson County. A trained spotter reported a snapped power pole in Cedar Hill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Earlier in the day on June 30th, an MCS made its way across southern Iowa and northern Missouri into north-central Illinois. As it moved eastward, an outflow boundary moved south and interacted with a surface boundary stretching across central Missouri. There was a strong gradient of surface-based CAPE. Storms developed and followed that gradient and frontal boundary southeast across central Missouri into south central Illinois. The severe storms produced large hail and damaging winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated to widely scattered storms developed along remnant outflow boundaries on the  afternoon the 15th. Saturated soils from previous rainfall in the week led to flash flooding with the afternoon and evening storms. Minor to moderate river flooding also occurred along the Jacks Fork, Current, and Big Piney rivers along with Roubidoux Creek. A few severe storms occurred and one storm near Fair Grove produced an intermittent EF-0 tornado that impacted homes, trees and outbuildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 40 at Center Creek was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe storms associated with a large mesoscale convective complex that developed over the southern high Plains during the afternoon of the 17th moved eastward into our region during the early morning hours of the 18th. Widespread wind damage occurred as the storms moved eastward across southwest Missouri.  Rainfall accumulations were held to under an inch over most of the area due to the fast movement of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell into a transformer knocking out power to around 30 homes for a short period of time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) Drought conditions expanded across portions central Missouri through the end of June. Very limited rainfall allowed drought conditions to worsen over most of central Missouri into west central and southwestern Missouri during the month. Across central Missouri Extreme (D3) drought was expanded south as rainfall of only a quarter to a tenth of normal for the month fell. Severe (D2) Drought persisted and expanded as well in response to the lack of rainfall with Moderate (D1) to Abnormally Dry (D0) conditions expanding as well around the Extreme and Severe Drought areas. The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop and vegetation impacts due to limited soil and surface moisture, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers. Final reports of damages will be placed in the last month the drought occurred in the counties impacted in 2023.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After falling out of Severe (D2) Drought in May, the county saw no significant rainfall during the month of June. The lack of rainfall allowed drought conditions to deteriorate rapidly across the county through the month of June. Rainfall across much of the county was less than a quarter of normal monthly rainfall which allowed Severe (D2) Drought to expand across the northern third of the county with the remainder of the county in Moderate (D1) Drought. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture. Farm and stock ponds saw continuing impacts with ground water levels and reservoirs showed modest impacts due to limited rainfall. Crop and vegetation growth showed signs of stress due to limited moisture. Monetary crop losses for 2023 beginning January first will be included in future reports when the drought ends and loses are assessed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After 2 months of relatively dry conditions portions of Missouri were brought into severe drought conditions. According to the Advanced Hydrologic Precipitation Service page there was a deficit of 2-5 inches across May and June which led to the declaration of severe drought, and eventual upgrade to extreme drought later in the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After 2 months of relatively dry conditions portions of Missouri were brought into severe drought conditions. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) Drought conditions expanded across portions central Missouri through the end of June. Very limited rainfall allowed drought conditions to worsen over most of central Missouri into west central and southwestern Missouri during the month. Across central Missouri Extreme (D3) drought was expanded south as rainfall of only a quarter to a tenth of normal for the month fell. Severe (D2) Drought persisted and expanded as well in response to the lack of rainfall with Moderate (D1) to Abnormally Dry (D0) conditions expanding as well around the Extreme and Severe Drought areas. The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop and vegetation impacts due to limited soil and surface moisture, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers. Final reports of damages will be placed in the last month the drought occurred in the counties impacted in 2023.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: No significant rainfall occurred in the county through the month allowing drought conditions to deteriorate rapidly across the county through the month of June. Rainfall across much of the county was less than a twentieth of normal monthly rainfall which allowed Extreme (D3) Drought to expand across all of the county. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture. Farm and stock ponds saw continuing impacts with ground water levels and reservoirs showed modest impacts due to limited rainfall. Crop and vegetation growth showed signs of stress due to limited moisture. Monetary crop losses for 2023 beginning January first will be included in future reports when the drought ends and loses are assessed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed across the Mid-South during the late morning and early afternoon of April 5, 2023. These storms developed ahead of an approaching cold front as a mid-level shortwave trough rotated through the area. The region was also under the right entrance region of a 140-knot upper-level jet which helped enhance lift across the area. The airmass was unstable with surface-based CAPE values of up to 2000 j/kg which supported severe storms across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight-line winds uprooted numerous trees around downtown Malden. A funeral home suffered roof and wall damage. Numerous homes had roof damage. A shed was destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) Drought conditions expanded across portions central Missouri through the end of June. Very limited rainfall allowed drought conditions to worsen over most of central Missouri into west central and southwestern Missouri during the month. Across central Missouri Extreme (D3) drought was expanded south as rainfall of only a quarter to a tenth of normal for the month fell. Severe (D2) Drought persisted and expanded as well in response to the lack of rainfall with Moderate (D1) to Abnormally Dry (D0) conditions expanding as well around the Extreme and Severe Drought areas. The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop and vegetation impacts due to limited soil and surface moisture, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers. Final reports of damages will be placed in the last month the drought occurred in the counties impacted in 2023.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After falling out of Severe (D2) Drought in May, the county saw no significant rainfall during the month of June. The lack of rainfall allowed drought conditions to deteriorate rapidly across the county through the month of June. Rainfall across much of the county was less than a quarter of normal monthly rainfall which allowed Severe (D2) Drought to expand across the northern two thirds of the county with the remainder of the county in Moderate (D1) Drought. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture. Farm and stock ponds saw continuing impacts with ground water levels and reservoirs showed modest impacts due to limited rainfall. Crop and vegetation growth showed signs of stress due to limited moisture. Monetary crop losses for 2023 beginning January first will be included in future reports when the drought ends and loses are assessed.

A second round of storms developed over central Nebraska during the morning of the 14th and moved southeastward across eastern Kansas and into the  Missouri Ozarks through the evening of the 14th. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over central Missouri during the early evening hours. They moved east/southeast through the overnight hours. Numerous large hail and damaging wind reports were received. During the late evening through overnight hours, widespread heavy rain fell causing flash flooding. There was fairly widespread 1-3 rainfall totals, with isolated 6-8+ in central Missouri and south-central Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread wind damage across St. Francois County. Several trees were blown down in Terre Du Lac, with some of them falling onto homes causing moderate roof damage. In Bonne Terre, numerous power lines and trees were blown down including a large tree that fell onto a house at the intersection of Main and Cross Streets. In the Desloge, Park Hills and Leadington, there was widespread tree and power line damage. In Farmington, numerous trees and power lines were blown down around town. Also, a stop light pole was bent over. No deaths or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms occurred from the early afternoon to the evening of the 17th ahead of the southward moving cold front. The storms mainly affected locations along and east of a line from Stockton to Springfield to Branson, and produced hail up to 3 inches in diameter at Preston in Hickory County. Wind damage and wind gusts over 70 mph were also reported. The town of Theodosia in Ozark County was especially hard hit with widespread tree damage. Rainfall accumulations averaged between one to two inches, with isolated accumulations over three inches in Laclede and Camden counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Emergency Manager recorded a wind gust of 71 mph on a weather station at the emergency management office.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms moved into western Missouri during the morning of the 20th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few large trees were reported blown down at Belle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After another month of below normal precipitation the severe drought across eastern Kansas persisted through the month of July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: While some areas saw some average or above average rain through July, most of the area across eastern Kansas saw the severe drought conditions continue through the month of July. As of now, widespread drought impacts have not been reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The upper-level ridge over the Mid-South began to weaken and northwest flow aloft developed across the region. Outflow boundaries from earlier convection over Missouri helped trigger isolated to scattered showers and thunderstorms over mainly the northern half of the Mid-South throughout the day into the evening. Adequate wind shear combined with strong instability helped some storms become severe with damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were knocked down across town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developed over the central Plains near the Kansas and Nebraska border during the afternoon of the 7th and moved southeastward across central and south central Missouri from the pre-dawn hours to the early morning of the 8th. These storms left an outflow boundary over central Missouri that sparked a second round of storms during the afternoon. These storms were much stronger and produced hail across central Missouri through the evening of the  8th. The strongest storm produced hail to the size of baseballs in Dent County near Stone Hill. Rainfall amounts over two inches occurred with these storms near Nevada, Lake of the Ozarks, and north of Salem.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickle to quarter sized hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developed over eastern Oklahoma and southeast Kansas during the evening of the 13th and moved along the Missouri and Arkansas border during the early morning of the 14th. The strongest storms produced up to ping-pong ball sized hail over Oregon County. Rainfall accumulations ranged from one to two inches near the Missouri and Arkansas Border.  A second round of storms developed over central Nebraska during the morning of the 14th and moved southeastward across eastern Kansas and into the  Missouri Ozarks through the evening of the 14th. These storms produced a swath of wind gusts over 60 mph from Fort Scott, Kansas to Branson, and led to widespread tree and powerline damage. Rainfall amounts averaged between one-half inch and one inch along and west of a line from Nevada to Gainesville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limbs of at least 1 inches in diameter and some shingles were damaged by high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms occurred from the early afternoon to the evening of the 17th ahead of the southward moving cold front. The storms mainly affected locations along and east of a line from Stockton to Springfield to Branson, and produced hail up to 3 inches in diameter at Preston in Hickory County. Wind damage and wind gusts over 70 mph were also reported. The town of Theodosia in Ozark County was especially hard hit with widespread tree damage. Rainfall accumulations averaged between one to two inches, with isolated accumulations over three inches in Laclede and Camden counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was reported down along Route 64.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms developed over far southwest Missouri and southeast Kansas during the early morning of the 9th as a low level jet interacted with a stalled front along the northwest Arkansas and northeast Oklahoma border. These storms dissipated during the late morning, but left another outflow boundary that interacted with an approaching storm complex to produce another round of strong to severe storms during the afternoon and evening of the 9th. Numerous reports of quarter to golf ball sized hail and wind gusts over 60 mph were reported over southwest Missouri and southeast Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms moved into western Missouri during the morning of the 20th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Highway D at Spencer Creek and Bank Branch was flooded and impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful storm system moved across eastern Iowa into southwest Wisconsin, sweeping a strong cold front through the area during the afternoon hours of Thursday, April 20th. A line of thunderstorms developed quickly along the front, with one of these storms becoming severe in Clark County as it neared the Mississippi River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The time of the event was estimated using radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The upper-level ridge over the Mid-South began to weaken and northwest flow aloft developed across the region. Outflow boundaries from earlier convection over Missouri helped trigger isolated to scattered showers and thunderstorms over mainly the northern half of the Mid-South throughout the day into the evening. Adequate wind shear combined with strong instability helped some storms become severe with damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 66 mph gust was measured at the Malden Municipal Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heat wave brought three consecutive days of heat indices near or above 105 degrees. Excessive heat occurred on the afternoons of the 27th and 28th, when heat indices peaked from 107 to around 120 degrees. The peak heat indices of this event were on the 27th, including 122 at Perryville, 114 at Cape Girardeau, and 110 at Poplar Bluff. Peak heat indices ranged from 104 to 113 degrees on the 29th, with an isolated higher reading at Perryville. The heat and humidity occurred in a southwest wind flow ahead of a cold front that progressed southeast from the Plains over the three-day period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developed over central Missouri and southeast Kansas ahead of an approaching cold front during the evening of the 12th and moved across southwest and south central Missouri through the early morning of the 13th. Sporadic wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph downed trees and power poles from Arcadia and Fort Scott in Kansas eastward to near Joplin, Billings, Marshfield, and Cabool. Widespread rainfall accumulations over an inch occurred across the entire region, with a band of two to five inches extending along and north of a line from Pittsburg, Kansas to Bolivar to Salem. Flash flooding was reported in Pittsburg, Kansas where water rescues and stranded vehicles were reported. Flash flooding also occurred near Couch in Oregon County when Townfork Creek rose rapidly out of its banks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway E at Louse Creek was flooded and impassable.

Peak heat indices ranged from 104 to 113 degrees on the 29th, with an isolated higher reading at Perryville. The heat and humidity occurred in a southwest wind flow ahead of a cold front that progressed southeast from the Plains over the three-day period.

A line of thunderstorms moved eastward across parts of southeast Missouri during the late afternoon hours. Isolated damaging wind gusts occurred with this line, which stayed mainly to the north of U.S. Highway 60. Shortly thereafter, a cluster of strong to severe thunderstorms formed south of Highway 60. These storms produced isolated wind damage shortly before crossing the Mississippi River into Kentucky. The storms developed in response to a rather amplified 500 mb shortwave trough that progressed east into Iowa and Missouri by nightfall. With daytime heating, the atmosphere became very unstable in the afternoon. Mixed-layer capes were around 3000 j/kg late in the day. A belt of 35 to 45 knot mid-level west-southwesterly winds resulted in sufficient deep-layer wind shear for organized storm structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was down on a truck a few miles north of Rombauer. Just inside the Wayne County line near Lake Wappapello, power lines were down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong to severe storm dropped over five inches of rain in a 2-hour time period on the southeast side of Springfield during the late afternoon. This led to flash flooding along Galloway Creek, and tree damage due to winds estimated in excess of 50 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several intersections along Battlefield Road had cars stalled in high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developed over central Missouri and southeast Kansas ahead of an approaching cold front during the evening of the 12th and moved across southwest and south central Missouri through the early morning of the 13th. Sporadic wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph downed trees and power poles from Arcadia and Fort Scott in Kansas eastward to near Joplin, Billings, Marshfield, and Cabool. Widespread rainfall accumulations over an inch occurred across the entire region, with a band of two to five inches extending along and north of a line from Pittsburg, Kansas to Bolivar to Salem. Flash flooding was reported in Pittsburg, Kansas where water rescues and stranded vehicles were reported. Flash flooding also occurred near Couch in Oregon County when Townfork Creek rose rapidly out of its banks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple medium to large trees were uprooted or snapped with numerous large limbs down across town from Geneva Street to Zell Road. Several small outbuildings suffered minor damage and a barn had minor roof damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms quickly developed with strong daytime heating. The storms formed into loosely organized clusters, with a few damaging wind gusts in the most intense cells. The storms formed along and ahead of a cold front that trailed southwest from a low pressure center over northern Indiana. A 500 mb trough extending from the Great Lakes down across the Mississippi Valley provided additional support for storms. Moderately strong mid-level winds contributed to effective bulk wind shear around 35 knots, sufficient for some organization of the convection.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A semi was flipped in the parking lot of an outdoor retailer. Merchandise was thrown around.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong upper level disturbance dropped southward from the northern Plains into western Missouri during the evening of the 30th and continued southward through the early morning of the 31st. Widespread wind gusts measured over 70 mph affected locations from Fort Scott to Lamar to South Springfield to Branson, resulting in widespread power outages from downed trees and powerlines. Some locations were without power for several days as a result of these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Branson Fire Department measured a wind gust of 64 mph.

Cape Girardeau County was on the western edge of a swath of extremely heavy rainfall that caused catastrophic flooding in western Kentucky and the southern tip of Illinois. Rainfall amounts were generally 6 to 12 inches in those areas, but rainfall amounts in Cape Girardeau County were mostly 5 to 6 inches. The historic rainfall event was caused by a persistent thunderstorm complex that continually regenerated as it moved southeast across the region during the early morning hours of the 19th. This complex of thunderstorms was indirectly caused by the complex of severe thunderstorms during the midday hours of the 18th. The complex on the 18th left an outflow boundary from south central Missouri into northeast Arkansas. This boundary lifted back northeast into southeast Missouri during the overnight and early morning hours of July 19th. At the same time, a southwest low level jet ramped up over the top of the surface boundary. Extremely high moisture levels were present, with precipitable water values up to 2.3 inches. The position of the boundary oriented parallel with the upper-level wind flow provided an ideal setup for training convection over the same areas. The initial storms blew up by 11 PM and continued to channel southeastward across the same areas through the following morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flash flooding occurred in the city of Cape Girardeau. A sport utility vehicle was swept off a roadway and into a creek. The vehicle came to rest against a pile of debris in the creek in Arena Park. The occupants safely escaped with no injuries. Elsewhere in Cape Girardeau, emergency crews responded to a water rescue when a sport utility vehicle became stalled in floodwaters. The driver was safely carried to safety. There was also some flooding in Jackson, but no major impacts were reported there. The co-operative observer at Jackson measured 5.11 inches for the total rainfall on the 18th and 19th. Most of this rainfall occurred during the flash flood on the morning of the 19th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms rapidly formed in a strongly unstable air mass during the heat of the afternoon. The storms organized into a line of storms over extreme southeast Missouri for a brief time. A couple of the storms produced damaging winds and a brief tornado. The storms occurred ahead of a slow-moving weak cold front that extended from central Indiana to the Ozarks of southwest Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief EF-2 tornado struck a homestead near the community of Bertrand in Scott County, MO. The tornado started on County Highway 532 just west of the intersection of Highway O. The tornado shifted and damaged several grain bins and destroyed two barns. A person in a truck nearby had their vehicle partially lifted briefly by the tornado. After crossing Highway O, the tornado severely damaged a house and adjacent auxiliary home, removing the roofs and collapsing some exterior walls of both buildings. Two people suffered minor injuries in the primary residence when they were cut by flying debris. After damaging some trees behind the buildings, the tornado dissipated as it moved into an agricultural field east of the residences. Peak winds were estimated near 115 mph.

A bowing line of strong to severe thunderstorms moved rapidly southeast from central Missouri. This line was associated with strong to isolated damaging winds. The line of storms moved through a strongly unstable air mass during the heat of the day, when mixed-layer capes were as high as 3000 j/kg. A similar airmass extended east into western Kentucky, which helped the storms maintain organization as they moved east-southeastward through the lower Ohio Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The automated weather observing system at the Perryville airport measured the gust to 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms quickly developed with strong daytime heating. The storms formed into loosely organized clusters, with a few damaging wind gusts in the most intense cells. The storms formed along and ahead of a cold front that trailed southwest from a low pressure center over northern Indiana. A 500 mb trough extending from the Great Lakes down across the Mississippi Valley provided additional support for storms. Moderately strong mid-level winds contributed to effective bulk wind shear around 35 knots, sufficient for some organization of the convection.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree branches were down on Highway 51.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line developed over southeast Missouri and moved rapidly east-southeast into western Kentucky. Locally damaging winds occurred shortly before the squall line exited Missouri. The bow-shaped squall line formed from a cluster of thunderstorms over central Missouri during the late morning hours. South of a surface front that extended from central Missouri to the I-64 corridor of southern Illinois, rapid heating of a moist air mass produced strong instability. A core of strong mid-level winds from 50 to 60 knots in the vicinity of the storms aided in rapid propagation and organization of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 66 mph was measured at the Cape Girardeau airport automated weather observation site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved eastward across parts of southeast Missouri during the late afternoon hours. Isolated damaging wind gusts occurred with this line, which stayed mainly to the north of U.S. Highway 60. Shortly thereafter, a cluster of strong to severe thunderstorms formed south of Highway 60. These storms produced isolated wind damage shortly before crossing the Mississippi River into Kentucky. The storms developed in response to a rather amplified 500 mb shortwave trough that progressed east into Iowa and Missouri by nightfall. With daytime heating, the atmosphere became very unstable in the afternoon. Mixed-layer capes were around 3000 j/kg late in the day. A belt of 35 to 45 knot mid-level west-southwesterly winds resulted in sufficient deep-layer wind shear for organized storm structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were down on power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heat wave brought three consecutive days of heat indices near or above 105 degrees. Excessive heat occurred on the afternoons of the 27th and 28th, when heat indices peaked from 107 to around 120 degrees. The peak heat indices of this event were on the 27th, including 122 at Perryville, 114 at Cape Girardeau, and 110 at Poplar Bluff. Peak heat indices ranged from 104 to 113 degrees on the 29th, with an isolated higher reading at Perryville. The heat and humidity occurred in a southwest wind flow ahead of a cold front that progressed southeast from the Plains over the three-day period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous counties over central and western Missouri were plagued by severe to extreme drought through the entire month of October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cooper County experienced extreme drought for the vast majority of October, with conditions improving slightly to severe drought by the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon and evening of August 4th, severe storms developed over western Missouri and moved eastward into central Missouri. A supercell produced a 4.5 mile long EF0 tornado in rural Ray County near the community of Millville, with another supercell producing a 1.5 mile long EF0 near Malta Bend in Saline County. A few other wind damage and 60 mph wind gusts reports were received with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A house in eastern Adair County had some external wall and roof removal due to strong winds moving through that area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While some locations saw improvement from Extreme (D3) Drought by the end of July Extreme drought remained for portions of west central and central Missouri. Extreme (D3) and Severe (D2) Drought conditions did contract across portions the Missouri Ozarks through the end of July but remained generally along and north of I-44. What rainfall occurred during the month provided little improvement for drought conditions with some areas deteriorating over the month. Across central Missouri Extreme (D3) and Severe (D2) drought contracted slightly but Severe (D2) drought expanded in southwestern Missouri. Moderate (D1) Drought and Abnormally Dry (D0) condition areas made up much of the remainder of the area. The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop and vegetation impacts due to limited soil and surface moisture, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers. Final reports of damages will be placed in the last month the drought occurred in the counties impacted in 2023.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: No change to the Extreme (D3) Drought conditions were noted in July.  Farm and stock ponds saw continuing impacts with ground water levels and reservoirs showing limited drought condition improvement. Crop and vegetation growth was showing signs of stress due to limited moisture. Monetary crop losses for 2023 beginning January first will be included in future reports when the drought ends and loses are assessed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening hours of August 11th into early morning August 12th, storms brought hail, damaging winds, flash flooding, and even a brief tornado to northern Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief EF-0 tornado began just south of Highway O in far northern Linn County just south of the Linn and Sullivan County line. The tornado initially damaged some trees and small outbuildings before moving to the east and damaging the roof, siding, and garage doors of a newly built barndominium type building on the north side of Highway O just west of the Highway O and Highway V intersection. The tornado traveled just over a mile and was on the ground for about 3 minutes. Thanks to the Linn County EM for their assistance on the survey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong upper ridge of high pressure that dominated the weather across the southwest US during the summer of 2023 bulged east into the Mid-South at times during the month of August. High temperatures combined with high humidity produced heat index values over 105 degrees and in some cases over 110 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat index values reached or exceeded 105 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon and evening of August 4th, severe storms developed over western Missouri and moved eastward into central Missouri. A supercell produced a 4.5 mile long EF0 tornado in rural Ray County near the community of Millville, with another supercell producing a 1.5 mile long EF0 near Malta Bend in Saline County. A few other wind damage and 60 mph wind gusts reports were received with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado began just west of the intersection of Highway B and Highway K in rural Ray County, MO where a structure sustained minor roof damage and a toppled chimney. The tornado continued to the east, crossing Highway K just north of the small community of Millville. The tornado then damaged a house and several outbuildings at the intersection of Highway E and E 176th Street. It was around this time that the tornado began displaying a tornado debris signature on EAX radar. The tornado then continued east approximately down E 176th street, doing minor damage to a house, trees, outbuildings, and tossing hay bales a short distance just past the intersection of E 176th St and Wickstrom Road. After this point, no more damage was noted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong upper level disturbance dropped southward from the northern Plains into western Missouri during the evening of the 30th and continued southward through the early morning of the 31st. Widespread wind gusts measured over 70 mph affected locations from Fort Scott to Lamar to South Springfield to Branson, resulting in widespread power outages from downed trees and powerlines. Some locations were without power for several days as a result of these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to pennies in size was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After another month of below average precipitation, the area saw generally worsening drought conditions across central and northern Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After another relatively dry month across the area central and northern Missouri saw generally deteriorating drought conditions. By the middle to end of the month almost the entire area was covered in D3 extreme drought conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developed over central Missouri and southeast Kansas ahead of an approaching cold front during the evening of the 12th and moved across southwest and south central Missouri through the early morning of the 13th. Sporadic wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph downed trees and power poles from Arcadia and Fort Scott in Kansas eastward to near Joplin, Billings, Marshfield, and Cabool. Widespread rainfall accumulations over an inch occurred across the entire region, with a band of two to five inches extending along and north of a line from Pittsburg, Kansas to Bolivar to Salem. Flash flooding was reported in Pittsburg, Kansas where water rescues and stranded vehicles were reported. Flash flooding also occurred near Couch in Oregon County when Townfork Creek rose rapidly out of its banks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few large limbs were reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest to southeast oriented bands of thunderstorms moved southeast, training over the same areas for several hours. A strong and very moist low-level wind flow fed the storms, with 850 mb winds generally from 35 to 40 knots. Tropical precipitable water values were estimated to be between 2 and 2.4 inches. Instability was sufficient for strong updrafts, with 1000 to 2000 J/kg of elevated cape near the confluence of the Ohio and Mississippi Rivers. The focus for the heaviest rain was from southeastern Missouri into far western Kentucky, northeast of a surface warm front draped across the central Missouri/Arkansas border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was over Highway 25 near the community of Arbor. The rainfall total from midnight to late afternoon at a site in west central Cape Girardeau County was 4.29 inches.

The focus for the heaviest rain was from southeastern Missouri into far western Kentucky, northeast of a surface warm front draped across the central Missouri/Arkansas border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several streets were flooded in Patton. Highway 34 was flooded in Marble Hill. Flash flooding of Crooked Creek inundated a park in Marble Hill. At the co-op station in Marble Hill, the 12-hour rainfall total ending late in the morning of the 3rd was 5.83 inches. The 36-hour rainfall total was 7.38 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developed over central Missouri and southeast Kansas ahead of an approaching cold front during the evening of the 12th and moved across southwest and south central Missouri through the early morning of the 13th. Sporadic wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph downed trees and power poles from Arcadia and Fort Scott in Kansas eastward to near Joplin, Billings, Marshfield, and Cabool. Widespread rainfall accumulations over an inch occurred across the entire region, with a band of two to five inches extending along and north of a line from Pittsburg, Kansas to Bolivar to Salem. Flash flooding was reported in Pittsburg, Kansas where water rescues and stranded vehicles were reported. Flash flooding also occurred near Couch in Oregon County when Townfork Creek rose rapidly out of its banks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple large trees were uprooted or snapped. Shingle and siding damage occurred to several out building an at least one home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over central Missouri during the early evening hours. They moved east/southeast through the overnight hours. There was fairly widespread 1-3 rainfall totals, with isolated 6-8+ in central Missouri and south-central Illinois.

This caused Saline Creek to rise well above it's banks flooding several roads near it, including State Route Z.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An MCV moved through northern sections of Missouri during the evening of August 4th into the early morning hours of August 5th. Widespread showers and storms developed. Some of the storms became severe. There were some damaging winds reported as well as two tornadoes. One tornado moved through Baring, MO with most of the town sustaining some kind of damage. Also, two people sustained minor injuries. Another tornado developed in Adams County in Illinois near Marcelline.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado began on the southeast side of town just southeast of the Highway K and Dairy Avenue intersection. The tornado quickly strengthened as it moved northwest producing EF2 damage southwest of the intersection. A farm outbuilding was completely destroyed, several grain bins were thrown 1/4 to 1/2 mile downwind, and a home was heavily damaged with the roof and several walls removed. The tornado then crossed Highway K and the railroad tracks and moved through the center of the town of Baring. In Baring, 6-7 businesses and 62 homes were damaged. Notable was the post office which was heavily damaged with the removal of the roof and several walls. The tornado then exited town tracking to the northwest producing damage to a farm outbuilding on the east side of Highway 15 and damage to trees on the west side of the highway. The tornado dissipated shortly thereafter. Overall the tornado was rated EF2 with a path length of 1.64 miles. The max path width was 450 yards. Two people sustained minor injuries and no deaths were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An MCV moved through northern sections of Missouri during the evening of August 4th into the early morning hours of August 5th. Widespread showers and storms developed. Some of the storms became severe. There were some damaging winds reported as well as two tornadoes. One tornado moved through Baring, MO with most of the town sustaining some kind of damage. Also, two people sustained minor injuries. Another tornado developed in Adams County in Illinois near Marcelline.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds uprooted several trees which fell across Bank Road in Loose Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous thunderstorms with very heavy rainfall developed over southeast Missouri during the night of the 3rd. These southeastward-moving storms gradually morphed into an area of widespread moderate rainfall with isolated embedded storms through the morning of the 4th. The heavy rainfall on the 4th coincided with areas of heavy rainfall that occurred on the morning of the 3rd. Two-day rainfall totals approached a foot. The corridor of 6 to 12 inches was focused from Bollinger County, Missouri southeast across Mississippi and New Madrid Counties into Fulton County, Kentucky. The synoptic pattern featured northwest flow at 500 mb across the lower Ohio Valley. Meanwhile, the surface analysis indicated a frontal boundary was draped across southeast Missouri into west Tennessee. Southwest of the frontal boundary, extreme heat and humidity was present, which led to high levels of instability. The thunderstorms developed along the instability gradient which was positioned near and west of the Mississippi River. The mean steering flow was southeastward parallel to the surface frontal boundary that was in place. Precipitable water values were extremely high, peaking around 2.3 to 2.4 inches both nights and mornings (Aug. 3-4). Meanwhile, a healthy low-level jet developed late at night into the morning, which helped provide the lift needed for thunderstorms to back-build and continuously move over the same locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 25 was closed due to flooding near Delta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms that developed along the advancing cold front on the evening of the 29th produced damaging wind gusts mainly along and south of Highway 60 from Monett to West Plains. Rainfall accumulations averaged between one-half inch and one and a half inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple large tree limbs and a large tree were reported down on and along Highway 76.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developed over central Missouri and southeast Kansas ahead of an approaching cold front during the evening of the 12th and moved across southwest and south central Missouri through the early morning of the 13th. Sporadic wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph downed trees and power poles from Arcadia and Fort Scott in Kansas eastward to near Joplin, Billings, Marshfield, and Cabool. Widespread rainfall accumulations over an inch occurred across the entire region, with a band of two to five inches extending along and north of a line from Pittsburg, Kansas to Bolivar to Salem. Flash flooding was reported in Pittsburg, Kansas where water rescues and stranded vehicles were reported. Flash flooding also occurred near Couch in Oregon County when Townfork Creek rose rapidly out of its banks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms moved southeast across eastern Missouri during the early nighttime hours. A portion of this complex evolved into a bowing line, producing damaging winds in parts of southeast Missouri to the north and northwest of Cape Girardeau. The storms occurred in the vicinity of a warm front that extended east-northeast from a low pressure center over the Oklahoma panhandle. The activity was aided by a very unstable feed of air from the southwest, with mixed-layer cape of 3000-3500 J/kg. Northwest flow of 30 to 40 knots in the middle levels of the atmosphere supported the bowing line of storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were down, with power outages at Lake Perry.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest to southeast oriented bands of thunderstorms moved southeast, training over the same areas for several hours. A strong and very moist low-level wind flow fed the storms, with 850 mb winds generally from 35 to 40 knots. Tropical precipitable water values were estimated to be between 2 and 2.4 inches. Instability was sufficient for strong updrafts, with 1000 to 2000 J/kg of elevated cape near the confluence of the Ohio and Mississippi Rivers. The focus for the heaviest rain was from southeastern Missouri into far western Kentucky, northeast of a surface warm front draped across the central Missouri/Arkansas border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding was reported in Portageville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overnight August 11th into August 12th, discrete supercells congealed into a convective complex. This system produced widespread wind damage from northeast Missouri and west central Illinois as far south as the St. Louis Metropolitan Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The leading edge of the storm impacted Rensselaer and Palmyra at nearly the same time, causing widespread damage to a farm and destroying a garage in Rensselaer. Several trees were downed there as well, and a 10 inch diameter tree fell in Palmyra.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overnight August 11th into August 12th, discrete supercells congealed into a convective complex. This system produced widespread wind damage from northeast Missouri and west central Illinois as far south as the St. Louis Metropolitan Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down 5 miles southwest of Middletown, on the west side of Swope School Road, just north of intersection with Highway A. In this location, several trees and tree branches were blown down. The tornado moved to the southeast, crossing Highway A and then as it crossed Trinity Church Road, several large tree limbs were snapped off and blocked the road. The tornado continued to the southeast for almost two more miles with additional minor tree damage before lifting and dissipating near the intersection of Nagel Road and Poppy Road, about 4 miles south southwest of Middletown. Overall the tornado was rated EF0 with a path length of 2.54 miles and a max path width of 40 yards. No deaths or injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was widespread tree damage across St. Charles County. The damage was reported in Flint Hill, Wentzville, O'Fallon, St. Peters, St. Charles, Weldon Springs and Cottleville. Also, in Flint Hill there was minor shingle damage to a few homes. In O'Fallon, a large branch snapped off and damaged a vinyl fence. On the north side of St. Charles, a large tree branch was blown down onto a house, causing minor roof, soffit and gutter damage. In Weldon Springs, a large tree was blown over near the intersection of Independence Road and Patriotic Trail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple large trees, some at least 2 feet in diameter, were uprooted and laying across city streets. One uprooted tree damaged decking around a home, and other split and fell on a home causing minor roof and gutter damage.  Numerous large limbs were down across the city from west to east, some falling on and damaging fences. One large limb fell on a pickup truck causing minor damage.  Some power lines were downed by trees and limbs causing power outages as transformer blew.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant rain event occurred late Friday into Saturday across northeast Missouri, extreme southeast Iowa, and west central Illinois, which resulted in flash flooding and a rapid rise on the Fox River near Wayland, Missouri.  A slow moving mesoscale convective vortex (MCV) tracked over the area and brought very heavy rain in a short amount of time.  Rainfall amounts between 4 and 8 inches fell in less than 6 hours, which caused many stalled vehicles and several water rescues in the towns of Kahoka, Missouri and Keokuk, Iowa.  A culvert was washed out south of Kahoka and many secondary roads had water over them.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement report that Route T near Bible Grove was washed out.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat indices in excess of 105 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Starting on August 19, 2023 well-above normal temperatures impacted the central part of the United States, kicking off a heatwave that has not been experienced in the region in recent history. Kansas City International Airport reached high temperatures not recorded in the area since August of 2012. In contrast to the 2012 heatwave, this stretch of hot weather was accompanied by extremely high dewpoints, with frequently reached the upper 70s to middle 80s across the entire area. The combination of the extreme heat and the excessively high dewpoints led to heat indices from August 19 through August 25 to rise to 120-130 degrees. The hottest heat index in the area occurred in Lawrence, Kansas on the 20th and 21st, when it rose to 134 degrees.||Perhaps of note, the Topeka upper air program, which has sent radiosondes into the atmosphere twice per day since 1955 had only measured 6000 meters to the 500 millibar level two times in its history. During this heatwave Topeka measured 6000 meters to 500 mb on six straight launches, spanning three days. The highest 500 mb level measured out of Topeka for this stretch was 6030 meters, a testament to the extreme nature of this mid level ridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Starting on August 19, 2023 well-above normal temperatures impacted the central part of the United States, kicking off a heatwave that has not been experienced in the region in recent history. Kansas City International Airport reached high temperatures not recorded in the area since August of 2012. In contrast to the 2012 heatwave, this stretch of hot weather was accompanied by extremely high dewpoints, with frequently reached the upper 70s to middle 80s across the entire area. The combination of the extreme heat and the excessively high dewpoints led to heat indices from August 19 through August 25 to rise to 120-130 degrees. The hottest heat index in the area occurred in Lawrence, Kansas on the 20th and 21st, when it rose to 134 degrees.||Perhaps of note, the Topeka upper air program, which has sent radiosondes into the atmosphere twice per day since 1955 had only measured 6000 meters to the 500 millibar level two times in its history. During this heatwave Topeka measured 6000 meters to 500 mb on six straight launches, spanning three days. The highest 500 mb level measured out of Topeka for this stretch was 6030 meters, a testament to the extreme nature of this mid level ridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Max heat indices during the afternoons of August 19th through August 25th, 2023 primarily ranged from the 110 to 120 degree range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overnight August 11th into August 12th, discrete supercells congealed into a convective complex. This system produced widespread wind damage from northeast Missouri and west central Illinois as far south as the St. Louis Metropolitan Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter submitted photos of a large 8-10 inch tree limb down at their residence. This occurred as the leading edge of the line of storms surged as it passed over town.

The combination of the extreme heat and the excessively high dewpoints led to heat indices from August 19 through August 25 to rise to 120-130 degrees. The hottest heat index in the area occurred in Lawrence, Kansas on the 20th and 21st, when it rose to 134 degrees.||Perhaps of note, the Topeka upper air program, which has sent radiosondes into the atmosphere twice per day since 1955 had only measured 6000 meters to the 500 millibar level two times in its history. During this heatwave Topeka measured 6000 meters to 500 mb on six straight launches, spanning three days. The highest 500 mb level measured out of Topeka for this stretch was 6030 meters, a testament to the extreme nature of this mid level ridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Max heat indices during the afternoons of August 19th through August 25th, 2023 primarily ranged from the 110 to 120 degree range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon and evening of August 4th, severe storms developed over western Missouri and moved eastward into central Missouri. A supercell produced a 4.5 mile long EF0 tornado in rural Ray County near the community of Millville, with another supercell producing a 1.5 mile long EF0 near Malta Bend in Saline County. A few other wind damage and 60 mph wind gusts reports were received with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated 60 mph winds in Waverly.

A supercell produced a 4.5 mile long EF0 tornado in rural Ray County near the community of Millville, with another supercell producing a 1.5 mile long EF0 near Malta Bend in Saline County. A few other wind damage and 60 mph wind gusts reports were received with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated 60 mph winds in Clifton City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While some locations saw improvement from Extreme (D3) Drought by the end of July Extreme drought remained for portions of west central and central Missouri. Extreme (D3) and Severe (D2) Drought conditions did contract across portions the Missouri Ozarks through the end of July but remained generally along and north of I-44. What rainfall occurred during the month provided little improvement for drought conditions with some areas deteriorating over the month. Across central Missouri Extreme (D3) and Severe (D2) drought contracted slightly but Severe (D2) drought expanded in southwestern Missouri. Moderate (D1) Drought and Abnormally Dry (D0) condition areas made up much of the remainder of the area. The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop and vegetation impacts due to limited soil and surface moisture, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers. Final reports of damages will be placed in the last month the drought occurred in the counties impacted in 2023.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of rainfall allowed for improvements in the county through the month. Extreme (D3) Drought was alleviated through the month with the county improving with Severe (D2) Drought across the northwestern two thirds and the reminder of the county in Moderate (D1) Drought. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture. Farm and stock ponds saw continuing impacts with ground water levels and reservoirs showed modest impacts due to limited rainfall. Crop and vegetation growth showed signs of stress due to limited moisture. Monetary crop losses for 2023 beginning January first will be included in future reports when the drought ends and loses are assessed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe drought continued through the entire month of November for 9 counties in the Pleasant Hill/Kansas City CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought continued through the entire month of November for Cooper County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe drought impacted Adair County, MO beginning on November 7th and continuing through the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions worsened to severe drought (D2) for the eastern half of Adair County by the November 7th US Drought Monitor, and continued through the end of the month.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Nov 14th installment of the Drought Monitor introduced severe drought (D2) for much of Gentry County. Severe drought continued through the end of November 2023.
