EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of May 6 strong thunderstorms formed in far northwest Missouri. Through the afternoon these storms strengthened as they moved east across northern Missouri, ultimately turning into robust supercells that started diving southeast toward Highway 36. Along the way they produced giant hail reaching, and perhaps exceeding 4 inches at times. As the main storm pushed southeast toward Linneus, Missouri it produced a tornado that at times was several hundred yards wide. This tornado produced EF-2 damage just north of Linneus. Perhaps as noteworthy, if not more so, was the extremely strong and wide rear flank downdraft that produced some intense damage well south of the main tornado track. This RFD destroyed numerous homes along the way and produced extreme tree damage to the city of Linneus, which was largely spared a direct hit from the actual tornado. Overall the storm produced three separate tornadoes, the most noteworthy of which was the EF-2 just north of Linneus, Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After the large tornado across central Linn County there was a brief interruption in the damage path. The next tornado formed a few miles southeast of where the larger tornado circulation became discontinuous. This tornado did some minor damage to residences and outbuildings between Linneus and Bucklin. Some of the more extensive damage occurred from rear-flank downdraft winds 1-2 miles south of the main tornado circulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of July 30th, a squall line moved south southeast out of SE Nebraska into eastern Kansas and western Missouri. This squall line produced widespread 60 to 70 mph wind gusts with a few gusts up to 80 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured peak wind gust of 66 mph at Kansas City International Airport (MCI).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening/night of May 7th, a squall line moved from NW Missouri into central MO, leading to a couple of wind reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roof damage was reported to a structure in Salisbury due to strong winds as the leading edge of a squall line moved through.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening/night of May 7th, a squall line moved from NW Missouri into central MO, leading to a couple of wind reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts up to 60 mph were estimated in Tarkio as a squall line moved through town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the late afternoon of May 8th, severe storms dropped half dollar and ping pong ball hail in Bates County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar sized hail was reported in Rich Hill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While some locations saw improvement from Extreme (D3) Drought by the end of July Extreme drought remained for portions of west central and central Missouri. Extreme (D3) and Severe (D2) Drought conditions did contract across portions the Missouri Ozarks through the end of July but remained generally along and north of I-44. What rainfall occurred during the month provided little improvement for drought conditions with some areas deteriorating over the month. Across central Missouri Extreme (D3) and Severe (D2) drought contracted slightly but Severe (D2) drought expanded in southwestern Missouri. Moderate (D1) Drought and Abnormally Dry (D0) condition areas made up much of the remainder of the area. The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop and vegetation impacts due to limited soil and surface moisture, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers. Final reports of damages will be placed in the last month the drought occurred in the counties impacted in 2023.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few periods of limited rainfall allowed for some improvements in the county through the month allowing drought conditions to improve slightly. Extreme (D3) Drought was alleviated through the month with the county improving to Severe (D2) Drought. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture. Farm and stock ponds saw continuing impacts with ground water levels and reservoirs showed modest impacts due to limited rainfall. Crop and vegetation growth showed signs of stress due to limited moisture. Monetary crop losses for 2023 beginning January first will be included in future reports when the drought ends and loses are assessed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of May 6 strong thunderstorms formed in far northwest Missouri. Through the afternoon these storms strengthened as they moved east across northern Missouri, ultimately turning into robust supercells that started diving southeast toward Highway 36. Along the way they produced giant hail reaching, and perhaps exceeding 4 inches at times. As the main storm pushed southeast toward Linneus, Missouri it produced a tornado that at times was several hundred yards wide. This tornado produced EF-2 damage just north of Linneus. Perhaps as noteworthy, if not more so, was the extremely strong and wide rear flank downdraft that produced some intense damage well south of the main tornado track. This RFD destroyed numerous homes along the way and produced extreme tree damage to the city of Linneus, which was largely spared a direct hit from the actual tornado. Overall the storm produced three separate tornadoes, the most noteworthy of which was the EF-2 just north of Linneus, Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mostly quarter to golf ball sized hail with a few stones up to baseball size was reported just south of Bethany.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While some locations saw improvement from Extreme (D3) Drought by the end of July Extreme drought remained for portions of west central and central Missouri. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall through the month allowed for improvement across the county though Moderate (D1) Drought conditions continued across the region through the end of July. The US Drought Monitor indicated that the county remained in Moderate (D1) Drought with the far southwestern corner having improved to Abnormally (D0) dry. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture with farm and stock ponds low but improved. Dry conditions caused grasses to dry and significantly impact hay production causing losses in hay cutting. Many farmers saw impacts to row crops such as soy beans, corn and other specialty crops. Crop loss estimates notated in this month encompass losses from continued drought since January 2023.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of May 6 strong thunderstorms formed in far northwest Missouri. Through the afternoon these storms strengthened as they moved east across northern Missouri, ultimately turning into robust supercells that started diving southeast toward Highway 36. Along the way they produced giant hail reaching, and perhaps exceeding 4 inches at times. As the main storm pushed southeast toward Linneus, Missouri it produced a tornado that at times was several hundred yards wide. This tornado produced EF-2 damage just north of Linneus. Perhaps as noteworthy, if not more so, was the extremely strong and wide rear flank downdraft that produced some intense damage well south of the main tornado track. This RFD destroyed numerous homes along the way and produced extreme tree damage to the city of Linneus, which was largely spared a direct hit from the actual tornado. Overall the storm produced three separate tornadoes, the most noteworthy of which was the EF-2 just north of Linneus, Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball sized hail was reported in NW Schuyler County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several severe thunderstorms developed across much of eastern Iowa and northeast Missouri, into portions of northwest Illinois on the evening of May 6, 2023. Storms mainly produced hail up to half dollar size (1.25 inches) in northeast Missouri.

Several severe thunderstorms developed across much of eastern Iowa and northeast Missouri, into portions of northwest Illinois on the evening of May 6, 2023. Storms mainly produced hail up to half dollar size (1.25 inches) in northeast Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea to half dollar sized hail was reported, with three rounds of storms. This was the time estimated by radar for the half dollar sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While some locations saw improvement from Extreme (D3) Drought by the end of July Extreme drought remained for portions of west central and central Missouri. Extreme (D3) and Severe (D2) Drought conditions did contract across portions the Missouri Ozarks through the end of July but remained generally along and north of I-44. What rainfall occurred during the month provided little improvement for drought conditions with some areas deteriorating over the month. Across central Missouri Extreme (D3) and Severe (D2) drought contracted slightly but Severe (D2) drought expanded in southwestern Missouri. Moderate (D1) Drought and Abnormally Dry (D0) condition areas made up much of the remainder of the area. The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop and vegetation impacts due to limited soil and surface moisture, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers. Final reports of damages will be placed in the last month the drought occurred in the counties impacted in 2023.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of rainfall allowed for improvements in the county through the month. Extreme (D3) Drought was alleviated through the month with the county improving with Severe (D2) Drought across the norther third and the reminder of the county in Moderate (D1) Drought or Abnormally (D0) Dry conditions. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture. Farm and stock ponds saw continuing impacts with ground water levels and reservoirs showed modest impacts due to limited rainfall. Crop and vegetation growth showed signs of stress due to limited moisture. Monetary crop losses for 2023 beginning January first will be included in future reports when the drought ends and loses are assessed.

Moderate (D1) Drought and Abnormally Dry (D0) condition areas made up much of the remainder of the area though some locations saw the removal of all drought conditions. The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop and vegetation impacts due to limited soil and surface moisture, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers. Final reports of damages will be placed in the last month the drought occurred in the counties impacted in 2023.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Enough rain fell across the region during the month allowing Extreme (D3) Drought to contract and move out of the county. Severe (D2) Drought conditions contracted north slightly with Moderate (D1) Drought across the remainder of the county. Farm and stock ponds saw continuing impacts with ground water levels and reservoirs showing limited drought condition improvement. Crop and vegetation growth was showing signs of stress due to limited moisture. Monetary crop losses for 2023 beginning January first will be included in future reports when the drought ends and loses are assessed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The thunderstorm that produced quarter sized hail in Warren County intensified quickly as it entered St. Charles County. There were numerous reports of severe hail in and around Wentzville, ranging from quarters to golf balls. Hailstones damaged windows and siding in multiple neighborhoods in Wentzville before weakening after passing the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first freeze of the season brought an end to the growing season. Low temperatures in Southeast Missouri were from 20-30 degrees. A cold front plunged into the Deep South, resulting in northwesterly winds bringing in strong cold air advection late in the day on the 30th. An approaching secondary cold front and a 1036 mb high over the Dakotas reinforced the cold air, with widespread hard freeze conditions Halloween morning.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall through the month allowed for improvement across the county though Moderate (D1) Drought conditions continued across the region through the end of October. The US Drought Monitor indicated that the county remained in Moderate (D1) Drought with the southwestern corner having improved to Abnormally (D0) dry. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture with farm and stock ponds low but improved. Dry conditions caused grasses to dry and significantly impact hay production causing losses in hay cutting. Many farmers saw impacts to row crops such as soy beans, corn and other specialty crops. Crop loss estimates notated in this month encompass losses from continued drought since July 2023.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Breezy SSW winds were occurring during the day as high pressure was located over the Southeast and a surface low near the IA/MN border. A particularly strong gust out ahead of a band of showers caused damage in Poplar Bluff, MO.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple power poles were blown over in Poplar Bluff due to gusty winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions in the Upper Midwest and Central Plains, with widespread Severe Drought (D2) or worse, resulted in significantly reduced river flow along the Mississippi River during the fall. While local drought conditions were D0 in the 10/3, 10/10, and 10/17 drought monitor updates along the Mississippi River in the NWS Paducah county warning area, and D1 drought only added to far Western Kentucky 10/24, water levels reached a preliminary record low of -6.58 feet at New Madrid, Missouri at 11pm CST October 15th. River levels were at their lowest along the Mississippi River from around October 5th to October 24th. River transportation was significantly hindered due to low water levels, with frequent dredging necessary to keep levels sufficiently deep. Tower Rock became reachable on foot again as it did during last years' low water levels.

A broken line of strong thunderstorms became weaker and more disorganized as it moved south across southeast Missouri. However, outflow winds from these weakening storms were locally damaging shortly before they crossed into western Kentucky. The storms fed on a southwest wind flow of unstable air. The most unstable air was elevated, characterized by most-unstable layer capes around 2500 j/kg. The storms occurred ahead of a cold front that trailed southwest across the Ozarks from a low pressure center over the Great Lakes region.
Severe drought continued through the entire month of November for 9 counties in the Pleasant Hill/Kansas City CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought continued through the entire month of November for Howard County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a broad area of rain moved through the region earlier in the day, a boundary sagged slowly south through the Ozarks. This boundary became the focus for  rounds of rain and storms. With ample instability available and a strong low level jet, storms were able to produce measured winds as high as 76 mph at times. With storms moving across the same locations thanks to the slow moving boundary, flooding also occurred for some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 76mph was measured by a home anemometer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of very hot and extremely humid weather began on Sunday, August 20 and lasted through Friday, August 25. Although the temperature rarely threatened climatological records, the humidity caused the heat index to reach extreme levels. The combination of heat and humidity led to several heat related illnesses across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of very hot and extremely humid weather began on Sunday, August 20 and lasted through Friday, August 25. The combination of heat and humidity led to heat index values of 110 to 120 as observed at numerous reporting sites in the region. The max heat index at the St Louis International Airport was 117 on August 20. A total of 87 people were seen in hospital emergency rooms between St Louis City and County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe mesoscale convective system (MCS) moved through the forecast area during the afternoon hours producing widespread 60-70mph winds and associated damage. The complex moved to the southeast taking advantage of 3500-4500 J/kg of surface-based CAPE.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wide swath of wind damage across Franklin County. In Gerald, several power lines were blown down. In Union, several large trees were blown down as well as numerous tree limbs. Several trees were blown down in St. Clair and in Catawissa. Numerous limbs were covering Highway HH just south of Catawissa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Even though an old outflow boundary was across southeast Missouri and the far southern portions of Illinois during the morning hours, the boundary dissipated, allowing 75+ dew points to surge north along the Mississippi River. As skies cleared the atmosphere was able to quickly destabilize. Surface based CAPEs were in excess of 3000 J/kg with 40kts of 0 to 6km bulk shear. This combined with a shortwave trough that moved eastward across the region, produced strong to severe storms. There were numerous reports of damaging winds and some reports of large hail. There were three fatalities due to fallen trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down several large tree limbs around Bonne Terre. A couple limbs landed on vehicles causing minor to moderate damage. Further east, numerous trees and tree limbs were blown down in the Goose Creek Lake area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along an old outflow boundary across parts of central and east central Missouri. Some of the storms produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down the flag pole at the Bland Fire Department. Just south southwest of Canaan trees were blown down near the intersection of Highway CC and Canaan Road. Also, trees were blown down near the intersection of Highway V and Tappmeyer road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Some locations saw improvement from Extreme (D3) Drought by the end of August Extreme drought remained for portions of west central Missouri. Extreme (D3) and Severe (D2) Drought conditions did contract across portions the Missouri Ozarks through the end of August but remained generally along and north of I-44. Rainfall amounts from 1 to 5 inches with localized 6 to 10 inch amounts occurred during the month allowing for improvement of drought conditions over southern Missouri during the month. Across central Missouri Extreme (D3) and Severe (D2) drought contracted but remained across parts of west central Missouri. Severe (D2) drought also contracted in southwestern Missouri. Moderate (D1) Drought and Abnormally Dry (D0) condition areas made up much of the remainder of the area though some locations saw the removal of all drought conditions. The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop and vegetation impacts due to limited soil and surface moisture, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers. Final reports of damages will be placed in the last month the drought occurred in the counties impacted in 2023.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall through the month allowed for improvement across the county though Moderate (D1) Drought conditions continued across the region through the end of July. The US Drought Monitor indicated that the county remained in Moderate (D1) Drought with the far southern parts of the county having improved to Abnormally (D0) dry. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture with farm and stock ponds low but improved. Dry conditions caused grasses to dry and significantly impact hay production causing losses in hay cutting. Many farmers saw impacts to row crops such as soy beans, corn and other specialty crops. Crop loss estimates notated in this month encompass losses from continued drought since June 2023.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strongly forced line of storms moved across the plains and into the Missouri Ozarks during the late afternoon into the evening. As the storms moved into the region severe storms produced widespread wind damage with winds measured as high as 61 mph. Numerous trees and power lines were downed by high winds, one of which fell into a home injuring 3 residents. The storms took advantage of a very moist airmass to result in  very efficient rainfall. Rainfall was measures from 2 to 4 inches across much of southwestern Missouri with local reports in excess of 6 inches in Lawrence County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous large limbs and trees were reported downed by high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed in the late afternoon into the evening along a slow moving to nearly stationary frontal boundary. Hail up to golf balls in size were reported with the strongest updrafts in central Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State highway BB at Gabriel Creek was flooded and impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong upper level ridge built up over the region from July 25th through 29th. This ushered in much warmer air and higher dewpoints. So heat index values 100+ were reported from July 25th through 29th. The hottest days were July 27th, 28th and 29th, so an excessive heat warning was issued for portions of northeast/east central Missouri and west central/southwest Illinois. At least 50 people were seen in hospital emergency room departments during this three day period in St. Louis County/City for heat-related illnesses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive heat across the county with highs in the upper 90s to 102 degrees each day. Heat index values as high as 110 both days.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Some locations saw improvement from Extreme (D3) Drought by the end of August Extreme drought remained for portions of west central Missouri. Extreme (D3) and Severe (D2) Drought conditions did contract across portions the Missouri Ozarks through the end of August but remained generally along and north of I-44. Rainfall amounts from 1 to 5 inches with localized 6 to 10 inch amounts occurred during the month allowing for improvement of drought conditions over southern Missouri during the month. Across central Missouri Extreme (D3) and Severe (D2) drought contracted but remained across parts of west central Missouri. Severe (D2) drought also contracted in southwestern Missouri. Moderate (D1) Drought and Abnormally Dry (D0) condition areas made up much of the remainder of the area though some locations saw the removal of all drought conditions. The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop and vegetation impacts due to limited soil and surface moisture, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers. Final reports of damages will be placed in the last month the drought occurred in the counties impacted in 2023.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: No change to the Severe (D2) Drought conditions were noted in August. Farm and stock ponds saw continuing impacts with ground water levels and reservoirs showing limited drought condition improvement. Crop and vegetation growth was showing signs of stress due to limited moisture. Monetary crop losses for 2023 beginning January first will be included in future reports when the drought ends and loses are assessed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong upper level ridge built up over the region from July 25th through 29th. This ushered in much warmer air and higher dewpoints. So heat index values 100+ were reported from July 25th through 29th. The hottest days were July 27th, 28th and 29th, so an excessive heat warning was issued for portions of northeast/east central Missouri and west central/southwest Illinois. At least 50 people were seen in hospital emergency room departments during this three day period in St. Louis County/City for heat-related illnesses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive heat across the county with highs in the upper 90s to 102 degrees each day. Heat index values as high as 110 both days.

With storms moving across the same locations thanks to the slow moving boundary, flooding also occurred for some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were uprooted or snapped along Highway 5 to the Arkansas State line. Several power lines were down as well. One tree fell on a home causing minor shingle roof damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strongly forced line of storms moved across the plains and into the Missouri Ozarks during the late afternoon into the evening. As the storms moved into the region severe storms produced widespread wind damage with winds measured as high as 61 mph. Numerous trees and power lines were downed by high winds, one of which fell into a home injuring 3 residents. The storms took advantage of a very moist airmass to result in  very efficient rainfall. Rainfall was measures from 2 to 4 inches across much of southwestern Missouri with local reports in excess of 6 inches in Lawrence County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Spring River at La Russell Rose 6 feet in a few hours, exceeding flood stage and  flooding Lawrence County Avenue making it impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of very hot and extremely humid weather began on Sunday, August 20 and lasted through Friday, August 25. Although the temperature rarely threatened climatological records, the humidity caused the heat index to reach extreme levels. The combination of heat and humidity led to several heat related illnesses across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of very hot and extremely humid weather began on Sunday, August 20 and lasted through Friday, August 25. The combination of heat and humidity led to heat index values of 110 to 120 as observed at numerous reporting sites in the region. The max heat index at the St Louis International Airport was 117 on August 20. A total of 87 people were seen in hospital emergency rooms between St Louis City and County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe mesoscale convective system (MCS) moved through the forecast area during the afternoon hours producing widespread 60-70mph winds and associated damage. The complex moved to the southeast taking advantage of 3500-4500 J/kg of surface-based CAPE.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down several trees as well as power lines along Highway J about 5 miles northeast of Linn. Also, more trees were blown down about 5 miles east of Linn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Some locations saw improvement from Extreme (D3) Drought by the end of August Extreme drought remained for portions of west central Missouri. Extreme (D3) and Severe (D2) Drought conditions did contract across portions the Missouri Ozarks through the end of August but remained generally along and north of I-44. Rainfall amounts from 1 to 5 inches with localized 6 to 10 inch amounts occurred during the month allowing for improvement of drought conditions over southern Missouri during the month. Across central Missouri Extreme (D3) and Severe (D2) drought contracted but remained across parts of west central Missouri. Severe (D2) drought also contracted in southwestern Missouri. Moderate (D1) Drought and Abnormally Dry (D0) condition areas made up much of the remainder of the area though some locations saw the removal of all drought conditions. The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop and vegetation impacts due to limited soil and surface moisture, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers. Final reports of damages will be placed in the last month the drought occurred in the counties impacted in 2023.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall through the month allowed for improvement across the county though Moderate (D1) Drought conditions continued across the region through the end of August. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture with farm and stock ponds low but improved. Dry conditions caused grasses to dry and significantly impact hay production causing losses in hay cutting. Many farmers saw impacts to row crops such as soy beans, corn and other specialty crops. Crop loss estimates notated in this month encompass losses from continued drought since June 2023.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed in the late afternoon into the evening along a slow moving to nearly stationary frontal boundary. As storms moved across central Missouri Additional rainfall fell over areas that received rain the previous day and earlier in the morning of the 3rd. Hail up to golf balls in size were reported with the strongest updrafts in central Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall produced significant street flooding in the city limits. Flood waters were a foot deep in some locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated storms developed in an area where dewpoints in the low to mid 70s pooled. There was some weak surface convergence, ML CAPEs over 2000 J/kg, and steep low-level lapse rates, so there were some localized damaging microbursts with the severe storms. An area of storms over northern St. Clair County in Illinois produced up to 5 inches of rain in about a two hour period with some reports of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused widespread damage across the central portions of Callaway County. Numerous trees, tree limbs and power lines were blown down, including across Fulton and Calwood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong upper level ridge built up over the region from July 25th through 29th. This ushered in much warmer air and higher dewpoints. So heat index values 100+ were reported from July 25th through 29th. The hottest days were July 27th, 28th and 29th, so an excessive heat warning was issued for portions of northeast/east central Missouri and west central/southwest Illinois. At least 50 people were seen in hospital emergency room departments during this three day period in St. Louis County/City for heat-related illnesses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive heat across the county with highs in the upper 90s to 101 degrees each day. Heat index values as high as 110 each day. At least 25 people were seen in hospital emergency room departments during this three day period in St. Louis County for heat-related illnesses.

Moderate (D1) Drought and Abnormally Dry (D0) condition areas made up much of the remainder of the area though some locations saw the removal of all drought conditions. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture. Farm and stock ponds saw continuing impacts with ground water levels and reservoirs showing modest impacts due to limited rainfall.  Monetary crop losses for 2023 beginning January first will be included in future reports when the drought ends and loses are assessed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of July 14, 2023 a strong storm formed in northeast Kansas and eventually transformed into a strong squall line that impacted far eastern Kansas and western Missouri. This storm left a swath of wind damage mainly through the heart of the Kansas City metro area in the form of widespread tree damage. Several structures were impacted by falling trees as the storm moved through the city, and into central Missouri. The highest reported wind gust came from western Johnson County where a personal weather station recorded 89 mph wind just west of Lenexa. Numerous wind reports, both estimated and measured were over 70 mph. At one point nearly 200,000 people across the Kansas City metro lost power. As of the next day, roughly 60,000 Kansas Citians were still without power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County dispatch reports multiple trees downed east of Windsor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of July 29th, a squall line moved out of southeastern Nebraska and into NW Missouri, eventually moving east southeast into central and northern Missouri before exiting into WFO St. Louis's area. This squall line produced widespread damaging wind gusts on the order of 60 to 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees down blocking roadways in St. Joseph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured peak wind gust of 67 mph at St. Joseph Airport (STJ).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Thursday, June 29, a powerful severe wind event moved over the region. This event was known as a derecho given its long duration from north of Kansas City to southern Indiana and its consistent strong intensity. Almost all of the damage in the area surveyed locally was due to straight-line winds, at times easily peaking between 100-120 mph in far western Illinois. Those who experienced the event conveyed how the strongest winds persisted for 10 to 15 minutes, and in some locations, it lasted as long as 20 minutes, which is atypical for derecho events.||There were some swaths of enhanced damage. These were largely due to intense wind surges in the local area. One of these in Clark county, Mo was determined to be a short-lived EF1 tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey determined an EF1 tornado, with peak winds near 90 mph, tracked for 2.4 miles from southwest of Medill to just west of Kahoka. Damage to an outbuilding occurred, along with convergent damage to numerous large trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms occurred from the early afternoon to the evening of the 17th ahead of the southward moving cold front. The storms mainly affected locations along and east of a line from Stockton to Springfield to Branson, and produced hail up to 3 inches in diameter at Preston in Hickory County. Wind damage and wind gusts over 70 mph were also reported. The town of Theodosia in Ozark County was especially hard hit with widespread tree damage. Rainfall accumulations averaged between one to two inches, with isolated accumulations over three inches in Laclede and Camden counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to quarters in size was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of July 14, 2023 a strong storm formed in northeast Kansas and eventually transformed into a strong squall line that impacted far eastern Kansas and western Missouri. This storm left a swath of wind damage mainly through the heart of the Kansas City metro area in the form of widespread tree damage. Several structures were impacted by falling trees as the storm moved through the city, and into central Missouri. The highest reported wind gust came from western Johnson County where a personal weather station recorded 89 mph wind just west of Lenexa. Numerous wind reports, both estimated and measured were over 70 mph. At one point nearly 200,000 people across the Kansas City metro lost power. As of the next day, roughly 60,000 Kansas Citians were still without power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs downed along Highway 150 west of Lone Jack.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong upper level disturbance dropped southward from the northern Plains into western Missouri during the evening of the 30th and continued southward through the early morning of the 31st. Widespread wind gusts measured over 70 mph affected locations from Fort Scott to Lamar to South Springfield to Branson, resulting in widespread power outages from downed trees and powerlines. Some locations were without power for several days as a result of these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were downed by high winds across the Springfield Metro. At lease 40 homes had trees on the roof causing shingle, gutter, vent, soffit and siding damage. Weather heads were pulled out or damaged on several homes. several fences were damages or destroyed by falling limbs and trees. At lease a dozen outbuilding were damages with one a total loss. In excess of a dozen vehicles were damaged by falling limbs and trees. As many 4 light poles were bent or down along with many power poles and lines. Around 15,500 homes were without power.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous large trees and tree branches down along HWY 71 approaching downtown Kansas City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Thursday, June 29, a powerful severe wind event moved over the region. This event was known as a derecho given its long duration from north of Kansas City to southern Indiana and its consistent strong intensity. Almost all of the damage in the area surveyed locally was due to straight-line winds, at times easily peaking between 100-120 mph in far western Illinois. Those who experienced the event conveyed how the strongest winds persisted for 10 to 15 minutes, and in some locations, it lasted as long as 20 minutes, which is atypical for derecho events.||There were some swaths of enhanced damage. These were largely due to intense wind surges in the local area. One of these in Clark county, Mo was determined to be a short-lived EF1 tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several buildings with rooftops peeled. Storm survey estimates that winds of 90 to 100 mph caused this damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large ridge of high pressure aloft set up over the center of the country around August 20th, leading to a week-long heatwave across southeast Missouri. High temperatures were primarily only in the low to mid 90s most days, but the main headline was the extreme humidity levels witnessed over the entirety of this event. Portions of the region experienced very heavy rainfall amounts of 15 to 25 inches from July 1st through mid August. This set the stage for record levels of evapotranspiration from the vegetation and crops. Dew points, a measure of humidity levels, were routinely in the upper 70's to lower 80's, with several locations peaking in the mid 80's. Cape Girardeau tied its highest dew point on record of 83 degrees. This caused heat index readings to peak between 105 and 120 degrees each day from August 20th through the 26th. The high humidity levels also led to dense fog formation several nights during this event, which was rather unusual for a prolonged heat wave. The highest hourly heat index readings at Cape Girardeau were 118 on the 20th, 117 on the 21st, 116 on the 22nd, 113 on the 23rd, 119 on the 24th, 120 on the 25th, and 106 on the 26th. For those same dates, the highest readings at Poplar Bluff were 114, 112, 113, 108, 115, and 110 respectively. Many schools cancelled outdoor activities or moved them indoors, including football practices.

Scattered to numerous thunderstorms with very heavy rainfall developed over southeast Missouri during the night of the 3rd. These southeastward-moving storms gradually morphed into an area of widespread moderate rainfall with isolated embedded storms through the morning of the 4th. The heavy rainfall on the 4th coincided with areas of heavy rainfall that occurred on the morning of the 3rd. Two-day rainfall totals approached a foot. The corridor of 6 to 12 inches was focused from Bollinger County, Missouri southeast across Mississippi and New Madrid Counties into Fulton County, Kentucky. The synoptic pattern featured northwest flow at 500 mb across the lower Ohio Valley. Meanwhile, the surface analysis indicated a frontal boundary was draped across southeast Missouri into west Tennessee. Southwest of the frontal boundary, extreme heat and humidity was present, which led to high levels of instability. The thunderstorms developed along the instability gradient which was positioned near and west of the Mississippi River. The mean steering flow was southeastward parallel to the surface frontal boundary that was in place. Precipitable water values were extremely high, peaking around 2.3 to 2.4 inches both nights and mornings (Aug. 3-4). Meanwhile, a healthy low-level jet developed late at night into the morning, which helped provide the lift needed for thunderstorms to back-build and continuously move over the same locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway H north of Zalma was closed due to flooding. The two-day rainfall totals for the 3rd and 4th were 9.15 inches at the Marble Hill co-op site and 8.77 inches at a station just south of Grassy.

A large complex of thunderstorms moved slowly southeast over southeast Missouri, producing torrential downpours. A second complex of storms moved eastward over some of the same areas just before sunrise. The heaviest rainfall amounts were centered over Bollinger County, which suffered very damaging flash flooding. The storms occurred south of a stationary front that extended from east central Missouri to southwest Indiana. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large ridge of high pressure aloft set up over the center of the country around August 20th, leading to a week-long heatwave across southeast Missouri. High temperatures were primarily only in the low to mid 90s most days, but the main headline was the extreme humidity levels witnessed over the entirety of this event. Portions of the region experienced very heavy rainfall amounts of 15 to 25 inches from July 1st through mid August. This set the stage for record levels of evapotranspiration from the vegetation and crops. Dew points, a measure of humidity levels, were routinely in the upper 70's to lower 80's, with several locations peaking in the mid 80's. Cape Girardeau tied its highest dew point on record of 83 degrees. This caused heat index readings to peak between 105 and 120 degrees each day from August 20th through the 26th. The high humidity levels also led to dense fog formation several nights during this event, which was rather unusual for a prolonged heat wave. The highest hourly heat index readings at Cape Girardeau were 118 on the 20th, 117 on the 21st, 116 on the 22nd, 113 on the 23rd, 119 on the 24th, 120 on the 25th, and 106 on the 26th. For those same dates, the highest readings at Poplar Bluff were 114, 112, 113, 108, 115, and 110 respectively. Many schools cancelled outdoor activities or moved them indoors, including football practices.

Afternoon heat indices peaked from 104 to 112 degrees across southeast Missouri. The highest hourly heat index readings were 112 degrees at Poplar Bluff, 109 at Sikeston, 108 at Perryville, and 104 at Cape Girardeau. Hot and humid southwest winds prevailed ahead of a weak cold front trailing from a low pressure center over central Illinois.

Northwest to southeast oriented bands of thunderstorms moved southeast, training over the same areas for several hours. A strong and very moist low-level wind flow fed the storms, with 850 mb winds generally from 35 to 40 knots. Tropical precipitable water values were estimated to be between 2 and 2.4 inches. Instability was sufficient for strong updrafts, with 1000 to 2000 J/kg of elevated cape near the confluence of the Ohio and Mississippi Rivers. The focus for the heaviest rain was from southeastern Missouri into far western Kentucky, northeast of a surface warm front draped across the central Missouri/Arkansas border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Streets were flooded, and there were homes with flooded basements in Advance. A personal weather station in Advance measured 5.86 inches from midnight to late afternoon on the 3rd.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flash flooding was reported from the Marble Hill area west-southwest along Highway 34 to the county line. Between 40 and 50 homes and businesses were damaged by flooding in the county. Highway 34 was shut down as a nearby creek flowed over it. At one point, 22 county highways and roads were flooded. Several county roadways were washed out, including bridges. Some of the washouts were 8 to 10 feet deep. Flooding occurred at the main bridge in Marble Hill. Sixteen individuals were rescued from floodwaters throughout the county. Up to 6 feet of water was flowing in some locations. Water flowing 3 to 4 feet deep was observed in Marble Hill. Water was in buildings in Glenallen. Close to the Wayne County line, water was into numerous campers at Twin Bridges Park. Significant flooding occurred near the intersection of Highway Y and Highway 34. Water rescues occurred at the Castor River Campground west of Grassy. The two-day rainfall total at the Marble Hill co-op observation site was 5.73 inches, which occurred on the 13th and 14th. Total flood damage to public infrastructure in the county was conservatively estimated at 18.2 million dollars. Some of this total may have occurred in other flash flood events earlier in the month.
